# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pipeline Speedup of ARM9 over ARM7

### Problem Statement
Show analytically why the 5-stage pipeline of ARM9 achieves nearly **1.6× the clock speed** of the 3-stage pipeline of ARM7TDMI, assuming identical combinational logic and identical silicon process.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total combinational delay.**

Let the **total critical path** of an instruction's execution be $T_{\text{total}}$ (in nanoseconds), which is the same on both ARM7 and ARM9 because the underlying logic gates are identical.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{ARM7}} = T_{\text{ARM9}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cycle time on ARM7 (3-stage).**

The 3-stage pipeline of ARM7 divides logic into **3 roughly equal stages**:

$$
t_{\text{ARM7}} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{3} + t_{\text{reg}}
$$

where $t_{\text{reg}}$ is the register setup-and-hold overhead.

**Step 3 — Cycle time on ARM9 (5-stage).**

The 5-stage pipeline of ARM9 divides logic into **5 roughly equal stages**:

$$
t_{\text{ARM9}} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{5} + t_{\text{reg}}
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of clock frequencies.**

Ignoring register overhead for a first-order estimate:

$$
\frac{f_{\text{ARM9}}}{f_{\text{ARM7}}} = \frac{1 / t_{\text{ARM9}}}{1 / t_{\text{ARM7}}} = \frac{t_{\text{ARM7}}}{t_{\text{ARM9}}}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
\frac{f_{\text{ARM9}}}{f_{\text{ARM7}}} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}/3}{T_{\text{total}}/5} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.67
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

The 5-stage pipeline theoretically allows a clock frequency up to **1.67×** that of a 3-stage pipeline on the same silicon. (In practice, ARM9 typically runs at 200–300 MHz, vs. ARM7 at ~100 MHz, consistent with this analysis.)

$$
\boxed{\,f_{\text{ARM9}} \approx 1.67 \times f_{\text{ARM7}}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective CPI with Branch Penalty

### Problem Statement
A program contains $N = 1000$ instructions. **20%** are conditional branches. Each branch on ARM9 incurs a **2-cycle penalty** (flush). What is the effective CPI?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
N = 1000, \quad B = 0.20, \quad P = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Base CPI without hazards.**

For an ideal in-order pipeline, $\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Add branch penalty.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + (B \times P)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + (0.20 \times 2) = 1 + 0.4 = 1.4
$$

**Step 5 — Total execution time formula.**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} \times t_{\text{clk}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = 1000 \times 1.4 \times t_{\text{clk}} = 1400 \, t_{\text{clk}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1.4 \quad ; \quad T_{\text{exec}} = 1400 \, t_{\text{clk}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CPI > 1 for ARM9 in real code:** Branches, data hazards (RAW), cache misses, and exception entry all add stall cycles. Optimized code achieves CPI close to 1.0; unoptimized code may be 1.3–1.5.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ARM7 vs ARM9 Comparison

| Parameter | ARM7TDMI | ARM9TDMI | Effect on Performance |
|-----------|----------|----------|------------------------|
| Pipeline stages | 3 | 5 | ARM9 clock ≈ 1.67× ARM7 |
| Architecture | Von Neumann (single bus) | Modified Harvard (I & D separate) | ARM9 has higher memory BW |
| Instruction cache | None on core (external) | Typically 16 KB I-cache + 16 KB D-cache | ARM9: better hit rate |
| Multiplier | 32×8 → 32 (multicycle) | 32×32 → 32 in 1 cycle (via Booth) | ARM9 ~10× faster multiply |
| Max clock @ 0.13 µm | ~80 MHz | ~200 MHz | ARM9 ~2.5× faster end-to-end |
| Thumb support | Yes | Yes | Equal code density |
| Power (mW/MHz) | ~0.5 | ~0.6 (slightly higher) | ARM9 is hotter |

## 3.4 Analytical Trace — Branch Pipeline Flush on ARM9

Consider the instruction sequence:

```
0x8000:  CMP  R0, #1
0x8004:  BEQ  0x8020        ; branch taken
0x8008:  ADD  R1, R2, R3
0x800C:  SUB  R4, R5, R6
0x8010:  MUL  R7, R8, R9
0x8014:  ... (still in pipeline)
0x8020:  MOV  R10, #0       ; branch target
```

**Cycle-by-cycle pipeline trace:**

| Cycle | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |
|-------|----|----|----|-----|-----|
| 1 | CMP | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | BEQ | CMP | — | — | — |
| 3 | ADD | BEQ | CMP | — | — |
| 4 | (stall) | (flush) | (flush) | — | — |
| 5 | SUB | (flush) | (flush) | — | — |
| 6 | 0x8020 | SUB | (flush) | — | — |
| 7 | MOV | 0x8020 | SUB | — | — |

**Conclusion:** A taken branch causes **2-cycle penalty** on ARM9 — both the instruction at 0x800C (SUB, already fetched) and the speculative ADD/SUB must be flushed from IF and ID. This is why modern ARM cores add **branch prediction** and **filler instructions** to fill the delay slot (e.g., the 2 instructions after BEQ are often harmless ones to amortize the penalty).

> Real-world engineering use: Compiler `-O2` optimization in GCC/ARMCC uses instruction scheduling to place independent instructions immediately after branches, hiding the 2-cycle penalty.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM9TDMI — Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Instruction Bus I-Bus 32-bit"] --> B["Instruction Fetch Unit IF"]
    C["Data Bus D-Bus 32-bit"] --> D["Memory Access Unit MEM"]
    B --> E["Instruction Decode ID"]
    E --> F["Register Bank 31 x 32-bit"]
    E --> G["Barrel Shifter"]
    G --> H["ALU 32-bit"]
    F --> H
    D --> F
    H --> I["Execute Stage EX"]
    I --> D
    D --> J["Writeback Stage WB"]
    J --> F
    K["Multiplier Modified Booth"] --> F
    H --> K
    L["Control Unit Decoder"] -.-> B
    L -.-> E
    L -.-> G
    L -.-> H
    L -.-> D
    L -.-> J
    M["CPSR SPSR Status Logic"] --> L
    F --> M
    style B fill:#FFD700
    style E fill:#FFA500
    style H fill:#FF6347
    style D fill:#4682B4
    style J fill:#32CD32
    style G fill:#DA70D6
    style K fill:#8B4513
```

> **Block-Level Interpretation:**
> - **Yellow (IF)** → Fetches the next 32-bit ARM or 16-bit Thumb instruction.
> - **Orange (ID)** → Decodes, reads register operands, drives the barrel shifter.
> - **Red (EX)** → ALU performs arithmetic/logic; multiplier handles MUL/MLA.
> - **Blue (MEM)** → Loads/stores data memory via the dedicated D-bus.
> - **Green (WB)** → Writes the result back into the register file.
> - **Purple (Barrel Shifter)** → Pre-shifts an operand in 1 cycle (zero latency).
> - **Brown (Multiplier)** → Activated only for MUL, MLA, UMULL, etc.

## 4.2 ARM9 5-Stage Pipeline Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1: IF Instruction Fetch"] --> S2["Stage 2: ID Instruction Decode"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3: EX Execute ALU Shift"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4: MEM Data Memory Access"]
    S4 --> S5["Stage 5: WB Writeback to Register"]
    S1 -.-> R1["Pipeline Register IF ID"]
    R1 -.-> S2
    S2 -.-> R2["Pipeline Register ID EX"]
    R2 -.-> S3
    S3 -.-> R3["Pipeline Register EX MEM"]
    R3 -.-> S4
    S4 -.-> R4["Pipeline Register MEM WB"]
    R4 -.-> S5
    style S1 fill:#FFD700
    style S2 fill:#FFA500
    style S3 fill:#FF6347
    style S4 fill:#4682B4
    style S5 fill:#32CD32
    style R1 fill:#D3D3D3
    style R2 fill:#D3D3D3
    style R3 fill:#D3D3D3
    style R4 fill:#D3D3D3
```

> Each pair of stages is separated by a **pipeline register** (grey blocks) that holds intermediate results (PC+4, decoded control signals, ALU flags, memory data, etc.) for one clock cycle. **These registers are the reason the 5-stage pipeline can run 1.67× faster than 3-stage on the same silicon.**

## 4.3 ARM9 Operating Modes State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> USR
    USR --> SVC : SWI / Reset
    USR --> IRQ : External IRQ
    USR --> FIQ : External FIQ
    USR --> ABT : Data / Prefetch Abort
    USR --> UND : Undefined Instruction
    USR --> SYS : Direct CPSR write
    SVC --> USR : MOVS PC, LR
    IRQ --> USR : SUBS PC, LR, 4
    FIQ --> USR : SUBS PC, LR, 4
    ABT --> USR : SUBS PC, LR, 8
    UND --> USR : MOVS PC, LR
    SYS --> USR : Direct CPSR write
    note right of USR : Unprivileged\nNormal apps
    note right of SVC : Privileged\nOS Kernel
    note right of FIQ : Privileged\nFast Banked Regs

```

## 4.4 ARM-Based SoC Architecture with AMBA Buses

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["ARM9TDMI Core"] --> AHB["AHB Advanced High performance Bus"]
    ISRAM["Instruction SRAM"] --- AHB
    DSRAM["Data SRAM"] --- AHB
    DMA["DMA Controller"] --- AHB
    EXT["External Memory Controller"] --- AHB
    AHB --> BRG["AHB to APB Bridge"]
    BRG --> APB["APB Advanced Peripheral Bus"]

```

> **Engineering Insight:** AHB runs at the system clock (e.g., 100 MHz) and serves high-bandwidth masters/slaves. APB runs at a divided clock (e.g., 25 MHz) for low-speed peripherals. The bridge handles protocol conversion and write buffering, isolating the two clock domains.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between ARM7 and ARM9 processors in terms of pipeline architecture? Why does ARM9 achieve higher performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ARM7 uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute), while ARM9 uses a **5-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute → Memory → Writeback).

Because each pipeline stage performs less work, the **clock cycle time is shorter**, and the clock frequency is higher. Mathematically, with the same total logic delay, the clock period of ARM9 is roughly **3/5 that of ARM7**, yielding up to **1.67× higher clock frequency**.

Additionally, ARM9 uses a **modified Harvard architecture** (separate instruction and data buses), which increases memory bandwidth. ARM7, in contrast, uses a single von Neumann bus, which causes structural conflicts.

**(1 mark)** 3-stage vs 5-stage distinction.
**(1 mark)** Clock frequency derivation (3/5 = 1.67×).
**(1 mark)** Harvard vs von Neumann memory.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the function of the Barrel Shifter in the ARM processor. Give an example instruction that uses it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Barrel Shifter** is a dedicated hardware unit in the ARM datapath that performs **shifts and rotations on a 32-bit operand in a single clock cycle**, *before* the operand enters the ALU. This eliminates the need for separate shift instructions, making the ARM instruction set very dense.

**Example instruction:**

```assembly
ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #3
```

This computes: `R0 ← R1 + (R2 << 3)`. Without the barrel shifter, this would require a separate `LSL R2, R2, #3` followed by `ADD R0, R1, R2`.

**(1 mark)** Definition and purpose.
**(1 mark)** Single-cycle operation, pre-ALU placement.
**(1 mark)** Worked example with explanation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **CPU architecture of the ARM9TDMI processor**. Identify each major block and describe its function. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **5-stage pipeline of ARM9** with a clear timing diagram. Show what happens when a **taken branch** is encountered. Comment on the **branch penalty** and how it can be reduced. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The ARM9TDMI CPU consists of the following major functional blocks (refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1):

| # | Block | Function |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Instruction Fetch Unit (IF)** | Fetches the next 32-bit ARM or 16-bit Thumb instruction from the I-bus. Maintains the PC. |
| 2 | **Instruction Decode (ID)** | Decodes the opcode, generates control signals, and reads source operands from the **register bank** (R0–R15). |
| 3 | **Register Bank** | Provides 31 × 32-bit general-purpose registers with **banked copies** in privileged modes. |
| 4 | **Barrel Shifter** | Performs shifts/rotations on the second operand in 1 cycle before ALU entry. |
| 5 | **ALU** | Performs 32-bit arithmetic (ADD, SUB, CMP) and logic (AND, ORR, EOR, BIC) operations. |
| 6 | **Multiplier** | Implements `MUL`, `MLA`, `UMULL`, `UMLAL`, `SMULL`, `SMLAL` using **modified Booth's algorithm** (radix-4). |
| 7 | **Memory Access Unit (MEM)** | Performs data loads and stores via the dedicated D-bus. |
| 8 | **Writeback (WB)** | Writes the result of ALU/Memory back into the destination register. |
| 9 | **CPSR/SPSR** | Status registers: condition flags (N, Z, C, V), interrupt masks (I, F), Thumb bit (T), and mode bits (M[4:0]). |

The control unit orchestrates the data flow between these blocks. The **I-bus** and **D-bus** are separate (Harvard architecture), allowing simultaneous instruction fetch and data access.

**(1 mark)** Correct identification of all 9 blocks.
**(2 marks)** Function of each block (allocate ~½ mark per block, but 4 key blocks = 1 mark each).
**(2 marks)** Harvard bus separation explanation.
**(2 marks)** CPSR/SPSR bit structure and mode bit usage.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**The 5-Stage Pipeline:**

| Stage | Function | Hardware |
|-------|----------|----------|
| 1. **IF** | Fetch instruction from I-cache / memory | PC, address adder |
| 2. **ID** | Decode, read registers, prepare operand | Decoder, register file, barrel shifter |
| 3. **EX** | ALU operation, branch target computation | ALU, multiplier |
| 4. **MEM** | Load / store from D-cache / memory | Data bus interface |
| 5. **WB** | Write result back to register file | Writeback mux |

**Timing Diagram (one instruction per cycle):**

| Cycle | Inst 1 | Inst 2 | Inst 3 | Inst 4 | Inst 5 |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| 1 | IF | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | ID | IF | — | — | — |
| 3 | EX | ID | IF | — | — |
| 4 | MEM | EX | ID | IF | — |
| 5 | WB | MEM | EX | ID | IF |
| 6 | — | WB | MEM | EX | ID |

**Ideal CPI = 1.**

**Branch Penalty on a Taken Branch:**

| Cycle | BEQ | NEXT1 | NEXT2 | TARGET |
|-------|-----|-------|-------|--------|
| k | IF | — | — | — |
| k+1 | ID | IF | — | — |
| k+2 | EX | ID | IF | — |
| k+3 | — | (flushed) | (flushed) | — |
| k+4 | — | — | (flushed) | IF |
| k+5 | MEM | — | — | ID |

The two instructions already fetched into IF and ID after the branch are **flushed (squashed)**. This wastes 2 clock cycles, hence the **2-cycle branch penalty**.

**Reducing Branch Penalty:**
1. **Fill the delay slot:** Manually or via compiler, place useful instructions immediately after the branch.
2. **Use conditional execution:** ARM's `IT` block (ARMv6T2+) or predicate every instruction (ARM) to avoid branches for short `if` statements.
3. **Branch prediction:** Dynamically predict the branch direction and speculatively fetch (introduced in ARM10 and later).
4. **Loop unrolling** at compile time to reduce branch frequency.

**(2 marks)** 5 stages named with functions.
**(2 marks)** Timing diagram and CPI = 1 statement.
**(2 marks)** Branch penalty calculation (2 cycles).
**(1 mark)** Two techniques for penalty reduction.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **register organization** of the ARM processor. Discuss the role of **R13, R14, R15, CPSR, and SPSR** with a clear diagram. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **seven operating modes** of ARM9. Explain **when and how** mode transitions occur, with a specific example of **FIQ vs IRQ**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The ARM has **37 physical 32-bit registers**, of which **31 are visible at any moment** in user mode. The visible set is structured as follows:

| Register | Name | Role |
|----------|------|------|
| R0 – R12 | General Purpose | Used by programmers; not banked in user mode |
| **R13** | **SP — Stack Pointer** | Holds the current top-of-stack address. **Banked per mode** (each privileged mode has its own R13). |
| **R14** | **LR — Link Register** | Holds the return address after a subroutine call (`BL` instruction). **Banked per mode.** |
| **R15** | **PC — Program Counter** | Current instruction address + 8 (in ARM state). Readable; writing it causes a branch. |
| **CPSR** | Current Program Status Register | Holds condition flags, interrupt masks, Thumb bit, and current mode. **Not banked.** |
| **SPSR** | Saved Program Status Register | On exception entry, **CPSR is saved into SPSR** of the new mode. **Banked per privileged mode.** |

**CPSR / SPSR bit layout (re-iterated):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bits 31-28:} & \quad N, Z, C, V \\
\text{bit  27:} & \quad Q \text{ (sticky overflow)} \\
\text{bit  7:} & \quad I \text{ (IRQ mask)} \\
\text{bit  6:} & \quad F \text{ (FIQ mask)} \\
\text{bit  5:} & \quad T \text{ (Thumb state)} \\
\text{bits 4-0:} & \quad M[4:0] \text{ (mode bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key behaviors:**
- Writing to R15 (`MOV PC, R0`) = branch.
- The lowest two bits of PC are always 0 in ARM state (instructions are word-aligned).
- In Thumb state, bit 0 of R15 is always 0, bit 1 is ignored.

**(2 marks)** Naming and roles of R0–R12, R13, R14, R15.
**(2 marks)** CPSR and SPSR with banked/unbanked distinction.
**(2 marks)** Bit layout of CPSR with flag meanings.
**(1 mark)** Programming convention notes (e.g., R13 as SP).

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**The Seven Operating Modes:**

| Mode | Code (M[4:0]) | Purpose |
|------|----------------|---------|
| **User (USR)** | `10000` | Normal unprivileged execution |
| **FIQ** | `10001` | Fast interrupt, supports high-speed data transfer |
| **IRQ** | `10010` | Normal interrupt handling |
| **Supervisor (SVC)** | `10011` | Software interrupt (SWI), OS kernel mode, reset |
| **Abort (ABT)** | `10111` | Memory access violations |
| **Undefined (UND)** | `11011` | Undefined instruction traps (e.g., coprocessor) |
| **System (SYS)** | `11111` | Privileged mode using USR register set |

**Mode Transitions:**

1. **External event** (interrupt, reset) → hardware writes M[4:0] of CPSR.
2. **Software execution** of SWI → enters Supervisor.
3. **Software writes** to CPSR M-bits → manual switch (privileged only).

**On exception entry, the ARM automatically:**
1. Saves CPSR into SPSR of the new mode.
2. Sets LR\_mode = return address (PC + 4 or PC + 8).
3. Sets CPSR M[4:0] = new mode code.
4. Sets I = 1 (mask IRQ) or F = 1 (mask FIQ) as appropriate.
5. Forces PC = vector address (e.g., `0x00000018` for IRQ).

**FIQ vs IRQ — Specific Example:**

| Aspect | FIQ | IRQ |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Vector address | `0x0000001C` | `0x00000018` |
| Priority | Higher (cannot be interrupted by IRQ) | Lower (can be interrupted by FIQ) |
| Banked registers | **R8–R14 banked** (extra 5) | Only R13, R14 banked |
| Tail-chaining speed | Very fast (less stacking) | Slower |
| Use case | Time-critical (DMA completion, A/D done) | General peripherals (UART RX, timers) |

**Return from exception:** `SUBS PC, LR, #4` for IRQ/Abort; `MOVS PC, LR` for SVC/FIQ/UND. The `S` suffix restores CPSR from SPSR.

**(2 marks)** All 7 modes listed with purpose.
**(2 marks)** Mode transition mechanism (steps 1–5 above).
**(2 marks)** FIQ vs IRQ comparison table.
**(1 mark)** Exception return instruction explanation.

---

## Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing 3-stage vs 5-stage pipeline** — Many students wrongly attribute the 5 stages to ARM7 or say ARM9 has 3 stages. **Always state ARM7 = 3, ARM9 = 5.**
> 2. **Wrong endianness** — Saying ARM9 is fixed little-endian. It is **bi-endian**; the BIGEND pin selects at reset.
> 3. **Forgetting Thumb state** — When asked "what is Thumb?", many only say "compressed instructions" without mentioning the **30% code density / 30% slower** trade-off or the `BX` instruction to switch states.
> 4. **Confusing CPSR vs SPSR** — SPSR is **banked per mode** and is **not accessible in User mode**. CPSR is **always accessible in privileged modes** but **read-only in User mode**.
> 5. **Branch penalty = 1 (not 2)** — On ARM9, a taken branch discards **two instructions** from the pipeline, giving a 2-cycle penalty, not 1.
> 6. **Wrong M-bits** — Mode bits are in CPSR\[4:0\] (5 bits), not 3 or 4 bits. User mode = `0b10000`, not `0`.
> 7. **Skipping the Barrel Shifter** — It is a **separate hardware block** that pre-shifts operands for the ALU. Don't merge it with the ALU.
> 8. **Confusing AMBA buses** — AHB is for high-speed (CPU, memory), APB is for slow peripherals, and a **bridge** connects them. Do not say "APB for CPU".

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam Day):**
> - [ ] ARM9 = **ARMv4T** architecture, **5-stage** pipeline, **Harvard** bus structure.
> - [ ] Stages: **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB**.
> - [ ] **31 × 32-bit** visible registers (R0–R15, with 6 status registers).
> - [ ] **R13 = SP**, **R14 = LR**, **R15 = PC** — all 32-bit.
> - [ ] **CPSR** is the active status register; **SPSR** is banked per privileged mode.
> - [ ] CPSR\[31:28\] = **N Z C V**; CPSR\[7:6\] = **I F**; CPSR\[5\] = **T**; CPSR\[4:0\] = **M\[4:0\]**.
> - [ ] **7 modes**: USR, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS — first is unprivileged.
> - [ ] **Thumb** = 16-bit compressed ISA; **ARM** = 32-bit full ISA. Switch via `BX Rn` (LSB of Rn).
> - [ ] **Barrel shifter** = pre-ALU shift in 1 cycle.
> - [ ] **Modified Booth (radix-4)** for 32×32 → 32 multiplication.
> - [ ] **Bi-endian**: BIGEND pin selects little or big.
> - [ ] **AMBA**: AHB (high-speed), ASB (legacy), APB (peripherals) connected via **bridge**.
> - [ ] **Branch penalty on ARM9 = 2 cycles** for a taken branch.
> - [ ] **Ideal CPI = 1**; with branches CPI > 1.
> - [ ] **Performance gain over ARM7**: ~1.67× clock (5/3), ~2.5× end-to-end.
> - [ ] **Pipeline speedup formula**: $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + (k-1)}$.
> - [ ] **Effective CPI**: $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + B \cdot P$.
> - [ ] **Exception vector table** at address `0x00000000` (Reset, UND, SVC, PABT, DABT, reserved, IRQ, FIQ).
> - [ ] **Exception return**: `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (IRQ/ABT) or `MOVS PC, LR` (SVC/FIQ/UND) — the `S` restores CPSR from SPSR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPU Bus Organization and Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CPU Bus Organization and Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of CPU Bus Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPU Bus Organization** refers to the structured interconnection of communication pathways (buses) that transfer **data**, **addresses**, and **control signals** between the Central Processing Unit (CPU), memory units, and Input/Output (I/O) peripheral devices within an embedded or computer system.

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, bus organization is the architectural backbone that defines **how components exchange information**, **who gets control** of the shared communication lines, and **under what timing rules** the data transfer occurs. The **Bus Protocol** is the predefined set of rules, signaling conventions, and timing relationships that govern the exchange of information across these shared lines.

### 1.1.1 The Three Fundamental Bus Types

A standard CPU bus system is composed of three logically distinct sub-buses:

| Bus Type | Function | Direction | Typical Width |
|----------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Data Bus** | Carries the actual data being transferred | **Bidirectional** | 8, 16, 32, 64 bits |
| **Address Bus** | Carries the source/destination memory or I/O address | **Unidirectional (CPU → Peripheral)** | 16, 20, 24, 32 bits |
| **Control Bus** | Carries command & timing signals (Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock) | Mixed | Each line is 1 bit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: The **width of the address bus** determines the maximum addressable memory space ($2^n$ locations for an $n$-bit address bus). The **width of the data bus** determines the amount of data transferred per bus cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System"

Imagine a busy city with three types of roads:

1. **Data Bus** = The **main cargo trucks** carrying actual goods (data). They can travel in both directions (bidirectional) — bringing raw materials *into* a factory and shipping finished products *out*.

2. **Address Bus** = The **delivery address labels** on those trucks. They tell the recipient exactly *where* the goods are going. These labels are written only *from* the CPU (unidirectional).

3. **Control Bus** = The **traffic police and traffic lights** (e.g., Read/Write signals, Ready, Interrupt). They do not carry goods but instead coordinate the *flow*, *direction*, and *timing* of traffic.

> [!TIP]
> **Bus Protocol** is essentially the **"Highway Traffic Rules"** — the agreed-upon rules (e.g., who goes first, when to stop, when to go) that all vehicles (devices) connected to the highway must obey. Without these rules, collisions (data corruption) would occur.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard bus metrics every KTU student must memorize:
> - **Bus Bandwidth (Throughput)** is measured in **MB/s** (Megabytes per second) or **GB/s**.
> - **Bus Width** is measured in **bits**.
> - **Bus Clock Frequency** is measured in **MHz** or **GHz**.
> - **Bus Latency** is measured in **clock cycles** or **nanoseconds (ns)**.
> - **Standard Address Space:** A 32-bit address bus addresses $2^{32} = \mathbf{4 \text{ GB}}$ of memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of CPU Bus Organization

### 2.1.1 Single Bus Architecture
In the simplest configuration, **all components** (CPU, memory, I/O) share **one common bus**. While this is **cost-effective** and **easy to design**, it creates a major bottleneck — only **one data transfer** can occur at any given time across the entire system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The single bus suffers from the **"Single Shared Resource Problem"** — performance degrades significantly as the number of devices increases.

### 2.1.2 Multiple Bus Architecture
Modern embedded systems (e.g., ARM-based SoCs) use a **hierarchical multi-bus architecture** to overcome the single-bus bottleneck:

- **Local / CPU Bus:** High-speed, private connection between CPU and **Cache / Main Memory**.
- **System Bus:** Connects the **Memory Controller** to high-speed peripherals.
- **Peripheral / I/O Bus:** Lower-speed bus (e.g., AMBA APB) for slow peripherals like UART, GPIO, Timers.

> [!TIP]
> The **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** by ARM is the de-facto industry standard in modern embedded SoCs. It uses **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** for high-speed and **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** for low-power peripherals.

## 2.2 Bus Arbitration — "Who Gets Control of the Bus?"

When **multiple devices** (e.g., CPU and DMA controller) want to use the bus simultaneously, a **bus arbiter** decides who wins. Three common schemes are used:

| Arbitration Method | How It Works | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Daisy Chain Arbitration** | Devices connected in a serial chain; priority is fixed by physical position | Simple, low hardware cost | Fixed priority, not flexible |
| **Polling Arbitration** | Arbiter sends a poll signal to each device in sequence | Fair, programmable priority | Slow if many devices |
| **Independent Request (Parallel)** | Each device has dedicated Request and Grant lines | Fastest response | Expensive (2N lines for N devices) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "How"?** Arbitration prevents **data collision** on the shared bus. Only the **bus master** that wins arbitration is allowed to drive the data/address lines during a bus cycle.

## 2.3 Bus Protocols — Synchronous vs. Asynchronous

### 2.3.1 Synchronous Bus Protocol
- All operations are **synchronized to a common clock** line.
- Data transfer happens at fixed time intervals determined by the clock.
- **Faster** and **simpler** to implement.
- Used in: **AMBA AHB, PCI, SDRAM interfaces.**

### 2.3.2 Asynchronous Bus Protocol
- **No shared clock.** Instead, a **handshaking** mechanism is used.
- Two key control signals: **Request (REQ)** and **Acknowledge (ACK)**.
- **Slower** but **works with devices of any speed** — useful for legacy or mixed-speed peripherals.
- Used in: **I²C, USB, legacy peripheral buses.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse "asynchronous bus" with "no timing control." This is **FALSE**. Asynchronous buses use **handshaking** for timing control — it just isn't tied to a global clock.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **core mathematical relationships** for bus analysis. Master these for KTU numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_w = \dfrac{N_{lines} \times f_{clk}}{8}$ | Maximum Bus Bandwidth (data rate) | **MB/s** |
| 2 | $N_{addr} = 2^{n}$ | Addressable memory locations for $n$-bit address bus | Locations |
| 3 | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Duration of one bus clock cycle | **seconds (s)** |
| 4 | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{N_{bytes}}{B_w}$ | Time to transfer $N_{bytes}$ at bandwidth $B_w$ | **seconds** |
| 5 | $T_{total} = (N_{wait} + N_{data}) \times T_{cycle}$ | Total transfer time with $N_{wait}$ wait states | **seconds** |
| 6 | $E_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{data} + N_{wait}}$ | Effective data transfer efficiency | **Ratio (0 to 1)** |
| 7 | $A_{space} = 2^{n} \times W_{word}$ | Total addressable memory space | **Bytes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pipe safeguard:** In the table above, note the use of `2^{n}` instead of $2^n$ to avoid the markdown table-breaking pipe character `|`.

### 2.4.1 Variable Definitions
- $B_w$ = Bus Bandwidth (MB/s)
- $N_{lines}$ = Number of data bus lines (bits per transfer)
- $f_{clk}$ = Bus clock frequency (Hz)
- $n$ = Address bus width (bits)
- $N_{wait}$ = Number of wait states inserted by slow devices
- $N_{data}$ = Number of data beats (transfers) per burst
- $W_{word}$ = Word size in bytes

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CPU bus organization is the **lifeblood of every modern electronic system**:

- **Smartphones & Tablets:** ARM-based SoCs use **AMBA AXI** for CPU-to-memory traffic and **APB** for sensor/IO traffic.
- **Automotive ECUs:** **CAN (Controller Area Network)** bus protocol is used for communication between ECUs in a vehicle.
- **IoT Devices:** **I²C and SPI** are the workhorses for connecting low-speed sensors to microcontrollers.
- **Servers & PCs:** **PCIe (PCI Express)** is the dominant high-speed bus for connecting GPUs, NVMe SSDs, and network cards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bus Bandwidth from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A system has a **32-bit data bus** running at a clock frequency of **100 MHz**. Compute the **maximum theoretical bandwidth** in MB/s.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**
The data bus width $N_{lines} = 32$ bits and the bus clock frequency $f_{clk} = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$.

**Step 2 — Recall the fundamental definition of bandwidth.**
Bandwidth is defined as the **amount of data transferred per unit time**. Per clock cycle, the bus transfers $N_{lines}$ bits.

**Step 3 — Set up the dimensional equation.**
The number of bits transferred per second is simply:

$$B_{bits} = N_{lines} \times f_{clk}$$

**Step 4 — Convert bits to bytes.**
Since $1 \text{ Byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$, we divide by 8 to get bytes per second:

$$B_w = \frac{N_{lines} \times f_{clk}}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
B_w &= \frac{32 \times 100 \times 10^{6}}{8} \\
B_w &= \frac{3200 \times 10^{6}}{8} \\
B_w &= 400 \times 10^{6} \text{ Bytes/s} \\
B_w &= 400 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — State the final answer.**
> The maximum theoretical bandwidth of the bus is $\mathbf{400 \text{ MB/s}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Transfer Time with Wait States

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
A CPU needs to read **16 bytes** of data from a slow peripheral across a **16-bit data bus** operating at **50 MHz**. The peripheral inserts **2 wait states** per 16-bit transfer. Calculate the **total transfer time** and the **effective data rate**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the bus clock period.**
The clock period is the reciprocal of frequency:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} \\
T_{cycle} &= 20 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of data beats required.**
Since the bus is 16 bits wide and we need 16 bytes (128 bits):

$$N_{data} = \frac{16 \text{ Bytes}}{2 \text{ Bytes/beat}} = 8 \text{ beats}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total number of wait states.**
The peripheral inserts 2 wait states per transfer, so:

$$N_{wait} = 2 \times N_{data} = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ wait states}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total cycles per transfer.**
Every data beat consists of 1 normal cycle + the wait states:

$$N_{total\_cycles} = N_{data} + N_{wait} = 8 + 16 = 24 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total transfer time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= N_{total\_cycles} \times T_{cycle} \\
T_{total} &= 24 \times 20 \text{ ns} \\
T_{total} &= 480 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the effective data rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{eff} &= \frac{16 \text{ Bytes}}{480 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \\
R_{eff} &= 33.33 \times 10^{6} \text{ Bytes/s} \\
R_{eff} &= 33.33 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the efficiency ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{rate} &= \frac{N_{data}}{N_{data} + N_{wait}} = \frac{8}{24} \\
E_{rate} &= 0.3333 \text{ or } 33.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

> The total transfer time is $\mathbf{480 \text{ ns}}$, effective rate is $\mathbf{33.33 \text{ MB/s}}$, and the bus efficiency is only $\mathbf{33.33\%}$ due to wait states.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Address Space Calculation

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
A microcontroller has a **24-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data word**. Compute the **total addressable memory space** in **KB**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the number of unique addresses.**

$$N_{addr} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by word size to get total bytes.**

$$A_{space} = N_{addr} \times W_{word} = 2^{24} \times 2 \text{ Bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{space} &= 2^{25} \text{ Bytes} \\
A_{space} &= 33{,}554{,}432 \text{ Bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to KB.**

$$A_{space} = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{1024} = 32{,}768 \text{ KB} = 32 \text{ MB}$$

> The system can address a total of $\mathbf{32 \text{ MB}}$ of memory.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Synchronous Bus | Asynchronous Bus | Multi-Layer Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clock Required?** | Yes | No (Handshake) | Yes (per layer) |
| **Speed** | Very High | Low to Medium | Very High |
| **Hardware Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Flexibility** | Low (all devices must match clock) | High | Very High |
| **Example** | SDRAM, PCI, AHB | I²C, UART, USB | AMBA AXI, NoC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: CPU Bus Organization (Top-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core]
    DB[Data Bus 32 bit]
    AB[Address Bus 32 bit]
    CB[Control Bus]
    MEM[Main Memory RAM ROM]
    IOA[I O Module A]
    IOB[I O Module B]
    DMA[DMA Controller]
    ARB[Bus Arbiter]

    CPU --- DB
    CPU --- AB
    CPU --- CB
    CPU --- ARB
    DB --- MEM
    DB --- IOA
    DB --- IOB
    DB --- DMA
    AB --- MEM
    AB --- IOA
    AB --- IOB
    AB --- DMA
    CB --- MEM
    CB --- IOA
    CB --- IOB
    CB --- DMA
    ARB --- DMA
    ARB --- IOA
    ARB --- IOB
```

> The diagram above illustrates a **single shared bus system** where the CPU, memory, I/O modules, and the DMA controller all compete for the same data, address, and control lines under the supervision of a **Bus Arbiter**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: AMBA Multi-Bus Architecture (Industry Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE [Processor Core]
        CPU1[ARM CPU]
    end

    subgraph AHB_GROUP [High Performance AHB Bus]
        CPU1
        DMA1[DMA Controller]
        MEMC[Memory Controller SRAM SDRAM]
        ETH1[Ethernet MAC]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE [AHB to APB Bridge]
        DMA1
    end

    subgraph APB_GROUP [Low Power APB Bus]
        UART1[UART]
        GPIO1[GPIO]
        TIMER1[Timers]
        I2C1[I2C Controller]
    end

    BRIDGE --- UART1
    BRIDGE --- GPIO1
    BRIDGE --- TIMER1
    BRIDGE --- I2C1
```

> The **AMBA architecture** is the **industry standard** for ARM-based embedded SoCs. The high-speed **AHB** segment connects the CPU, memory, and high-bandwidth peripherals, while the **APB** segment handles low-power, low-speed peripherals via a bridge.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Synchronous Bus Handshaking Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master CPU
    participant B as Bus Lines
    participant S as Slave Memory

    Note over M,S: Clock Edge aligned transfer
    M->>B: Place ADDRESS on Address Bus
    M->>B: Assert CONTROL = READ
    Note over S: Slave decodes address
    S->>B: Place DATA on Data Bus
    Note over S: One clock cycle later
    M->>B: Latch DATA into register
    M->>B: De-assert CONTROL
```

> This timing diagram shows the **synchronous read cycle** where every action is locked to a clock edge — a defining feature of synchronous bus protocols.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Asynchronous Handshaking Protocol

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S as Slave

    M->>S: REQ = 1 (Request data)
    Note over S: Slave processes request
    S->>M: ACK = 1 (Data ready)
    Note over M: Master latches data
    M->>S: REQ = 0
    S->>M: ACK = 0
    Note over M,S: Cycle complete
```

> This **four-phase handshake** is the heart of asynchronous bus protocols such as I²C — the master and slave negotiate every transfer using request/acknowledge signals without needing a shared clock.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a bus. List and briefly explain the three types of buses used in a CPU system.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common signal lines used to transfer data, addresses, and control information between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

The **three types of buses** are:

1. **Data Bus:** A bidirectional set of lines that carries the **actual data** being transferred between source and destination. Its width (8, 16, 32, 64 bits) determines the amount of data transferred per cycle.

2. **Address Bus:** A unidirectional set of lines (from CPU to peripherals) that carries the **memory or I/O address** of the source or destination. Its width determines the addressable memory space ($2^n$ locations).

3. **Control Bus:** A set of lines that carries **command and timing signals** such as Read/Write (RD, WR), Memory/IO select, Bus Request (BR), Bus Grant (BG), and Interrupt signals. It coordinates the operation of devices connected to the bus.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous bus protocols. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Synchronous Bus | Asynchronous Bus |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock** | Uses a common shared clock | No shared clock |
| **Timing** | Fixed timing tied to clock edges | Variable timing using handshaking |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower |
| **Flexibility** | Low — all devices must match clock | High — works with mixed-speed devices |
| **Handshaking** | Not required | Required (REQ / ACK signals) |
| **Example** | AMBA AHB, PCI, SDRAM | I²C, USB, legacy peripheral bus |

> Synchronous buses are **faster but rigid**; asynchronous buses are **slower but more flexible**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain the various bus arbitration schemes used in CPU bus organization with neat diagrams. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

When multiple devices (bus masters) need to use the shared bus simultaneously, a **bus arbiter** decides which device gets control. This is called **bus arbitration**. The three major schemes are:

**1. Daisy Chain Arbitration:**
Devices are connected in a **serial daisy chain** with request and grant lines passing through them in sequence. The device **closest to the arbiter** has the **highest priority**. It is simple, cheap, and requires only 2 lines. However, the priority is **fixed by physical position** and **cannot be changed dynamically**.

**2. Polling (Rotating Priority) Arbitration:**
The arbiter generates a **set of poll lines** (e.g., for 4 devices, 2 poll lines are used to generate 4 binary codes). The arbiter sends poll codes in sequence on these lines; the device that recognizes its code is granted the bus. This allows **programmable and fair priority** but is **slow** when many devices are present.

**3. Independent Request (Parallel) Arbitration:**
Each device has a **dedicated Bus Request (BR)** and **Bus Grant (BG)** line connected directly to the arbiter. The arbiter uses a **priority encoder** to resolve simultaneous requests. This is the **fastest** method (single arbitration cycle) but requires **2N signal lines** for N devices, making it **expensive**.

**Conclusion:** The choice of arbitration depends on the trade-off between **speed, hardware cost, and flexibility**.

---

**(b) A 32-bit computer has a system bus with a 32-bit data bus and a 50 MHz clock. Compute the maximum bus bandwidth. If 4 wait states are inserted per transfer, calculate the new effective data rate. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Data bus width $N_{lines} = 32$ bits
- Clock frequency $f_{clk} = 50 \text{ MHz}$
- Wait states per transfer = 4

**Step 1 — Maximum Bus Bandwidth (theoretical):**

$$B_w = \frac{N_{lines} \times f_{clk}}{8} = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^{6}}{8} = 200 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Data Rate with Wait States:**

Total cycles per transfer $= 1 \text{ (data)} + 4 \text{ (wait)} = 5 \text{ cycles}$

Efficiency $E_{rate} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2$ (or 20%)

$$R_{eff} = B_w \times E_{rate} = 200 \times 0.2 = 40 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Result:**
- Maximum theoretical bandwidth = **200 MB/s**
- Effective data rate with wait states = **40 MB/s**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the AMBA bus architecture used in ARM-based embedded systems. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is ARM's open-standard on-chip bus protocol for SoC designs. It defines **three buses**:

**1. AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):**
A high-speed, high-bandwidth bus used to connect the **CPU, on-chip memory, DMA controller, and high-speed peripherals** (Ethernet MAC, USB, etc.). It supports **burst transfers, pipelined operation, and split transactions** for maximum throughput.

**2. ASB (Advanced System Bus):**
An older, lower-performance alternative to AHB. It is still used in some legacy AMBA 2.0 designs but has been largely replaced by AHB.

**3. APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):**
A **low-power, low-complexity** bus for connecting **slow peripherals** such as UART, GPIO, Timers, and I²C controllers. It uses a **simplified protocol** (no burst, no pipelining) to minimize power consumption. An **AHB-to-APB bridge** interfaces APB peripherals to the AHB backbone.

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2):**
The ARM CPU is connected to the AHB backbone along with high-speed peripherals and the memory controller. The APB bridge then fans out to all low-speed peripherals.

**Advantages of AMBA:**
- Modular and scalable
- IP vendor independent
- Optimized for low power (ideal for embedded systems)

---

**(b) Explain the bus protocol used in I²C communication with a timing diagram. Why is it called a synchronous or asynchronous bus? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)** is a **multi-master, serial, two-wire bus** developed by Philips. It uses only **two lines**:

- **SCL (Serial Clock Line)** — carries the clock generated by the master.
- **SDA (Serial Data Line)** — carries the actual data (bidirectional).

**Protocol Operation:**
1. The master issues a **START condition** (SDA goes LOW while SCL is HIGH).
2. The master transmits a **7-bit slave address** + 1-bit R/W direction.
3. The addressed slave returns an **ACK bit** (pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock).
4. Data bytes are transferred, each followed by an ACK bit.
5. The master issues a **STOP condition** (SDA goes HIGH while SCL is HIGH).

**Timing Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.4):**
The sequence shows the START bit, address, ACK, data, and STOP bit, all synchronized to the SCL clock line.

**Is I²C Synchronous or Asynchronous?**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> I²C is classified as a **synchronous serial bus** because **data transmission is synchronized to a shared clock line (SCL)**. However, the SCL clock is **not a free-running global clock** — it is **generated by the master only during active transfers** and **stretched by the slave** when it needs more time (a feature called **clock stretching**). Hence, some texts call it a *"synchronous but clock-on-demand"* bus.

**Key Features of I²C:**
- Only 2 wires
- Supports multiple masters and slaves (addressed by 7/10-bit address)
- Standard speeds: 100 kbps (Standard), 400 kbps (Fast), 3.4 Mbps (High-Speed)
- Pull-up resistors required on both lines

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing bus types with their directionality:** Students often write that the *data bus* is unidirectional. **WRONG.** Data bus is **bidirectional**; only the address bus is unidirectional.
>
> 2. **Address space formula misuse:** When asked "addressable memory," always give the answer in **bytes**, not just the number of locations. For a 16-bit address bus, write $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations $= 64 \text{ KB}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the `÷8` in bandwidth calculation:** A 32-bit bus at 100 MHz gives **400 MB/s**, not 3,200 MB/s. Always divide by 8 to convert bits to bytes.
>
> 4. **Mixing up bus standards:** AMBA = ARM standard, I²C = Philips, CAN = Bosch (automotive), PCIe = Intel. Mixing these up costs **direct concept marks**.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the arbiter in block diagrams:** In KTU, any diagram showing multiple masters **must include a bus arbiter**, or marks are deducted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for CPU Bus Organization and Protocol:**

- **Bus Definition:** A shared communication pathway for data, address, and control signals.
- **Three Bus Types:** Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional), Control (mixed).
- **Address Space:** $2^n$ locations for an $n$-bit address bus.
- **Bus Bandwidth Formula:** $B_w = \dfrac{N_{lines} \times f_{clk}}{8}$ in **MB/s**.
- **Wait State Formula:** $E_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{data} + N_{wait}}$.
- **Arbitration Schemes:** Daisy Chain (cheap, fixed priority), Polling (fair, programmable), Independent Request (fast, expensive).
- **Synchronous Bus:** Uses a common clock. **Faster, simpler.** Example: AMBA AHB, PCI.
- **Asynchronous Bus:** Uses handshaking (REQ/ACK). **Slower, flexible.** Example: I²C.
- **AMBA Architecture:** AHB (high speed) + APB (low power) + Bridge connecting them. Industry standard for ARM.
- **I²C:** 2-wire (SCL + SDA), 7/10-bit addressing, synchronous clock-on-demand.
- **Single Bus vs Multi-Bus:** Single is cheap but bottlenecked; Multi-bus is faster and more complex.
- **Bus Master vs Bus Slave:** Masters can initiate transfers (CPU, DMA); Slaves only respond (Memory, peripherals).
- **DMA (Direct Memory Access):** Allows peripherals to transfer data to/from memory **without CPU intervention**, freeing the CPU for other tasks — a major performance optimization in modern embedded systems.

> **End of Module 1 — CPU Bus Organization and Protocol Notes**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Five Stage Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Five Stage Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Stage Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where instruction execution is divided into **five distinct, sequential sub-operations** (stages) that can be overlapped in time. Each stage performs a specific sub-task on a different instruction simultaneously, thereby increasing the instruction throughput of the processor without reducing the execution time of an individual instruction.

The classical **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** five-stage pipeline divides instruction processing into the following phases:

| Stage | Acronym | Operation Performed |
|-------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **IF** | Instruction Fetch from instruction memory |
| 2 | **ID** | Instruction Decode & Register Fetch |
| 3 | **EX** | Execute (ALU operation) or calculate effective address |
| 4 | **MEM** | Memory Access (load/store data memory) |
| 5 | **WB** | Write Back result to register file |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Embedded System Design context, this concept is foundational because modern embedded processors (e.g., **ARM Cortex-M3/M4**, **RISC-V RV32I**) employ variants of this 3-stage to 5-stage pipeline architecture to balance power, performance, and area (PPA) constraints in real-time embedded applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Assembly Line

Imagine a small laundry shop with **5 workers** and **5 washing machines**, but only **1 drying station**, **1 folding table**, and **1 packaging desk**.

- **Without Pipelining (Sequential):** Worker 1 takes a load: washes → dries → folds → packs → delivers. Only then Worker 1 starts the next load. **Time per load = sum of all stages.**
- **With Pipelining (Assembly Line):** Worker 1 washes Load 1, then immediately moves to wash Load 2 while Worker 2 dries Load 1. As soon as Load 1 is dried, Worker 3 starts folding, while Worker 4 packs the folded clothes. At steady state, **one complete load finishes every "stage time"**, drastically improving throughput.

**Key Insight:** The *latency* of one load remains the same, but the *throughput* improves by a factor of **5** (ideally). Similarly, in a 5-stage pipeline, a single instruction still takes **5 clock cycles**, but **one instruction completes every cycle**.

## 1.3 Standard Pipeline Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The standard assumption for KTU problems is that all five stages require **exactly 1 clock cycle each**, unless explicitly stated otherwise. The clock period $T$ is therefore set by the **slowest stage** (worst-case combinational delay).

- **Clock Period ($T$):** Time taken by the slowest pipeline stage.
- **Pipeline Latency:** Time for a single instruction to traverse all 5 stages = $5T$.
- **Pipeline Throughput:** Number of instructions completed per unit time = $\frac{1}{T}$ (at steady state).
- **Ideal Speedup:** $\frac{\text{Time without pipeline}}{\text{Time with pipeline}} \to 5$ for a balanced 5-stage pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Five Stages

### Stage 1 — Instruction Fetch (IF)
- The **Program Counter (PC)** provides the address of the next instruction.
- Instruction is read from the **Instruction Memory (I-Mem)**.
- Simultaneously, **PC + 4** is computed (for word-aligned RISC ISAs).
- Output is latched into the **IF/ID pipeline register** at the rising clock edge.

### Stage 2 — Instruction Decode & Register Fetch (ID)
- The 32-bit instruction is **decoded** to identify opcode and operands.
- Source registers (`Rs1`, `Rs2`) and the destination register (`Rd`) are read from the **Register File**.
- For immediate-type instructions, the 16-bit immediate is **sign-extended** to 32 bits.
- Outputs (decoded control signals, register values, extended immediate) are latched into the **ID/EX pipeline register**.

### Stage 3 — Execute (EX)
- The **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** performs the required operation.
- For R-type: ALU operates on `Rs1` and `Rs2` (e.g., `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `OR`).
- For Load/Store: ALU computes the **effective memory address** = `Base Register + Offset`.
- For Branch: ALU compares registers and computes the **branch target address**.
- Outputs (ALU result, destination register index, control flags) are latched into the **EX/MEM pipeline register**.

### Stage 4 — Memory Access (MEM)
- Active **only for Load and Store** instructions.
- For Load (`LW`): Data is read from **Data Memory (D-Mem)** at the computed address.
- For Store (`SW`): Data is written to **D-Mem** at the computed address.
- For all other instructions, this stage acts as a **pass-through** (a "bubble" or no-operation stage).
- Outputs latched into the **MEM/WB pipeline register**.

### Stage 5 — Write Back (WB)
- Active **only for instructions that produce a register result** (R-type, Load, etc.).
- Result is written back into the destination register (`Rd`) of the register file.
- For Store and Branch, **no write-back** occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Registers** (`IF/ID`, `ID/EX`, `EX/MEM`, `MEM/WB`) are the **synchronization boundaries** that hold intermediate results between stages. They are clocked flip-flop banks that prevent data races and ensure deterministic behaviour. There are **4 pipeline registers** for a 5-stage pipeline.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Underlying Logic

- **Why pipeline?** Because **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** of a non-pipelined processor is high (e.g., 4–5 for a RISC). Pipelining reduces the **effective CPI toward 1**, multiplying throughput.
- **How?** By exploiting **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** through **temporal overlap** of independent sub-tasks. Each sub-task operates on a *different* instruction.
- **Trade-off:** Increased hardware (5× combinational logic + 4 pipeline registers), more complex control, and the introduction of **pipeline hazards** that must be resolved.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit / Value |
|------------------|---------|--------------|
| $T_{\text{clock}}$ | Clock period = max stage delay | seconds (s) |
| $T_{\text{latency}}$ | Time for 1 instruction to finish | $5 \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$ |
| $T_{\text{throughput}}$ | Time between instruction completions (steady state) | $1 \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$ |
| $N$ | Total number of instructions | dimensionless |
| $S_{\text{ideal}}$ | Ideal speedup | $5$ (for balanced 5-stage) |
| $S_{\text{actual}}$ | Actual speedup | $\dfrac{5N}{N + 4}$ (assuming no stalls) |
| $CPI_{\text{ideal}}$ | Ideal CPI | $1$ |
| $CPI_{\text{actual}}$ | CPI with stalls | $1 + \text{stall cycles per instruction}$ |
| $T_{\text{exec}}$ | Total execution time for $N$ instructions | $(N - 1 + 5) \cdot T_{\text{clock}} = (N + 4) \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$ |
| Speedup formula | General | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{non-pipelined}}}{T_{\text{pipelined}}}$ |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | Pipeline efficiency | $\dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{N}{N + k - 1}$ where $k=5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formula** for KTU problems: For a 5-stage pipeline processing $N$ instructions without any hazards, the total number of clock cycles required is $N + 4$ (the first instruction takes 5 cycles to drain, and each subsequent instruction completes in 1 additional cycle).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Embedded Engineering

- **ARM Cortex-M0/M0+:** 2-stage pipeline (Fetch + Execute) — ultra-low power for IoT.
- **ARM Cortex-M3/M4:** 3-stage pipeline (Fetch + Decode + Execute) — balanced for real-time control.
- **ARM Cortex-M7:** 6-stage superscalar pipeline — high-performance DSP applications.
- **RISC-V SiFive E31:** 5-stage in-order pipeline — reference open-source embedded core.
- **Automotive ECUs, IoT edge nodes, motor controllers, and sensor fusion MCUs** all rely on pipelining to meet strict **latency-deadline** constraints in **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Execution Time for N Instructions

**Given:** A balanced 5-stage RISC pipeline, with each stage taking exactly 1 clock cycle of period $T$.

**Step 1 — Identify the pipeline fill phase.**
In the first clock cycle, only **Stage 1 (IF)** operates on Instruction 1. No other stage has work.

**Step 2 — Identify the steady-state phase.**
Starting from cycle 2, every clock cycle sees **all 5 stages active simultaneously**, each handling a different instruction. This continues until the last instruction enters Stage 1.

**Step 3 — Identify the pipeline drain phase.**
After the last instruction ($I_N$) enters Stage 1, it must still propagate through the remaining 4 stages. This takes 4 more clock cycles.

**Step 4 — Combine the phases.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total cycles} &= \text{Fill cycles} + \text{Steady-state cycles} + \text{Drain cycles} \\
&= 1 + (N - 1) + 4 \\
&= N + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to time.**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = (N + 4) \cdot T_{\text{clock}}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** The "+4" accounts for the 4 extra cycles needed for the final instruction to traverse Stages 2 through 5 after Stage 1 is no longer fed new instructions. This is a **first-order derivation** assuming **zero hazards**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ideal Speedup for N Instructions

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined execution time.**
In a non-pipelined processor, each instruction must fully complete (all 5 stages sequentially) before the next begins. Time per instruction = $5T$. For $N$ instructions:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipelined}} = 5N \cdot T
$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined execution time (from Derivation 1).**

$$
T_{\text{pipelined}} = (N + 4) \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Form the speedup ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{non-pipelined}}}{T_{\text{pipelined}}} = \frac{5N \cdot T}{(N + 4) \cdot T} \\
S &= \frac{5N}{N + 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{5N}{N + 4} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{5}{\left(1 + \frac{4}{N}\right)} = 5
$$

**Logic Explanation:** For very large programs, the fixed "+4" overhead becomes negligible, and the **maximum achievable speedup** equals the number of pipeline stages ($k = 5$). This is the theoretical upper bound for an **ideal, balanced, hazard-free pipeline**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speedup with Pipeline Stalls

**Given:** The pipeline incurs $S$ stall cycles distributed across $N$ instructions, in addition to the base $N + 4$ cycles.

**Step 1 — Total cycles with stalls.**

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{total}} = (N + 4) + S
$$

**Step 2 — New execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{exec, stalled}} = (N + 4 + S) \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Actual speedup formula.**

$$
S_{\text{actual}} = \frac{5N \cdot T}{(N + 4 + S) \cdot T} = \frac{5N}{N + 4 + S}
$$

**Step 4 — Average CPI (a frequently asked KTU quantity).**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Cycles}_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{N + 4 + S}{N} = 1 + \frac{4 + S}{N}
$$

> For $N \gg S$, $\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \to 1$ — confirming the ideal pipeline behavior.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pipeline Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test hazard identification. A hazard is any condition that **prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated cycle**, forcing a stall.

| Hazard Type | Cause | Typical Effect | Common Resolution |
|-------------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Structural** | Two instructions need the **same hardware resource** in the same cycle | Forced stall | Duplicate hardware (e.g., separate I-Mem & D-Mem) |
| **Data (RAW)** | Read-After-Write dependency: instruction $i+1$ needs the result of instruction $i$ | 2–3 stall cycles | **Forwarding (bypassing)**, pipeline interlock, or code scheduling |
| **Data (WAR)** | Write-After-Read dependency (rare in 5-stage in-order) | Potential write before read | Register renaming (out-of-order cores) |
| **Data (WAW)** | Write-After-Write dependency | Wrong final value | Stall or renaming |
| **Control (Branch)** | PC decision known only after EX stage | Flush 2–3 fetched instructions | **Branch prediction**, delay slots, early branch resolution |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 5-stage RISC pipeline with clock period $T = 2 \text{ ns}$. Compute the total execution time and speedup for executing $N = 100$ instructions, assuming (a) no hazards, and (b) exactly 40 stall cycles due to data dependencies.

### Part (a) — No Hazards

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles} &= N + 4 = 100 + 4 = 104 \\
T_{\text{exec}} &= 104 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 208 \text{ ns} \\
T_{\text{non-pipelined}} &= 5N \cdot T = 500 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 1000 \text{ ns} \\
S &= \frac{1000}{208} = 4.808
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — With 40 Stalls

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles}_{\text{total}} &= 104 + 40 = 144 \\
T_{\text{exec, stalled}} &= 144 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 288 \text{ ns} \\
S_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{1000}{288} \approx 3.472
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Stalls degrade the achievable speedup by approximately **27.7%**, highlighting why embedded designers invest in forwarding paths and branch prediction hardware.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 5-Stage Pipeline Datapath with Pipeline Registers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Program Counter] --> IF1[IF: Instruction Memory]
    IF1 --> RegA[IF ID Register]
    RegA --> ID1[ID: Decode + Register File Read]
    ID1 --> RegB[ID EX Register]
    RegB --> EX1[EX: ALU + Branch Compare]
    EX1 --> RegC[EX MEM Register]
    RegC --> MEM1[MEM: Data Memory Access]
    MEM1 --> RegD[MEM WB Register]
    RegD --> WB1[WB: Write Back to Register File]
    WB1 --> RF[(Register File)]
    RF -.feedback.-> ID1

    subgraph STAGE1["STAGE 1 IF"]
        IF1
    end
    subgraph STAGE2["STAGE 2 ID"]
        ID1
    end
    subgraph STAGE3["STAGE 3 EX"]
        EX1
    end
    subgraph STAGE4["STAGE 4 MEM"]
        MEM1
    end
    subgraph STAGE5["STAGE 5 WB"]
        WB1
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pipeline Timing (Instruction Flow Across Cycles)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Five-Stage Pipeline Timing Diagram (I1 to I5)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  Cycle %s
    section Instruction I1
    IF_I1   :a1, 0, 1
    ID_I1   :a2, 1, 1
    EX_I1   :a3, 2, 1
    MEM_I1  :a4, 3, 1
    WB_I1   :a5, 4, 1
    section Instruction I2
    IF_I2   :b1, 1, 1
    ID_I2   :b2, 2, 1
    EX_I2   :b3, 3, 1
    MEM_I2  :b4, 4, 1
    WB_I2   :b5, 5, 1
    section Instruction I3
    IF_I3   :c1, 2, 1
    ID_I3   :c2, 3, 1
    EX_I3   :c3, 4, 1
    MEM_I3  :c4, 5, 1
    WB_I3   :c5, 6, 1
    section Instruction I4
    IF_I4   :d1, 3, 1
    ID_I4   :d2, 4, 1
    EX_I4   :d3, 5, 1
    MEM_I4  :d4, 6, 1
    WB_I4   :d5, 7, 1
    section Instruction I5
    IF_I5   :e1, 4, 1
    ID_I5   :e2, 5, 1
    EX_I5   :e3, 6, 1
    MEM_I5  :e4, 7, 1
    WB_I5   :e5, 8, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Gantt chart:** Each row represents one instruction. Each column represents one clock cycle. Notice that from **Cycle 4 onwards**, all 5 stages are simultaneously active — this is the **steady-state** region where the pipeline achieves its maximum throughput of 1 instruction per cycle.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hazard Detection and Forwarding Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IDstage[ID Stage: Decode Rs1 and Rs2] --> HZD[Hazard Detection Unit]
    EXstage[EX MEM Pipeline Register] --> HZD
    MEMstage[MEM WB Pipeline Register] --> HZD
    HZD --> Decision{Hazard Detected?}
    Decision -- YES Data RAW --> Stall[Insert Pipeline Bubble Stall]
    Decision -- NO Data Dependency --> ForwardCheck[Check Forwarding Muxes]
    ForwardCheck --> FwdA[Forward A Mux]
    ForwardCheck --> FwdB[Forward B Mux]
    FwdA --> ALU[ALU Inputs]
    FwdB --> ALU
    Stall --> IDstage
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (ARM Cortex-M Series Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Fetch[Fetch Unit] --> Decode[Decode and Issue]
    Decode --> Execute[Execute ALU and MAC]
    Execute --> LS[Load Store Unit]
    LS --> WB[Write Back]
    WB --> RegBank[(Register Bank)]
    RegBank --> Decode
    Execute --> BRU[Branch Unit]
    BRU --> Fetch
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Embedded Mapping:** While classic textbook RISC pipelines are 5-stage, embedded processors (ARM Cortex-M3) use a **3-stage** variant: Fetch → Decode → Execute (with load/store folded in). The fundamental principle of pipelining — **temporal overlap of sub-tasks** — is identical. The 5-stage model is the **canonical academic reference** demanded by the KTU syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Explain the concept of pipelining in a RISC processor. List the five stages of a classical RISC pipeline.

**Model Answer:**

Pipelining is an implementation technique in which multiple instructions are overlapped in execution. The instruction processing is divided into a sequence of independent stages, each stage handling a different instruction simultaneously. This increases instruction throughput without reducing the latency of a single instruction.

The five classical stages of a RISC pipeline are:

1. **IF (Instruction Fetch):** Fetches the instruction from instruction memory using the PC.
2. **ID (Instruction Decode):** Decodes the opcode and reads source registers from the register file.
3. **EX (Execute):** The ALU performs arithmetic/logic operations or computes effective addresses.
4. **MEM (Memory Access):** Reads from or writes to data memory for load/store instructions.
5. **WB (Write Back):** Writes the result back to the destination register in the register file.

The pipeline registers `IF/ID`, `ID/EX`, `EX/MEM`, and `MEM/WB` isolate each stage and store intermediate results.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define pipeline hazard. Differentiate between structural, data, and control hazards with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

A **pipeline hazard** is any condition that prevents the next instruction in the pipeline from executing during its designated clock cycle, thereby reducing the ideal CPI of 1.

| Hazard Type | Definition | Example |
|-------------|------------|---------|
| **Structural** | Two instructions in different stages compete for the same hardware resource. | Single memory port being accessed simultaneously by IF (instruction fetch) and MEM (data load) in the same cycle. |
| **Data (RAW)** | An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that has not yet completed write-back. | `ADD R1, R2, R3` followed immediately by `SUB R4, R1, R5` — `SUB` needs `R1` which is not yet computed. |
| **Control** | The outcome of a branch/jump instruction is not known early enough to fetch the correct next instruction. | A conditional branch resolved in EX stage causes the 2 instructions already fetched in IF and ID to be discarded if branch is taken. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the five-stage pipelined datapath of a RISC processor. Explain the function of each pipeline register. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage RISC pipeline has a clock period of $T = 1.5 \text{ ns}$. Compute the total execution time and speedup for executing 500 instructions: (i) with no hazards, and (ii) with 120 stall cycles. Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The five-stage pipelined datapath is composed of five functional units separated by four pipeline registers. The functional units are:

1. **IF Unit:** Contains the Program Counter (PC), an adder (`PC + 4`), and the instruction memory. It fetches the 32-bit instruction at the address held in the PC.

2. **ID Unit:** Contains the instruction decoder, the register file (with two read ports), and the sign-extension unit for immediate values. It produces decoded control signals and reads two source operands.

3. **EX Unit:** Contains the ALU, a multiplier (optional), and branch comparison logic. The ALU produces arithmetic results or effective memory addresses.

4. **MEM Unit:** Contains the data memory for load and store operations. For non-memory instructions, it acts as a transparent pass-through.

5. **WB Unit:** Routes the result (from ALU or memory) back to the destination register in the register file via a 2-to-1 mux.

**Function of each pipeline register:**

- **IF/ID register:** Latches the fetched instruction and the incremented PC value, providing them to the ID stage.
- **ID/EX register:** Latches the decoded control signals, the two register-read values, the sign-extended immediate, and the destination register index.
- **EX/MEM register:** Latches the ALU result, the data to be stored (for `SW`), and the destination register index for the MEM stage.
- **MEM/WB register:** Latches the value read from memory (for `LW`) or the ALU result (for R-type), along with the destination register index for the WB stage.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for diagram, 1 for stage description each × 5, 0.5 for each pipeline register function × 4, with 0.5 for connecting narrative.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $T = 1.5 \text{ ns}$, $N = 500$ instructions.

**Case (i) — No Hazards:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles}_{\text{no hazard}} &= N + 4 = 500 + 4 = 504 \\
T_{\text{exec, ideal}} &= 504 \times 1.5 \text{ ns} = 756 \text{ ns} \\
T_{\text{non-pipelined}} &= 5N \cdot T = 5 \times 500 \times 1.5 \text{ ns} = 3750 \text{ ns} \\
S_{\text{ideal}} &= \frac{3750}{756} \approx 4.96
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (ii) — With 120 Stalls:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles}_{\text{stalled}} &= 504 + 120 = 624 \\
T_{\text{exec, stalled}} &= 624 \times 1.5 \text{ ns} = 936 \text{ ns} \\
S_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{3750}{936} \approx 4.006
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The 120 stall cycles reduce the speedup from 4.96 to 4.006, a degradation of approximately **19.2%**. This demonstrates that even a relatively small number of stalls (120 out of 504 base cycles ≈ 23.8% extra cycles) can significantly impact pipeline performance, justifying the need for **forwarding paths**, **branch prediction**, and **compiler-level instruction scheduling** in embedded processor design.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the three types of pipeline hazards with suitable examples. Describe how data hazards are resolved using **data forwarding (bypassing)** with a diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 5-stage pipeline processing the following instruction sequence, identify all RAW data hazards and determine the number of stall cycles required if **no forwarding** is implemented. Assume the EX stage produces a result, the MEM stage reads from memory, and WB completes in stage 5. **(7 Marks)**

```
I1: ADD R1, R2, R3
I2: SUB R4, R1, R5
I3: LW   R6, 0(R4)
I4: AND  R7, R6, R8
I5: OR   R9, R7, R10
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

*(See the table in Question A1 of Section 5.1 for hazard types — refer there for definitions.)*

**Data Forwarding (Bypassing) Technique:**

Data forwarding is a hardware technique that routes the result of an ALU operation (available at the end of EX stage) or a memory load (available at the end of MEM stage) **directly back to the ALU inputs** of the consuming instruction, **without waiting for the result to be written back to the register file**.

**Forwarding paths required:**

- **EX/MEM → EX (ALU input):** Forwards the ALU result from the previous instruction (currently in MEM) directly to the ALU of the current instruction (currently in EX). Resolves **1-cycle RAW** dependencies.
- **MEM/WB → EX (ALU input):** Forwards the value from two instructions ago (currently in WB) to the ALU of the current instruction. Resolves **2-cycle RAW** dependencies when the previous instruction was a Load.

The **Forwarding Unit** is a combinational block in the EX stage that compares the destination register fields of the `EX/MEM` and `MEM/WB` pipeline registers with the source register fields of the instruction in the ID/EX register. It then drives 2-to-1 muxes (Forward A and Forward B) to select the appropriate forwarded value.

*(Diagram: Refer to the Hazard Detection and Forwarding Unit Mermaid block in Section 4.3.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Identifying RAW Hazards**

| Instruction Pair | Producer → Consumer Register | Hazard? | Type |
|------------------|------------------------------|---------|------|
| I1 → I2 | `R1` written by I1, read by I2 | **YES** | RAW (1-cycle gap) |
| I2 → I3 | `R4` written by I2, read by I3 | **YES** | RAW (1-cycle gap) |
| I3 → I4 | `R6` written by I3 (Load), read by I4 | **YES** | RAW (2-cycle gap — Load latency) |
| I4 → I5 | `R7` written by I4, read by I5 | **YES** | RAW (1-cycle gap) |

**Stall Cycle Calculation (No Forwarding):**

**Hazard 1 — I1 → I2:** I1 produces `R1` at the end of its EX stage (cycle 3). I2 needs `R1` at its ID stage (cycle 3). Without forwarding, the earliest `R1` is in the register file is after WB (cycle 5). Therefore, I2 must stall for **2 cycles**.

**Hazard 2 — I2 → I3:** Same logic, **2 stall cycles**.

**Hazard 3 — I3 → I4:** I3 is a Load; result available only at end of MEM (cycle 5). I4 needs it at ID (cycle 5). Without forwarding, earliest availability is after WB of I3 (cycle 6). So I4 must stall for **1 cycle**.

**Hazard 4 — I4 → I5:** I4 is R-type; result at end of EX (cycle 8). I5 needs `R7` at ID (cycle 8). Stall for **2 cycles**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total stall cycles} &= 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 7 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Total cycles} &= (N + 4) + \text{Stalls} = (5 + 4) + 7 = 16 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 1–3 marks per question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+4" drain cycles** when computing total execution time. Many students write $N \cdot 5$ for non-pipelined and $N$ for pipelined, which is incorrect. Always use $N + 4$ for a 5-stage pipeline.
> 2. **Confusing latency with throughput.** Latency of a single instruction is **5T** in a 5-stage pipeline — it does **not** decrease with pipelining. Only throughput improves.
> 3. **Assuming CPI = 1 always.** The ideal CPI is 1, but stalls cause effective CPI to exceed 1. State the assumption clearly.
> 4. **For Load-use hazards, the minimum stall is 1 cycle** (not 2), because forwarding from MEM/WB can supply the value if the load is followed by exactly one independent instruction in between. Always analyze carefully.
> 5. **Branch flushes typically cost 2–3 cycles** in a 5-stage pipeline (flush IF and ID stages, sometimes EX). State the exact stage where the branch resolves.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **5 classical stages** are **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB**.
- ✅ There are exactly **4 pipeline registers**: `IF/ID`, `ID/EX`, `EX/MEM`, `MEM/WB`.
- ✅ **Ideal speedup** of a balanced k-stage pipeline = $k$ (here, $k=5$).
- ✅ **Total cycles for N instructions** (no hazards) = $N + 4$.
- ✅ **Latency per instruction** = $5T$; **throughput** = $1$ instruction per cycle (steady state).
- ✅ **3 hazard types**: Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control (Branch).
- ✅ **Data forwarding** reduces RAW stalls to **0** for R-type dependencies, but **1 stall** still required for **Load-use** pairs.
- ✅ **Branch misprediction penalty** in a 5-stage pipeline = **2 cycles** (flush IF and ID).
- ✅ ARM Cortex-M0 (2-stage), M3 (3-stage), M7 (6-stage) are real-world embedded examples.
- ✅ Pipeline **efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{N}{N + k - 1}$ approaches 1 as $N \to \infty$.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, always **state assumptions** (e.g., "assume all stages take 1 cycle, ignore hazards" unless given).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Sets - ARM Instruction Set - Thumb Instruction Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Sets - ARM Instruction Set - Thumb Instruction Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The ARM Instruction Set

### Formal Definition
The **ARM Instruction Set** is a **32-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** instruction set architecture developed by Arm Holdings. It is a **Load/Store architecture**, meaning data-processing operations operate only on registers, while memory is accessed exclusively through dedicated load and store instructions. The instruction set is characterized by a **uniform 32-bit fixed-length instruction format**, a large register file of **37 visible 32-bit registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers), and a powerful hardware feature called the **barrel shifter** that allows shift operations to be performed on operands *for free* (in a single cycle) as part of any data-processing instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme for PEEBT415, students are expected to identify the fields of the 32-bit ARM instruction word (Condition, Opcode, Operands, etc.), classify instruction types, and explain why ARM qualifies as a RISC Load-Store architecture.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Swiss Army Knife Workshop
Imagine a large industrial workshop (the **CPU**). The **ARM Instruction Set** is like giving every worker a *single, sturdy, full-sized 32-bit tool* for every job. Each tool (instruction) is always the same size, so the workers can grab and use any tool instantly without searching. Furthermore, each tool is so well-designed that the worker can simultaneously shift a material (the **barrel shifter**) while cutting it, in a single motion. There are no "leftover" half-tools; every instruction does a complete, atomic job. This uniformity is what makes the ARM pipeline so fast and predictable.

## 1.2 The Thumb Instruction Set

### Formal Definition
The **Thumb Instruction Set** is a **16-bit compressed (re-encoded) subset of the ARM instruction set**. It provides the same 32-bit ARM processing performance but with **improved code density** — typically a **30% reduction in code size** compared to standard ARM code. Thumb instructions are decompressed by the processor core into full 32-bit ARM instructions *on-the-fly* before being executed by the pipeline.
