## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for embedded systems. List any four major components of an embedded IDE.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** for embedded systems is a unified software application that combines all tools required to develop, build, debug, and deploy firmware on a target microcontroller, in a single graphical interface running on a host computer.

**Four major components:**
1. **Source Code Editor** – with syntax highlighting for C, C++, and Assembly. (0.75)
2. **Cross-Compiler and Cross-Assembler** – translates host-written code to target architecture machine code. (0.75)
3. **Linker and Locator** – combines object files and places them in target memory map using a scatter file. (0.75)
4. **Debugger with JTAG/SWD Interface** – provides breakpoints, watch variables, and register inspection on the running target. (0.75)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a cross-compiler and a native compiler with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Native Compiler | Cross-Compiler |
|---|---|---|
| Host = Target | Yes | No |
| Output runs on | Same machine as compiler | Different architecture |
| Example | `gcc` on Linux x86 → x86 binary | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` on x86 → ARM binary |
| Use case | Desktop apps, scripting | Embedded firmware, mobile, IoT |

**Example (Native):** Compiling a program on an Ubuntu laptop using `gcc` produces an executable that runs on the same Ubuntu laptop. (1)

**Example (Cross):** Using `arm-none-eabi-gcc` on a Windows PC to compile C code for STM32F407 (ARM Cortex-M4) produces a `.hex` file that runs on the STM32 board. (2)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Build Process & Toolchain*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete build process of embedded firmware from C source to flashable hex file, listing the tools used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The embedded firmware build process transforms human-readable C source code into a flashable binary image via **six sequential stages**:

**Stage 1: Source Code Authoring**
- Developer writes `main.c` and header files in the IDE editor (e.g., Keil µVision).
- Output: `.c`, `.h`, `.s` source files.

**Stage 2: Preprocessing**
- Tool: **Preprocessor** (`arm-none-eabi-cpp` for ARM, or built-in).
- Handles `#include`, `#define`, `#ifdef`.
- Output: `.i` file (pure C with macros expanded and headers inlined).

**Stage 3: Compilation (C → Assembly)**
- Tool: **Cross-Compiler** (`arm-none-eabi-gcc`).
- Performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, optimization.
- Output: `.s` file (target-specific assembly).

**Stage 4: Assembly → Object Code**
- Tool: **Cross-Assembler** (`arm-none-eabi-as`).
- Translates mnemonics to opcodes.
- Output: `.o` file (relocatable ELF object).

**Stage 5: Linking**
- Tool: **Linker** (`arm-none-eabi-ld` / `armlink`).
- Combines `main.o`, `startup.o`, `system.o`, and libraries.
- Resolves external symbols.
- Output: `.axf` / `.elf` relocatable executable.

**Stage 6: Locating & Image Generation**
- Tool: **Locator** + **FromELF** utility.
- Uses linker script (`.sct` or `.ld`) to assign physical addresses.
- Output: `.hex` (Intel HEX), `.bin` (raw binary).

```
main.c → cpp → main.i → gcc → main.s → as → main.o
                                                   ↓
                                  ld + .sct → project.axf
                                                   ↓
                                      FromELF → .hex, .bin
```

**(b)** Explain the role of the **linker script** in embedded systems. Discuss what happens if a linker script is missing or incorrect. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **linker script** (also called a **scatter file** in Keil, with extensions `.ld` for GNU, `.sct` for Keil, `.icf` for IAR) is a text file that instructs the linker/locator about the **target's memory map** — the regions of Flash, RAM, and special-function registers and their physical addresses.

**Key Roles:**
1. **Memory Region Definition:** Declares `MEMORY { FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x00000000, LENGTH = 512K ; RAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x10000000, LENGTH = 64K ; }`.

2. **Section Placement:** Specifies which output sections (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`) go into which region.

3. **Symbol Provision:** Provides `__StackTop`, `_estack`, `_sbss`, `_ebss` for startup code.

4. **Vector Table Location:** Ensures the ARM Cortex-M vector table is at address `0x00000000`.

5. **Stack and Heap Sizing:** Defines the RAM region size, indirectly controlling `Stack_Size` and `Heap_Size` macros.

**Consequences of Missing Linker Script:**
- The linker fails with error: `undefined reference to '__scatterload'` or `section `.text' will not fit in region`.
- No executable is produced.

**Consequences of Incorrect Linker Script:**
1. **Code placed at wrong address:** Firmware runs incorrectly or vector table misfires → HardFault on reset.
2. **Overlapping sections:** `.data` overlapping `.bss` → memory corruption at runtime.
3. **Stack overflow into heap:** Unpredictable crashes due to RAM region mis-sizing.
4. **Bootloader conflict:** Application written over bootloader address → bricking.
5. **External memory not used:** SDRAM unused → system operates in crippled mode.

**Example Linker Script Fragment (ARM GCC):**

```c
MEMORY
{
  FLASH (rx)  : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 256K
  RAM (rwx)   : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 64K
}
SECTIONS
{
  .isr_vector : { KEEP(*(.isr_vector)) } >FLASH
  .text       : { *(.text*) *(.rodata*) } >FLASH
  .data       : AT(LOADADDR(.text) + SIZEOF(.text))
                { *(.data*) } >RAM
  .bss        : { *(.bss*) *(COMMON) } >RAM
}
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Debugging & On-Chip Debug*

**(a)** Compare **JTAG** and **SWD** debug interfaces in detail. State at least four points of comparison. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (ARM) |
|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Joint Test Action Group | Serial Wire Debug |
| Pin Count | 4 mandatory + 1 optional (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, nTRST) | 2 wires (SWDIO, SWCLK) |
| Standard | IEEE 1149.1 (universal) | ARM proprietary |
| Max Data Rate | Up to 50 MHz TCK | Up to 50 Mbps |
| Daisy Chain | Yes (boundary scan across multiple ICs) | No (point-to-point) |
| Pin Reuse | Dedicated JTAG pins | Can share pins with GPIO via remap |
| Architecture Support | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, x86, DSP | ARM Cortex-M only |
| Multi-drop | Possible (star configuration limited) | Yes (multi-drop on SWDIO line) |
| Use Case | Production boundary scan + debug | Embedded MCU debug only |

**Explanation (Key Points):**

1. **JTAG** was originally designed for **boundary scan testing** of PCBs (testing inter-chip connections without physical probing). It uses a 4-wire TAP (Test Access Port). It supports boundary scan of any IC, making it industry-universal.

2. **SWD** was developed by **ARM** as a 2-wire alternative specifically for **Cortex-M debug access port (DAP)**. It is faster, uses fewer pins, and leaves more GPIO available for application use.

3. **JTAG supports daisy-chaining** — multiple devices on one scan chain, useful for testing complex boards. **SWD is point-to-point** but supports multi-drop where multiple targets can share the SWDIO line.

4. **JTAG allows boundary scan** of non-ARM devices (FPGAs, CPLDs, ASICs). **SWD is ARM-only** and cannot be used for boundary scan of heterogeneous boards.

**(b)** Describe the working of an **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** and an **On-Chip Debugger (OCD)**. State two advantages of OCD over ICE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**In-Circuit Emulator (ICE):**
An ICE is a hardware device that **physically replaces the target MCU** in the circuit via a special probe pod. The probe contains a special bond-out version of the MCU that exposes internal buses, allowing the developer to observe register states, execution flow, and memory in real time with zero overhead.

**Working Principle:**
1. The user removes the target MCU from its socket.
2. The ICE probe (with a bond-out chip) is plugged into the socket.
3. The ICE provides emulation memory, breakpoints, trace buffers.
4. The IDE debugger communicates with the ICE via parallel/USB.
5. Execution traces, memory dumps, and register snapshots are visible in real time.

**Drawbacks of ICE:** Expensive, bulky, requires socketed MCU, supports limited families.

**On-Chip Debugger (OCD):**
An OCD is **built into the MCU silicon itself** as a dedicated hardware block (e.g., ARM **DAP**, Microchip **ICD**, TI **JTAG TAP**). It uses a few pins of the MCU to expose debug functionality to the outside world via JTAG/SWD.

**Working Principle:**
1. The MCU has built-in hardware breakpoints, watchpoints, and single-step logic.
2. The host debugger (via GDB) sends commands over JTAG/SWD.
3. The DAP module on-chip intercepts CPU fetch/decode and halts execution at breakpoints.
4. CPU registers and memory are read/written via the debug bus.
5. Trace data is captured via **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell)** or **SWO (Serial Wire Output)**.

**Two Advantages of OCD over ICE:**
1. **Cost-effective** — no special bond-out chip or socket; uses standard production MCU. OCD-ready MCUs are pin-compatible with non-debug variants. (3.5)
2. **Field-upgradable** — same debugger works for in-production firmware updates and post-deployment diagnostics, because the debug port is always available. (3.5)

Additional advantages: smaller probe size (e.g., $5 ST-Link V2 vs. $5000 ICE), supports modern surface-mount packages (no socket needed).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse "Cross-Compiler" with "Compiler".** A cross-compiler produces code for a *different* architecture. Students who use a desktop `gcc` as the example for cross-compilation will lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Linker Script is NOT optional.** Writing "linker decides memory" without naming the **scatter file** or **linker script** loses **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **JTAG vs SWD confusion:** SWD is **ARM-specific**, not universal. Stating "SWD is also an industry standard like JTAG" is factually wrong and loses marks.
> 4. **Object File vs Executable confusion:** A `.o` is relocatable; a `.elf`/`.axf` is the final executable. Mixing these up loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Listing "IDE" as a single tool** (not recognizing Editor + Compiler + Debugger as subcomponents) costs the "list four components" portion in Q1.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Embedded System Development Environment & IDEs**

- ✅ **IDE = Editor + Cross-Compiler + Assembler + Linker + Locator + Debugger + Flash Programmer**, integrated in one GUI.
- ✅ **Cross-toolchain triplet naming:** `arm-none-eabi` means ARM target, no vendor, EABI calling convention. Examples: `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `avr-gcc`, `msp430-elf-gcc`, `riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc`.
- ✅ **Build pipeline:** `.c` → CPP → `.i` → Cross-Compiler → `.s` → Assembler → `.o` → Linker → `.axf/.elf` → FromELF → `.hex` / `.bin`.
- ✅ **Linker Script / Scatter file** maps sections to physical memory (Flash, RAM, peripherals); missing it = build failure.
- ✅ **JTAG** = 4–5 wire, IEEE 1149.1, universal, supports daisy-chain boundary scan.
- ✅ **SWD** = 2 wire (SWDIO + SWCLK), ARM Cortex-M only, faster, leaves more GPIO.
- ✅ **ICE** = external bond-out probe, replaces target MCU; rare today due to OCD dominance.
- ✅ **OCD (On-Chip Debug)** = built into MCU silicon (DAP, ETM, SWO); modern, cheap, ubiquitous.
- ✅ **Top embedded IDEs:** Keil µVision (ARM7/Cortex), IAR EW (ARM/MSP430), MPLAB X (PIC), STM32CubeIDE (STM32, Eclipse-based, free), CCS (TI), Arduino IDE (AVR/ARM, beginner), PlatformIO (multi-arch on VS Code), ESP-IDF (ESP32).
- ✅ **File formats:** `.elf` (debug, with symbols), `.hex` (Intel HEX for flash), `.bin` (raw binary), `.axf` (ARM Executable Format).
- ✅ **Memory footprint formulas** — Flash % = used/total × 100; RAM % = (heap+stack+data)/total × 100.
- ✅ **Build time equation** — sum of per-file compile time + link time + locate time.
- ✅ **Why cross-compilation?** Embedded targets lack RAM, OS, and IO to host a compiler — host (x86 PC) builds for target (ARM/AVR/PIC).
- ✅ **Vendor IDE vs Open-source:** Vendor IDEs (Keil, IAR) are tightly integrated, certified, and costly; open-source (STM32CubeIDE, PlatformIO) are free and flexible.
- ✅ **KTU lab focus:** Keil µVision for LPC2148/LPC1768, Arduino IDE for AVR, STM32CubeIDE for STM32 — expect questions on Keil's build process and JTAG/SWD on these targets.
- ✅ **OpenOCD + GDB** is the standard open-source debug backend for ARM Cortex-M.
- ✅ **JTAG TAP states:** Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Shift-DR, Shift-IR, Pause-DR, Pause-IR — total **16 states** in the state machine.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Compilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cross Compilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cross Compiler** is a software utility capable of generating executable machine code for a target platform whose architecture is **different** from the architecture of the host machine on which the compiler itself executes. In the context of embedded system design, the host is typically a general-purpose workstation (x86/x64) running an operating system like Windows, Linux, or macOS, while the target is a resource-constrained embedded processor (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC, MSP430, RISC-V).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *"A cross compiler is a compiler that runs on one machine (host) and produces object code for another machine (target). It is an essential part of the embedded software toolchain because most embedded targets lack the memory, processing power, and operating system support needed to host a native compiler."*

The complete cross-development environment consists of:
- **Cross Compiler** → translates high-level source to target assembly
- **Cross Assembler** → converts assembly to relocatable object code
- **Cross Linker** → resolves symbols and links multiple object files
- **Locator / Map Generator** → assigns physical memory addresses
- **Cross Debugger** → remote debugging protocol (e.g., GDB + OpenOCD/JTAG)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are an English-speaking diplomat (the **HOST**) who must prepare a speech in Japanese (the **TARGET LANGUAGE**) for a foreign dignitary who will deliver it. You cannot teach the dignitary to read English (the target cannot run a full compiler), and the dignitary cannot read English (the target CPU cannot interpret x86 instructions). You, the diplomat, must study Japanese (the cross compiler) and produce a perfectly translated, culturally adapted speech in advance.

Key parallels:
- You (Diplomat) = Host x86 PC
- Japanese = Target ARM instruction set
- Speech in Japanese = Cross-compiled binary (`.elf`/`.hex`/`.bin`)
- Dignitary = Embedded microcontroller
- The translated document = Firmware flashed into the target

## 1.3 Why Cross Compilers Are Mandatory in Embedded Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Constraint of Embedded Targets:**
> Most microcontrollers (e.g., STM32, ATmega328P) have **flash memory ranging from 32 KB to 2 MB** and **RAM from 4 KB to 256 KB**. A modern GCC native compiler requires **>500 MB of disk space and 1 GB+ of RAM** to operate. It is physically impossible to host a compiler ON the target.

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Resource Constraints | Target lacks RAM, ROM, and OS to host a compiler |
| Cost & Form Factor | Embedded devices must be cheap, small, and low-power |
| Specialized ISA | Target uses ISAs (ARM, RISC-V, AVR) different from x86 host |
| Determinism | Pre-compiled binaries guarantee real-time behavior |
| IP Protection | Source code never leaves the secure host machine |

## 1.4 Host vs. Target — The Cross-Development Model

| Parameter | Host Machine | Target Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Role | Runs the cross compiler | Runs the compiled firmware |
| CPU | x86 / x86-64 / Apple Silicon | ARM / AVR / PIC / RISC-V / MSP430 |
| OS | Windows 10/11, Ubuntu, macOS | Often None (Bare-metal), RTOS, or Linux |
| Resources | GBs of RAM, TBs of disk | KBs of RAM, KBs-MBs of flash |
| Compiler | Native GCC / Clang | Cannot host any compiler |
| Output | Cross-compiled `.elf`/`.hex`/`.bin` | Firmware executed in real-time |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Phases of Cross Compilation (The Build Pipeline)

The cross compilation process is a **multi-stage pipeline** where each stage transforms the source code progressively into a target-executable binary. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU exam questions on toolchain design.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

1. **Preprocessing (`cpp`)**
   - Handles directives starting with `#`
   - Performs macro expansion, file inclusion (`#include`), conditional compilation (`#ifdef`)
   - Output: Pure C/C++ source file with no preprocessor directives
   - Mathematical representation: $S_{preprocessed} = \text{cpp}(S_{source})$

2. **Compilation (`cc1` / `cross-gcc`)**
   - Translates preprocessed source into **target-specific assembly language**
   - Performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, optimization
   - Output: Assembly file (`.s`) for the target ISA
   - $A_{target} = \text{compile}(S_{preprocessed}, \text{target\_ISA})$

3. **Assembly (`as` / `cross-as`)**
   - Converts assembly mnemonics into **relocatable object code**
   - Output: Object file (`.o`) containing machine code + symbol table + relocation entries
   - $O_{relocatable} = \text{assemble}(A_{target})$

4. **Linking (`ld` / `cross-ld`)**
   - Combines multiple `.o` files and libraries into a single executable
   - Resolves external symbols, performs address resolution
   - Output: Relocatable executable (`.elf`)

5. **Locating / Address Fixing**
   - Maps logical addresses to physical memory addresses (Flash: `0x08000000`, RAM: `0x20000000` for ARM Cortex-M)
   - Generates Intel HEX (`.hex`) or raw binary (`.bin`) for the flash programmer

6. **Loading onto Target**
   - The `.hex`/`.bin` file is flashed via JTAG, SWD, UART bootloader, or USB

## 2.2 The Cross-Compiler Toolchain (Anatomy)

A complete cross-toolchain consists of **binutils**, **GCC**, **C library (newlib/picolibc/glibc)**, and **GDB**.

| Component | Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| C/C++ Compiler | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` | Translates C/C++ to target assembly |
| Assembler | `arm-none-eabi-as` | Assembly → relocatable object code |
| Linker | `arm-none-eabi-ld` | Links objects, produces ELF |
| Object Copy | `arm-none-eabi-objcopy` | Converts ELF to HEX/BIN |
| Object Dump | `arm-none-eabi-objdump` | Disassembles for analysis |
| Size Tool | `arm-none-eabi-size` | Reports section sizes |
| C Library | `newlib` / `picolibc` | Provides `printf`, `malloc`, etc. |
| Debugger | `arm-none-eabi-gdb` | Remote source-level debugging |
| Flash Programmer | `OpenOCD` / `st-link` | Writes firmware to target flash |

> [!NOTE]
> **The naming convention `arm-none-eabi-gcc` decodes as:**
> `arm` (target architecture) – `none` (no OS vendor) – `eabi` (Embedded Application Binary Interface) – `gcc` (GNU Compiler Collection). This triple is a KTU-favorite exam question.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Tables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Direct questions on cross compiler architecture, native vs cross compilation, and toolchain components carry **3 to 7 marks** in Part A and Part B respectively.

### Table 2.1: Native Compiler vs Cross Compiler

| Parameter | Native Compiler | Cross Compiler |
|---|---|---|
| Host = Target | Yes | No |
| Execution Platform | Same as target | Different from target |
| Use Case | Desktop/Server apps | Embedded systems, OS kernels |
| Example | `gcc` on x86 for x86 | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` on x86 for ARM |
| Output Format | Native executable | Cross-platform binary |
| Debugger | Local GDB | Remote GDB + JTAG/SWD |

### Table 2.2: Memory Mapping for Cross-Compiled ARM Cortex-M Binary

| Memory Region | Start Address (typical) | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash (Code) | `0x08000000` | 256 KB – 2 MB | `.text`, `.rodata` |
| SRAM | `0x20000000` | 32 KB – 256 KB | `.data`, `.bss`, heap, stack |
| Peripheral Registers | `0x40000000` | 512 MB | Memory-mapped I/O |
| System Memory | `0x1FFF0000` | 30 KB | Bootloader ROM |

### Table 2.3: Cross-Compilation Flags (GCC)

| Flag | Function |
|---|---|
| `-mcpu=cortex-m4` | Selects target CPU core |
| `-mthumb` | Generates Thumb-2 instructions (ARM) |
| `-march=armv7-m` | Target architecture version |
| `-mfloat-abi=hard` | Hardware FPU ABI |
| `-mfpu=fpv4-sp-d16` | Specifies FPU type |
| `-specs=nosys.specs` | Provides stub for newlib syscalls |
| `-T linker_script.ld` | Custom memory layout script |
| `-Wall -Wextra` | Enable warnings |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Bosch, Continental use cross compilers to build AUTOSAR-compliant firmware for PowerPC and ARM Cortex-R.
- **IoT Devices:** Espressif ESP32 toolchain (`xtensa-esp32-elf-gcc`) cross-compiles Wi-Fi/BLE stacks.
- **Aerospace:** NASA flight software uses cross-compiled binaries for radiation-hardened RAD750 processors.
- **Mobile Phones:** Android NDK cross-compiles C/C++ for ARM-based Snapdragon/Exynos SoCs.
- **Robotics:** ROS2 nodes cross-compiled for NVIDIA Jetson (aarch64) from x86 host.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Footprint Calculation of a Cross-Compiled Binary

> **Problem Statement:** A cross-compiled firmware for STM32F407 (ARM Cortex-M4) has the following section sizes:
> - `.text` (code in flash) = 38,920 bytes
> - `.rodata` (constants) = 4,512 bytes
> - `.data` (initialized variables) = 2,048 bytes
> - `.bss` (zero-initialized variables) = 6,144 bytes
>
> **Find:** (a) Total Flash usage, (b) Total SRAM usage at runtime.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify what gets stored where.**

In a Harvard architecture (ARM Cortex-M), **flash and RAM are physically separate**. The linker script maps sections to memory regions as follows:

$$
\text{Flash} \leftarrow \{.text, .rodata, .data_{init\_image}\}
$$

$$
\text{SRAM} \leftarrow \{.data_{runtime}, .bss, \text{heap}, \text{stack}\}
$$

The `.data` section is **special**: its **initial values** are stored in flash (to be copied to RAM on startup by the **C Runtime Init** code), while the **runtime copy** lives in RAM.

**Step 2: Compute Flash Footprint.**

$$
\text{Flash} = \text{Size}(.text) + \text{Size}(.rodata) + \text{Size}(.data_{init})
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Flash} = 38920 + 4512 + 2048
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Flash} = 45{,}480 \text{ bytes} \approx 44.41 \text{ KB}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute SRAM Footprint.**

The `.data` section's runtime copy occupies SRAM (equal in size to its initial image). The `.bss` is zero-initialized at runtime by the startup code.

$$
\text{SRAM} = \text{Size}(.data_{runtime}) + \text{Size}(.bss)
$$

$$
\text{SRAM} = 2048 + 6144
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SRAM} = 8{,}192 \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ KB}}
$$

**Step 4: Validate against target hardware.**

STM32F407 has **1 MB flash** and **192 KB SRAM**. Utilization:

$$
\text{Flash Utilization} = \frac{45480}{1048576} \times 100\% \approx 4.34\%
$$

$$
\text{SRAM Utilization} = \frac{8192}{196608} \times 100\% \approx 4.17\%
$$

> Both are within safe limits, leaving **95.66% flash** and **95.83% SRAM** for future expansion, heap, stack, and RTOS kernels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Endianness Conversion in Cross-Compilation

> **Problem Statement:** A 32-bit integer `0x12345678` is stored in a **little-endian** host (x86) and must be transmitted over a network to a **big-endian** target (legacy PowerPC MCU). Derive the byte-swap pattern.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write the little-endian storage pattern (host).**

In little-endian, the **least significant byte (LSB) is stored first** at the lowest address:

$$
\text{Memory (LE)}: \quad [0x78], [0x56], [0x34], [0x12]
$$

**Step 2: Express this mathematically using byte indexing.**

For a 32-bit word $W$ stored at address $A$, little-endian means:

$$
\text{Memory}[A + i] = (W \gg (8 \cdot i)) \ \& \ 0xFF, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, 3
$$

Applying this to $W = 0x12345678$:

$$
\text{Memory}[A+0] = 0x12345678 \ \& \ 0xFF = 0x78
$$

$$
\text{Memory}[A+1] = (0x12345678 \gg 8) \ \& \ 0xFF = 0x56
$$

$$
\text{Memory}[A+2] = (0x12345678 \gg 16) \ \& \ 0xFF = 0x34
$$

$$
\text{Memory}[A+3] = (0x12345678 \gg 24) \ \& \ 0xFF = 0x12
$$

**Step 3: Big-endian target requires reversed byte order.**

Big-endian expects the **most significant byte (MSB) first**:

$$
\text{Memory (BE, target)}: \quad [0x12], [0x34], [0x56], [0x78]
$$

**Step 4: Define the byte-swap transformation.**

$$
W_{swapped} = ((W \ \& \ 0x000000FF) \ll 24) \ \vert \ ((W \ \& \ 0x0000FF00) \ll 8) \ \vert \ ((W \ \& \ 0x00FF0000) \gg 8) \ \vert \ ((W \ \& \ 0xFF000000) \gg 24)
$$

Substituting $W = 0x12345678$:

$$
W_{swapped} = (0x78 \ll 24) \ \vert \ (0x56 \ll 8) \ \vert \ (0x34 \gg 8) \ \vert \ (0x12 \gg 24)
$$

$$
W_{swapped} = 0x78000000 \ \vert \ 0x00005600 \ \vert \ 0x00000034 \ \vert \ 0x00000000
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{swapped} = 0x78563412}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Functions like `htonl()`, `ntohl()`, `htons()` in `<arpa/inet.h>` perform this exact conversion. In cross-compiled embedded network stacks (e.g., lwIP), this is **manually optimized** using architecture-specific assembly instructions like `REV` (ARM) or `BSWAP` (x86).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cross-Compilation Decision Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix maps the **"Why"** behind each cross-compilation decision. This framework frequently appears in KTU 14-mark essay questions.

| Design Decision | Constraint | Cross-Compilation Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Bare-metal vs RTOS | No OS support on target | Use `arm-none-eabi-gcc` with `newlib-nano` |
| Flash size < 64 KB | Limited code storage | Enable `-Os` (optimize for size) |
| RAM < 16 KB | No dynamic allocation | Avoid `malloc`, use static allocation |
| Hard real-time | Deterministic execution | Disable optimization `-O0` for worst-case analysis |
| Multiple source files | Modular firmware | Use linker script with section placement |
| Mixed C/ASM | Critical inner loops | Inline assembly with target-specific mnemonics |
| Remote debugging | No display on target | GDB + OpenOCD + JTAG/SWD |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Cross-Compilation Toolchain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HOST X86 PC] --> B[Source Files dot C]
    B --> C[Preprocessor CPP]
    C --> D[Cross Compiler GCC Target ISA]
    D --> E[Cross Assembler AS]
    E --> F[Relocatable Object dot O]
    F --> G[Cross Linker LD]
    H[Linker Script dot LD] --> G
    G --> I[ELF Executable]
    I --> J[OBJCOPY]
    J --> K[HEX or BIN File]
    K --> L[Flash Programmer OpenOCD]
    L --> M[Target MCU ARM Cortex M]
    M --> N[Remote GDB Debugger]
    G --> N
    I --> N
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow moves from left (host development) to right (target execution). Note the **dual path to GDB**: the debugger connects both to the ELF file (for symbol resolution) and to the live target (for runtime state inspection) via JTAG/SWD.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Host vs. Target Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST_ENV["HOST ENVIRONMENT X86 PC"]
        H1[Windows or Linux OS]
        H2[Native Editor VS Code]
        H3[Cross Compiler arm-none-eabi-gcc]
        H4[Host Filesystem]
        H5[Cross Debugger gdb-multiarch]
        H1 --> H2
        H2 --> H3
        H3 --> H4
        H4 --> H5
    end

    subgraph COMM_CHANNEL["COMMUNICATION CHANNEL"]
        C1[JTAG or SWD Cable]
        C2[USB or Serial]
    end

    subgraph TARGET_ENV["TARGET ENVIRONMENT MCU"]
        T1[ARM Cortex M Core]
        T2[Flash Memory 0x08000000]
        T3[SRAM 0x20000000]
        T4[Peripherals GPIO ADC UART]
        T1 --> T2
        T1 --> T3
        T1 --> T4
    end

    HOST_ENV --> COMM_CHANNEL
    COMM_CHANNEL --> TARGET_ENV
```

> **Key Observation:** The host and target are **physically isolated** and communicate only through the narrow communication channel (JTAG/SWD/UART). All compilation, linking, and symbol resolution happens on the host.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Stages of Cross-Compilation with Output File Extensions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[main dot c] -->|cpp| B[main dot i]
    B -->|cc1| C[main dot s Assembly]
    C -->|as| D[main dot o Object]
    D -->|ld| E[firmware dot elf]
    E -->|objcopy| F[firmware dot hex]
    E -->|objcopy| G[firmware dot bin]
    F -->|st-link| H[Target Flash]
    G -->|st-link| H
    E -->|gdb and openocd| I[Live Debug Session]
    H --> I
```

> **File Type Glossary (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> - `.c` → C source
> - `.i` → Preprocessed C
> - `.s` → Target assembly
> - `.o` → Relocatable object
> - `.elf` → Executable and Linkable Format
> - `.hex` → Intel HEX (ASCII, for flashers)
> - `.bin` → Raw binary (compact)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question 1: Define a cross compiler. Why is it essential in embedded system development?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **cross compiler** is a compiler that executes on a host machine (typically x86 PC) but produces executable code for a target machine with a different architecture (e.g., ARM, AVR, RISC-V) [1 mark].

It is essential because:
- Embedded targets have **limited memory (KBs)** and **processing power**, insufficient to host a native compiler [1 mark].
- The target CPU's **instruction set differs** from the host, so a native compiler cannot generate correct machine code for it [1 mark].

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question 2: Differentiate between a native compiler and a cross compiler. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Native Compiler | Cross Compiler |
|---|---|---|
| Host = Target | Yes | No |
| Example | `gcc` on x86 producing x86 binary | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` on x86 producing ARM binary |
| Use Case | Desktop applications | Embedded firmware |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for the distinguishing table)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a cross compiler toolchain with a neat block diagram. List any four components of a typical GNU ARM cross-toolchain and state their functions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A cross-compiled firmware for STM32F103C8T6 (Cortex-M3, 64 KB Flash, 20 KB SRAM) has the following section sizes: `.text` = 28,672 bytes, `.rodata` = 2,048 bytes, `.data` = 1,024 bytes, `.bss` = 3,072 bytes. Calculate the total Flash and SRAM usage, and comment on whether the binary will fit. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **cross compiler toolchain** is a set of software utilities that collectively transform high-level source code into a target-executable binary. The architecture follows a pipeline model.

**Block Diagram:**
```
Source (.c) → Preprocessor → Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Locator → Target Flash
```

**Four Components of GNU ARM Cross-Toolchain:**

1. **`arm-none-eabi-gcc` (Cross Compiler):** Translates preprocessed C/C++ code into ARM/Thumb assembly instructions. The `-mcpu=cortex-m3` flag selects the target core. [2 marks]

2. **`arm-none-eabi-as` (Cross Assembler):** Converts assembly mnemonics into relocatable machine code object files (`.o`). Handles target-specific instruction encoding. [2 marks]

3. **`arm-none-eabi-ld` (Cross Linker):** Links multiple object files and libraries, resolves external symbols, applies the **linker script** (`*.ld`) to map sections to physical memory regions, and produces an `.elf` executable. [1.5 marks]

4. **`arm-none-eabi-gdb` (Cross Debugger):** Performs **remote source-level debugging** by communicating with a GDB server (e.g., OpenOCD) on the host, which in turn talks to the target via JTAG/SWD. [1.5 marks]

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Calculate Flash Usage** (Flash stores `.text`, `.rodata`, and the **initialization image** of `.data`):

$$
\text{Flash} = 28672 + 2048 + 1024 = 31{,}744 \text{ bytes} \approx 31.0 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate SRAM Usage** (SRAM stores the runtime copy of `.data` and `.bss`):

$$
\text{SRAM} = 1024 + 3072 = 4{,}096 \text{ bytes} = 4.0 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3: Check against hardware limits.**

| Resource | Used | Available | Utilization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash | 31,744 B (31 KB) | 65,536 B (64 KB) | **48.4%** ✅ Fits |
| SRAM | 4,096 B (4 KB) | 20,480 B (20 KB) | **20.0%** ✅ Fits |

**Comment:** The binary comfortably fits. However, the remaining **32.4 KB of flash** must accommodate future code additions, and the remaining **16 KB of SRAM** must reserve space for the **stack** (typically 1–4 KB), **heap** (if `malloc` is used), and any **RTOS kernel** (FreeRTOS needs ~5–10 KB). [1 mark for comment]

---

### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the various stages involved in the cross compilation process. Mention the output file extension at each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the challenges of cross compilation in embedded systems, focusing on **endianness**, **word alignment**, and **calling conventions**. Provide a suitable example for each. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The cross compilation process is divided into **six sequential stages**, each producing a specific output file:

1. **Preprocessing** (`cpp`): Handles `#include`, `#define`, `#ifdef`. Output: `.i` (preprocessed source) [1 mark]
2. **Compilation** (`cc1`): Translates to target assembly. Output: `.s` [1 mark]
3. **Assembly** (`as`): Generates relocatable object code. Output: `.o` [1 mark]
4. **Linking** (`ld`): Combines objects, resolves symbols, applies linker script. Output: `.elf` [1 mark]
5. **Object Copy** (`objcopy`): Strips ELF metadata, produces firmware image. Output: `.hex` or `.bin` [1.5 marks]
6. **Loading**: Flashes binary to target via JTAG/SWD/bootloader. [1.5 marks]

**Block Diagram:**
```
main.c → [cpp] → main.i → [cc1] → main.s → [as] → main.o → [ld] → firmware.elf → [objcopy] → firmware.hex → [st-link] → Target Flash
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Endianness Challenge [2.5 Marks]:**
- **Definition:** Endianness is the byte-ordering convention for multi-byte data.
- **Example:** A 32-bit value `0x12345678` stored in little-endian (x86 host) appears as `[78][56][34][12]` in memory, but must be reversed to `[12][34][56][78]` for a big-endian target (legacy PowerPC MCU).
- **Solution:** Use byte-swap functions like `htonl()`, `ntohl()`, or compiler built-ins like `__builtin_bswap32()`.

**2. Word Alignment Challenge [2.5 Marks]:**
- **Definition:** Word alignment requires multi-byte data to be placed at addresses divisible by their size.
- **Example:** A 4-byte `int` must be placed at an address divisible by 4. Misaligned access on ARM Cortex-M3 causes a **HardFault** exception.
- **Solution:** Use packed structures with caution (`__attribute__((packed))`), or perform manual byte-by-byte read/write with assembly `LDRB`/`STRB` instructions.

**3. Calling Convention Challenge [2 Marks]:**
- **Definition:** Calling conventions define how arguments are passed and return values are received.
- **Example:** ARM AAPCS specifies that the first 4 integer arguments are passed in `R0–R3`, with the return value in `R0`. x86 64-bit uses `RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9`.
- **Implication:** A function compiled with one ABI cannot be linked with code using a different ABI without thunking functions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "cross compiler" with "cross assembler" or "cross linker" — they are distinct components of the toolchain.
> 2. **Do NOT say** the cross compiler runs on the target — it ALWAYS runs on the host.
> 3. **Do NOT omit** the linker script role in address mapping — it is worth 2 marks in Part B.
> 4. **Do NOT assume** the binary can execute on the host — it CANNOT, due to ISA mismatch.
> 5. **Always mention** the naming convention `arch-vendor-os-abi` (e.g., `arm-none-eabi`) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- A **cross compiler** runs on the **host** but generates code for the **target**.
- The toolchain = `binutils` (as, ld, objcopy, objdump, size) + `gcc` + `newlib/picolibc` + `gdb`.
- Standard naming: `arm-none-eabi-gcc` → ARM, No OS, EABI, GCC.
- **Six stages:** Preprocess → Compile → Assemble → Link → Locate → Load.
- File extensions: `.c` → `.i` → `.s` → `.o` → `.elf` → `.hex`/`.bin` → Flash.
- **Flash stores:** `.text` + `.rodata` + `.data` initialization image.
- **SRAM stores:** `.data` runtime + `.bss` + heap + stack.
- **Big-endian vs Little-endian:** MSB-first vs LSB-first byte storage.
- **ARM AAPCS** calling convention: args in R0–R3, return in R0.
- **Cross debugging** uses GDB + OpenOCD + JTAG/SWD; it is *remote* and *symbol-aware*.
- **Linker script** (`.ld`) is the file that maps logical sections to physical memory addresses.
- A native compiler has host = target; a cross compiler has host ≠ target.
- Embedded targets CANNOT host a compiler due to memory and OS constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disassemblers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Disassemblers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **disassembler** is a software utility (or firmware/hardware component) that translates **machine code (binary executable object code)** — the lowest-level representation of a program stored in memory — back into its equivalent **human-readable assembly language mnemonics**. Formally, it is the mathematical inverse of an *assembler*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Mapping:**
> If an assembler defines a bijective mapping $f: \mathcal{A} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}$ from assembly instructions $\mathcal{A}$ to machine code $\mathcal{M}$, then a disassembler computes the inverse $f^{-1}: \mathcal{M} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$. In practice, this inverse is **non-unique** because the same byte sequence may decode into different instructions depending on the assumed starting offset (alignment ambiguity).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you have a **recipe written entirely in chemical formulas** (e.g., $C_6H_{12}O_6$ instead of "Sugar"). An assembler is like translating "Sugar" into "$C_6H_{12}O_6$". A **disassembler is like the chemist who reads the formula $C_6H_{12}O_6$ and tells you — ah, this is sugar!** 

In an embedded system, your microcontroller (ARM, AVR, PIC, MSP430) executes raw bytes. When you flash a `.hex` file, the firmware inside is just bytes. If the source code is lost or you want to understand what a competitor's firmware does, a disassembler reads those bytes and reconstructs the **assembly instructions** (`MOV R0, #1`, `ADD R1, R2`, `B loop`) for human analysis.

### 1.3 Why Disassemblers Matter in Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Embedded firmware is often distributed only as binary (`.hex`, `.bin`, `.elf`). Disassemblers are indispensable for:
> - **Reverse engineering** of proprietary bootloader and RTOS code.
> - **Firmware security auditing** and vulnerability analysis (CWE/CVE).
> - **Debugging optimized code** where high-level symbols have been stripped.
> - **Post-mortem crash analysis** on resource-constrained MCUs without source access.
> - **Malware analysis** of IoT botnets (e.g., Mirai variants).

### 1.4 Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Mnemonic** | Human-readable opcode symbol (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`, `STR`) |
| **Opcode** | Numeric operation code stored in machine code bytes |
| **Operand** | Register, immediate value, or memory address an opcode acts upon |
| **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** | The blueprint defining opcode-to-behavior mapping (ARMv7-M, AVR, x86, RISC-V) |
| **Endianness** | Byte ordering convention (**Little-Endian** vs **Big-Endian**) used when decoding multi-byte instructions |
| **Stripped Binary** | An ELF/HEX file with the symbol/debug table removed — disassembly still works, but symbol resolution is lost |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Disassembly Pipeline

A disassembler does not magically recover the original assembly. It performs a **deterministic, rule-based decoding** based on the target ISA specification. The pipeline has five logical stages:

1. **Binary Ingestion**: Load the raw `.bin`/`.hex`/`.elf` bytes into a linear memory buffer. Parse headers to determine entry point, architecture, load addresses, and section boundaries.
2. **Section Segmentation**: Partition memory into logical regions — `.text` (code), `.rodata` (constants), `.data` (initialized globals), `.bss` (zero-initialized globals).
3. **Decoding Strategy Selection**: Apply either *Linear Sweep* or *Recursive Traversal* (explained below) to decide the instruction stream order.
4. **Mnemonic Emission**: For each byte at the current offset, look up the opcode table of the target ISA, determine operand sizes, decode register fields, and emit assembly text.
5. **Symbolic Annotation (optional)**: If symbol tables are present, resolve addresses to function names, variable names, and string literals.

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Disassembly Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Examiner's commonly ask — *"Differentiate between Linear Sweep and Recursive Traversal."* Memorize this comparison.

#### A. Linear Sweep Disassembly

The simplest algorithm. It walks the **`.text` section sequentially from the lowest address to the highest**, decoding every byte as an instruction.

- **Strength**: Simple, fast, deterministic.
- **Weakness**: Fails badly when the compiler inserts **alignment padding bytes** or literal **data pools** (constants, jump tables) inline within code. These non-code bytes will be mis-decoded as garbage instructions, corrupting the entire downstream disassembly.

#### B. Recursive Traversal Disassembly

Begins at the program's **known entry point** (from the ELF header) and recursively follows every control-flow transfer (`JMP`, `BR`, `BL`, conditional branch). Each newly discovered branch target becomes a new starting address for recursive decoding.

- **Strength**: Naturally skips data interspersed in code; builds an accurate **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**.
- **Weakness**: Cannot statically resolve **indirect jumps** (`MOV PC, R0` in ARM, computed jumps in C `switch` statements) — these require **dynamic analysis** (e.g., via QEMU emulator or hardware tracing via SWD/JTAG).

### 2.3 Endianness in Disassembly

ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (used in STM32, NXP Kinetis, Nordic nRF) are **predominantly Little-Endian**. This means a 32-bit instruction `0xE3A01005` is stored in memory as the byte sequence `[0x05, 0x10, 0xA0, 0xE3]`. A disassembler must reverse the byte order before mnemonic lookup.

$$\text{Memory layout} = \big[ b_0,\ b_1,\ b_2,\ b_3 \big] \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Instruction} = b_3 \cdot 2^{24} + b_2 \cdot 2^{16} + b_1 \cdot 2^{8} + b_0$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in formulas. No raw $\vert$ pipes inside markdown tables.

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Significance in Disassembly |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| Address Calculation | $\text{addr}_{n+1} = \text{addr}_n + \text{sizeof}(\text{inst})$ | Linear sweep advancement step |
| ARM Branch Offset | $\text{target} = \text{PC} + 8 + \text{signed\_offset} \cdot 4$ | Recursive traversal edge discovery |
| Endianness Reverse (LE) | $\text{word} = \sum_{i=0}^{3} b_i \cdot 256^{\,i}$ | Reconstruct 32-bit instruction from bytes |
| Coverage Ratio | $C = \dfrac{\text{Bytes decoded as code}}{\text{Total .text size}} \cdot 100\%$ | Disassembler accuracy metric |
| Call Depth Bound | $D_{\max} = 2^{n}-1 \;\;(n = \text{branch bits})$ | Worst-case recursive traversal paths |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

In commercial embedded firmware security audits, a tool like **Ghidra** (NSA-released) or **IDA Pro** can disassemble a 256 KB STM32 firmware image in under 30 seconds. Security researchers then manually trace interrupt handlers, cryptographic routines, and peripheral register accesses to identify hard-coded keys, buffer overflows, or insecure debug interfaces (e.g., enabled JTAG/SWD locks in production silicon).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Disassembling a Thumb-2 Instruction Sequence

Consider the following **little-endian machine code bytes** extracted from an ARM Cortex-M0 `.text` section (entry at `0x08000000`):

```
Offset   Bytes (LE)         Hex Word
0x00   : 05 20              0x2005
0x02   : 00 21              0x2100
0x04   : 01 22              0x2201
0x06   : 88 47              0x4788
0x08   : FE E7              0xE7FE
```

We will manually disassemble this byte-by-byte using the **ARMv6-M (Cortex-M0) Thumb ISA reference**.

### Step 1 — Linear Sweep Initialization

Start decoding at offset `0x00`. Read 2 bytes (Thumb uses 16-bit instructions on Cortex-M0).

$$
\text{Raw bytes at } 0\text{x}00 = [0\text{x}05,\ 0\text{x}20] \implies \text{Word} = 0\text{x}05 + 0\text{x}20 \cdot 256 = 0\text{x}2005
$$

### Step 2 — Decode Opcode `0x2005`

Looking up the Thumb opcode map:

$$
0\text{x}2005 = \underbrace{0010}_{\text{opcode}=2} \ \underbrace{0000\ 0000}_{\text{imm8}} \ \underbrace{0101}_{\text{Rd}=R5}}
$$

This matches `MOVS Rd, #imm8`. So:

$$
\text{Mnemonic: } \texttt{MOVS R5, \#0x05}
$$

### Step 3 — Advance to Offset `0x02`

$$
\text{addr}_{1} = 0\text{x}00 + 2 = 0\text{x}02
$$

Read 2 bytes: `[0x00, 0x21]`:

$$
\text{Word} = 0\text{x}00 + 0\text{x}21 \cdot 256 = 0\text{x}2100
$$

Decode: `0010 0001 0000 0000` → `MOVS R0, #0x00`.

$$
\text{Mnemonic: } \texttt{MOVS R0, \#0x00}
$$

### Step 4 — Advance to Offset `0x04`

$$
\text{Word at } 0\text{x}04 = [0\text{x}01,\ 0\text{x}22] = 0\text{x}2201
$$

Decode: `MOVS R1, #0x01`.

$$
\text{Mnemonic: } \texttt{MOVS R1, \#0x01}
$$

### Step 5 — Advance to Offset `0x06`

$$
\text{Word at } 0\text{x}06 = [0\text{x}88,\ 0\text{x}47] = 0\text{x}4788
$$

The opcode `0x47xx` is the Thumb `BX` instruction. Decode:

$$
\text{`BX R5`} \quad \text{(where R5 holds the function address loaded earlier — an indirect branch)}
$$

### Step 6 — Advance to Offset `0x08`

$$
\text{Word at } 0\text{x}08 = [0\text{x}FE,\ 0\text{x}E7] = 0\text{x}E7FE
$$

Decode: this is `B .-2` (unconditional branch to PC-2, i.e., an infinite loop / `while(1)`). 

$$
\text{Mnemonic: } \texttt{B .}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Reconstruction

The disassembler emits the following clean assembly listing:

```asm
0x08000000: MOVS R5, #0x05       ; Load constant 5 into R5 (function pointer)
0x08000002: MOVS R0, #0x00       ; R0 = 0
0x08000004: MOVS R1, #0x01       ; R1 = 1
0x08000006: BX   R5              ; Indirect call to address in R5
0x08000008: B    .               ; Infinite loop (return never happens)
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linear Sweep vs Recursive Traversal

| Criterion | Linear Sweep | Recursive Traversal |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Algorithm** | Sequential walk from `.text` start | CFG-based following of branch edges |
| **Data Mis-Decoding** | Common failure mode | Naturally avoided |
| **Indirect Branch Handling** | No special handling | Requires heuristic fallback |
| **Speed** | $O(n)$ — fastest | $O(n)$ average, can be $O(2^n)$ worst-case |
| **Code Coverage** | Often over-estimates | More accurate CFG reconstruction |
| **Tool Examples** | `objdump -d`, default `radare2` | IDA Pro, Ghidra (default), Binary Ninja |
| **KTU Exam Preference** | Simpler to describe in 3 marks | Favored for 14-mark analysis questions |

### 3.3 Static vs Dynamic Disassembly

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students often confuse these — clarify:

- **Static Disassembly**: Examines the binary without execution. Uses ELF headers, section info, and static heuristics. Cannot resolve runtime-computed jump targets.
- **Dynamic Disassembly**: Runs the binary (often inside an emulator like **QEMU**, **Unicorn Engine**, or on real hardware with **JTAG/SWD trace**) and records executed instructions via instruction-level tracing. Produces 100% accurate execution paths but only for the test inputs executed.

Both approaches are often combined in **hybrid disassemblers** for IoT firmware security research.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Disassembly Pipeline (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Binary Input .bin or .hex or .elf] --> B[Header Parser]
    B --> C[Section Segmenter .text .data .rodata]
    C --> D{Disassembly Strategy}
    D -->|Linear Sweep| E1[Sequential Decoder]
    D -->|Recursive Traversal| E2[CFG Builder]
    E1 --> F[Opcode Lookup Table]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Endianness Adapter LE or BE]
    G --> H[Instruction Emitter]
    H --> I[Symbol Resolver optional]
    I --> J[Annotated Assembly Listing]
    E2 --> K[Indirect Jump Resolver]
    K -->|Static Heuristic| E2
    K -->|Dynamic Trace via SWD| E2
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Static vs Dynamic Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Firmware Image] --> Q1{Source Code Available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| No1[Use Native Compiler -d]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Indirect Jumps Frequent?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, e.g. switch statements| Dyn[Dynamic Disassembly with QEMU or Hardware Trace]
    Q2 -->|No| Static[Static Recursive Traversal with Ghidra or IDA]
    Dyn --> Output[Accurate CFG]
    Static --> Output
    No1 --> Output
```

### 4.3 Recursive Traversal State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Entry Point from ELF header] --> S1[Decode Current Instruction]
    S1 --> S2{Is it a branch?}
    S2 -->|Unconditional JMP| S3[Set Next Address to Target]
    S2 -->|Conditional BEQ BNE| S4[Branch to Target AND Fall Through]
    S2 -->|Indirect BX BLX| S5[Mark as Resolved at Runtime]
    S2 -->|Return BX LR| S6[Pop from Call Stack]
    S3 --> S1
    S4 --> S1
    S5 --> S1
    S6 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs above use alphanumeric prefixes (`S0`, `S1`, etc.) and avoid reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone names. Labels contain raw uppercase text only, with no markdown formatting tags embedded inside the quoted label strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Define a disassembler. List any two popular disassembler tools used for embedded firmware analysis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A disassembler is a software tool that translates machine code (binary object code) back into its equivalent assembly language mnemonics, effectively performing the reverse operation of an assembler. It is widely used in embedded systems for reverse engineering, debugging, and security analysis of firmware binaries where the original source code is unavailable.

**Two popular disassembler tools (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Ghidra** — Open-source NSA-developed reverse engineering framework supporting ARM, AVR, MIPS, RISC-V and many embedded ISAs.
2. **IDA Pro** — Industry-standard commercial disassembler with advanced recursive traversal and decompilation capabilities (also IDA Free / IDA Home for students).

*(Alternative valid tools: `objdump` from GNU binutils, Binary Ninja, radare2.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
What is meant by **Linear Sweep** disassembly? State one major limitation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Linear sweep disassembly is a decoding algorithm that walks through the code (`.text`) section of a binary sequentially, starting from the lowest address and decoding every successive byte/word as an instruction until the section end is reached.

**Major Limitation (1.5 marks):** It cannot distinguish between code bytes and embedded data such as **literal pools** (jump tables, constants) inserted by the compiler between functions. When it encounters such data, it decodes them as spurious instructions, corrupting the entire downstream disassembly output.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical disassembler. Discuss the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Compare Linear Sweep and Recursive Traversal disassembly algorithms. Which one is preferred for embedded firmware analysis and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Disassembler Architecture (7 marks):**

A typical disassembler consists of five sequential stages, as illustrated below:

| Stage | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **1. Binary Loader** | Reads `.bin`, `.hex`, or `.elf` files into memory; validates magic numbers and headers |
| **2. Section Parser** | Segments image into `.text` (code), `.rodata`, `.data`, `.bss` based on ELF program headers |
| **3. Decoding Engine** | Applies either Linear Sweep or Recursive Traversal to walk through instruction stream |
| **4. Opcode Decoder** | Maps each byte/word to the corresponding mnemonic by consulting the target ISA's opcode table |
| **5. Output Formatter** | Emits assembly listing with addresses, mnemonics, operands, and optional annotations |

Each stage is **modular** so that the same framework can support multiple ISAs (ARM, AVR, RISC-V) by simply swapping the opcode table.

**(b) Linear Sweep vs Recursive Traversal (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Linear Sweep | Recursive Traversal |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| Walking strategy | Sequential from base address | Follows control-flow edges |
| Data-in-code handling | Poor (mis-decodes literal pools) | Excellent (skips non-code) |
| Indirect branch support | None | Requires static/dynamic analysis |
| Speed | Very fast ($O(n)$) | Comparable but memory-intensive |
| Output quality | Lower — single linear listing | Higher — produces accurate CFG |

**Preferred for embedded firmware:** **Recursive Traversal** is preferred because compiled ARM Thumb code frequently contains literal pools and jump tables between functions; Linear Sweep would corrupt these and produce misleading output. Tools like Ghidra and IDA Pro default to Recursive Traversal for precisely this reason.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**  
**(a)** Explain **Static** and **Dynamic** disassembly. Discuss the situations where each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Describe how a recursive traversal disassembler handles **indirect branches** such as `BX R0` in ARM. What are the limitations of purely static analysis? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Static vs Dynamic Disassembly (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Disassembly | Dynamic Disassembly |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Execution | Binary is NOT executed | Binary runs inside emulator/on real hardware |
| Method | Reads ELF, decodes code section | Traces instruction stream during execution |
| Indirect jumps | Heuristic-based (may fail) | Resolved exactly via runtime observation |
| Coverage | All static paths (theoretical) | Only paths actually executed |
| Speed | Very fast | Slow due to emulation overhead |
| Tools | Ghidra, IDA Pro, objdump | QEMU + tracing, Unicorn Engine, JTAG/SWD |

**Static disassembly** is suitable for initial reconnaissance, large-scale analysis, and when the firmware cannot be executed safely (e.g., suspected malware). **Dynamic disassembly** is suitable for resolving complex indirect branches (function pointers, C++ vtables, RTOS task switches) and obtaining an accurate execution trace.

**(b) Handling Indirect Branches (7 marks):**

In ARM, an indirect branch like `BX R0` transfers control to the address held in register `R0`, which is **computed at runtime**. A purely static recursive traversal disassembler cannot know the target a priori. It resorts to one or more of the following strategies:

1. **Heuristic Target Enumeration:** Scan the binary for all function prologue patterns (`PUSH {LR}; ...`) within a plausible range and add them as candidate branch targets.
2. **Type-Recovery Analysis:** Use data-flow analysis to propagate known constant values into R0 (constant propagation) when possible.
3. **External Library Resolution:** If R0 is loaded from a vtable or known API table, resolve via the table contents.
4. **Fallback to Linear Sweep:** As a last resort, fall back to Linear Sweep decoding on the target region.

**Limitations of purely static analysis:**
- Cannot resolve **runtime-computed** jump targets (e.g., `switch` statements compiled to jump tables).
- Misses **dynamically generated code** (self-modifying firmware, Just-In-Time patching in bootloader updates).
- Ignores **hardware-triggered control flow** such as ARM Cortex-M exception vectors that fire only on interrupts.

These limitations motivate hybrid (static + dynamic) analysis frameworks used in modern IoT security audits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "assembler" with "disassembler"** in definitions — directionality matters (assembly → binary vs binary → assembly).
> 2. **Writing only definitions** for comparison questions without using a table — examiners award marks for structured contrast.
> 3. **Forgetting endianness** — stating that a 32-bit ARM instruction `0xE3A01005` is stored byte-wise as `[0xE3, 0xA0, ...]` will lose marks. Remember **Little-Endian** means reversed.
> 4. **Omitting examples** — naming `Ghidra`, `IDA Pro`, or `objdump` explicitly earns you tool-specific credit.
> 5. **Skipping the CFG aspect** — for 14-mark questions on Recursive Traversal, you must explicitly mention Control Flow Graph construction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **disassembler** is the reverse of an assembler — it converts **machine code → assembly mnemonics**.
- The mapping is **not always one-to-one**; alignment and data-in-code ambiguity make it non-trivial.
- **Linear Sweep** = simple, fast, but unreliable in the presence of literal pools and jump tables.
- **Recursive Traversal** = follows the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**; produces accurate but more complex output.
- **Static disassembly** examines the binary offline; **Dynamic disassembly** traces execution via emulators (`QEMU`, `Unicorn`) or hardware probes (`JTAG/SWD`).
- **Endianness** is critical — ARM Cortex-M is **Little-Endian**, so bytes must be reversed before opcode lookup.
- **Indirect branches** (`BX R0`, computed jumps) require **heuristics + dynamic analysis** for full resolution.
- **Industry tools** to remember: **Ghidra** (free), **IDA Pro** (commercial), **`objdump`** (GNU/Linux default), **radare2**, **Binary Ninja**.
- **Direct branch offset formula** (ARM): $\text{target} = \text{PC} + 8 + (\text{signed\_offset}) \cdot 4$.
- **Why it matters in KTU context:** Embedded firmware is shipped as binary; disassembly is the **only practical way** to audit, debug, or reverse-engineer it when source is unavailable — a recurring theme in IoT security and forensics modules.
- Always cite **at least two specific tools** when answering definitional questions to maximize marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decompilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decompilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Decompilers — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Decompiler** (also called a *reverse compiler*, *source code re-generator*, or *machine-code decompiler*) is a software program that takes an executable binary file (machine code, object code, or bytecode) as input and attempts to translate it back into a high-level programming language source code that is functionally equivalent to the original source from which the binary was compiled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A *decompiler* is a computer program that translates executable code (machine code or bytecode) into a high-level source language, attempting to recover the original source program's logic, structure, and semantics. It is the logical inverse of a *compiler*.

Mathematically, the decompilation process can be represented as a mapping function $D$ that operates on a binary program $B$:

$$D(B) \approx S$$

where $S$ represents the reconstructed high-level source code, and the symbol $\approx$ denotes *semantic equivalence* (functional equivalence) rather than exact textual equivalence, because perfect textual recovery is generally undecidable due to information loss during compilation (variable names, comments, formatting are stripped away).

## 1.2 The Compilation vs. Decompilation Relationship

To understand decompilers, we must first understand the **forward pipeline** of compilation:

$$\text{Source Code (C/C++)} \xrightarrow{\text{Preprocessor}} \text{Expanded Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Compiler Front-End}} \text{IR/AST} \xrightarrow{\text{Optimizer}} \text{Optimized IR} \xrightarrow{\text{Backend (Code Gen)}} \text{Assembly} \xrightarrow{\text{Assembler}} \text{Object Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Linker}} \text{Executable Binary}$$

The decompiler **reverses** this entire process — from the rightmost artifact back to a representation close to the leftmost one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU:** A compiler translates *high-level abstractions → low-level machine primitives*. A decompiler must *infer high-level abstractions from low-level machine primitives*. This inference problem is what makes decompilation fundamentally harder than compilation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World)

**The Shredded Document Analogy:** Imagine you wrote a 50-page research paper and then ran it through an industrial paper shredder. The shredder (compiler) destroys:
- The chapter titles (variable/function names)
- The paragraph spacing (code formatting)  
- Your margin notes (comments)
- The logical grouping of sections (data structures)

What remains are thousands of paper strips (assembly instructions / machine code).

A **decompiler** is like a forensic linguist who:
1. **Picks up each strip** (instruction decoding)
2. **Groups strips that likely belong to the same paragraph** (basic block / function detection)
3. **Tries to guess the original chapter they came from** (type recovery, variable naming)
4. **Reconstructs a "best-guess" version** of the paper in readable form

The reconstructed paper will **never be a perfect copy** — some words are guessed, some paragraphs may be slightly jumbled, and some strips might be permanently unreadable. This is the inherent uncertainty in decompilation.

## 1.4 Why Decompilers Matter in Embedded System Design

For KTU Embedded System Design students, decompilers are critical because:

1. **Firmware Reverse Engineering** — When original source code is lost or proprietary, embedded engineers use decompilers (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro) to understand how a microcontroller's firmware works.
2. **Security Auditing** — IoT devices, routers, and smart sensors are routinely analyzed via decompilation to find vulnerabilities (CVEs).
3. **Legacy Code Recovery** — Old automotive ECUs, industrial PLCs, and aerospace controllers often have firmware with lost source code — decompilers help recover logic.
4. **Malware Analysis** — Embedded malware (e.g., Stuxnet) targeting SCADA/PLC systems is dissected using decompilers.
5. **Bug Forensics** — Field-deployed embedded devices fail; decompilation of the firmware binary helps locate the root cause when source is unavailable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric / Industry Standard:** The de facto open-source national-grade decompiler is **Ghidra** (released by the U.S. NSA in 2019). Commercial standards include **IDA Pro** and **Binary Ninja**. Academic standards include **RetDec** (Avast) and **Radare2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2: Decompilers — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 The Decompilation Pipeline (Seven Logical Phases)

A modern decompiler does **not** magically transform binaries to C code. It follows a strict, layered pipeline. Each phase builds on the output of the previous one.

### Phase 1 — **Loading & Parsing (Binary Front-End)**
- Reads the **Executable File Format** (ELF for Linux/RTOS, COFF/AXF for ARM, Intel HEX for microcontrollers, Motorola S-record, raw binary for bare-metal).
- Parses **section headers** (.text, .data, .rodata, .bss), symbol tables, relocation entries, and entry-point addresses.
- For ELF specifically, the decompiler reads the *program header table* to map virtual addresses to file offsets.

### Phase 2 — **Disassembly (Machine Code → Assembly)**
- Converts raw **opcodes** (binary bytes) into human-readable **assembly mnemonics** (MOV, ADD, BL, PUSH, etc.).
- Two strategies:
  - *Linear Sweep* — Walk through .text section sequentially. Fast but vulnerable to mis-alignment when data and code are interleaved.
  - *Recursive Descent* — Follow control-flow from the entry point. Slower but more accurate (used by IDA Pro / Ghidra).

### Phase 3 — **Intermediate Representation (IR) Construction**
- Assembly instructions are lifted into a **platform-independent IR** (e.g., Ghidra's *p-code*, LLVM IR, RetDec's LLVM-based IR).
- IR enables decompilers to support multiple architectures (ARM, x86, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR, PIC) with one analysis engine.

### Phase 4 — **Control-Flow Analysis**
- Builds the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** — a directed graph where nodes are *basic blocks* (maximal straight-line instruction sequences) and edges are *control transfers* (jumps, branches, calls, falls-through).
- Identifies:
  - **Loops** (natural loops, irreducible loops)
  - **Conditionals** (if-then-else)
  - **Switch-case dispatch tables**
  - **Function entry and exit points**

### Phase 5 — **Data-Flow Analysis**
- Tracks how data propagates through registers and stack slots:
  - **Liveness Analysis** — which registers hold values still needed?
  - **Use-Def Chains** — where is each value defined and used?
  - **SSA (Static Single Assignment) Form** — each variable is assigned exactly once; simplifies analysis.

### Phase 6 — **Type Recovery & Variable Naming**
- The hardest phase. Infers:
  - Pointer vs. integer vs. array vs. struct
  - Function signatures (number of arguments, return type)
  - **Heuristics used:**
    - *Calling convention* analysis (caller/callee-saved registers)
    - *Use-pattern heuristics* (e.g., value ANDed with 0xFF → likely byte)
    - *Propagation heuristics* (data flows from a known source)

### Phase 7 — **Code Generation (IR → High-Level Language)**
- Translates IR back into C-like pseudocode using structured patterns:
  - `if (cond) { ... } else { ... }` reconstruction
  - `while/for` loop reconstruction
  - `switch` reconstruction from jump tables
  - Function call reconstruction

## 2.2 Decompiler vs. Disassembler — A Critical Distinction

| Feature | **Disassembler** | **Decompiler** |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Binary | Binary |
| Output | **Assembly language** (MOV, ADD, BL) | **High-level pseudocode** (if, while, for) |
| Abstraction level | Low (architecture-specific) | High (architecture-independent) |
| Requires IR | Usually no | Yes (always) |
| Recovers variables | No (uses registers/stack slots) | Yes (named, typed locals) |
| Recovers control structures | No (raw jumps) | Yes (structured C-like code) |
| Examples | `objdump`, `ndisasm`, `radare2` | Ghidra, IDA Pro Hex-Rays, RetDec |
| Use in KTU labs | Step-1 of reverse engineering | Final goal of reverse engineering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Pearl:** A disassembler shows you *what the CPU does*; a decompiler tells you *what the programmer intended*. The first is mechanical; the second is inferential.

## 2.3 The Inherent Difficulty — Why Decompilation Cannot Be Perfect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Information-Loss in Compilation):** Compilation is a *many-to-one* mapping from source programs to binaries (different sources compile to the same binary under optimization), hence its inverse — decompilation — is mathematically a *one-to-many* problem and is therefore *non-invertible* in the strict sense.

**What is lost during compilation (and thus unrecoverable by decompilation):**

1. **Variable and function names** — replaced by addresses or stack offsets.
2. **Comments and source-level documentation.**
3. **Source-level type information** — `int` and `pointer` look identical in assembly.
4. **Preprocessor directives** — `#define`, `#include` are erased.
5. **Original code formatting and structure** — lost during AST → IR → machine code.
6. **Distinct high-level constructs** that map to identical assembly — e.g., `for` vs. `while` vs. recursion may produce identical machine code.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decompilation Function | $D(B) \approx S$ | Binary $B$ mapped to source $S$ | None |
| Semantic Equivalence | $\forall\, \text{inputs}\; x: \; B(x) = S(x)$ | Same behavior on all inputs | None |
| CFG Node | $V = \{v_1, v_2, ..., v_n\}$ | Basic blocks | Count |
| CFG Edge | $E \subseteq V \times V$ | Control transfers | Count |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $M = E - V + 2P$ | Edges − Nodes + 2·components | Scalar |
| SSA Phi Function | $v_n = \phi(v_1, v_2, ..., v_k)$ | Variable merging at join | Scalar |
| Instruction-to-IR ratio | $\eta = \frac{N_{IR}}{N_{asm}}$ | Expansion factor | Scalar (typically 2-5) |
| Decompiler Accuracy | $A = \frac{N_{correct\,lines}}{N_{total\,lines}}$ | Quality metric (0 to 1) | Dimensionless |
| Stack Frame Size | $F = \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i$ | Sum of local variable sizes | Bytes |
| Calling Convention Overhead | $O_{call} = N_{regs} + N_{stack}$ | Saved registers + stack args | Slots |

## 2.5 Architectural Targets in Embedded Decompilation

KTU 2024 emphasizes that embedded binaries are typically for:

| Architecture | Endianness | Common Embedded Use | Decompiler Support |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ARM Cortex-M** (Thumb-2) | Little | STM32, NXP, Nordic, automotive MCUs | Excellent (Ghidra, IDA) |
| **AVR** (8-bit) | Little | Arduino, ATmega | Good (Ghidra, simavr) |
| **PIC** (8/16/32) | Little | Microchip MCUs | Moderate |
| **MIPS** | Both | Legacy routers, Wi-Fi chips | Excellent |
| **RISC-V** (RV32IMAC) | Little | New IoT SoCs | Excellent |
| **8051** | Big | Legacy industrial MCUs | Moderate |
| **x86 / x86-64** | Little | Edge AI, industrial PCs | Excellent |
| **Xtensa** | Little | ESP32 (Tensilica) | Good (Ghidra + plugins) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** **Ghidra** supports ~16 processor instruction sets out of the box including all major embedded architectures. It is the *de facto* teaching tool in reverse-engineering courses worldwide.

# Module 2: Decompilers — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Analysis 1 — Reconstructing a Simple Function

**Problem Statement:** Given the following ARM Cortex-M (Thumb-2) assembly extracted by a disassembler, derive the equivalent C pseudocode that a decompiler would produce.

**Input Assembly (ARM Thumb-2):**

```asm
func01:
    push    {r4, lr}
    mov     r2, r0
    mov     r3, #0
    cmp     r1, #0
    beq     .L_end
.L_loop:
    ldrb    r4, [r2, r3]
    cmp     r4, #0
    beq     .L_end
    add     r3, r3, #1
    b       .L_loop
.L_end:
    mov     r0, r3
    pop     {r4, pc}
```

### Step-by-Step Decompiler Reasoning:

**Step 1 — Identify Function Boundaries:**
- The function starts at the `push {r4, lr}` instruction (standard ARM function prologue — saves callee-saved registers and the link register).
- The function ends at `pop {r4, pc}` (epilogue — restores registers and returns via PC).

**Step 2 — Identify Arguments (via ARM Calling Convention AAPCS):**
- `r0` holds the **first argument** → parameter 1.
- `r1` holds the **second argument** → parameter 2.

We hypothesize:
- `param_1` (in `r0`) is a pointer (later copied to `r2` for indexed access).
- `param_2` (in `r1`) is some integer (used in comparison).

**Step 3 — Translate Each Instruction to IR:**

| Line | Assembly | Intermediate Meaning (Semantics) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `push {r4, lr}` | save callee-saved regs; allocate stack frame |
| 2 | `mov r2, r0` | `r2 := r0` (copy first arg into a working register) |
| 3 | `mov r3, #0` | `r3 := 0` (initialize index variable) |
| 4 | `cmp r1, #0` | compare r1 with 0 |
| 5 | `beq .L_end` | if zero flag set, jump to `.L_end` |
| 6 | `ldrb r4, [r2, r3]` | load *byte* from memory at `r2 + r3` → `r4` |
| 7 | `cmp r4, #0` | compare loaded byte with 0 |
| 8 | `beq .L_end` | if zero, exit loop |
| 9 | `add r3, r3, #1` | `r3 := r3 + 1` (increment index) |
| 10 | `b .L_loop` | unconditional branch back to loop start |
| 11 | `mov r0, r3` | move final index into return register |
| 12 | `pop {r4, pc}` | restore and return |

**Step 4 — Build Control Flow Graph (CFG):**

The CFG nodes and edges are:

```
nodeA (entry) → nodeB (init r2, r3)
nodeB → nodeC (cmp r1, #0) → branch-out (if r1==0 → nodeF)
nodeC → nodeD (loop body: ldrb, cmp r4) → branch-out (if r4==0 → nodeF)
nodeD → nodeE (add r3, increment) → back-edge to nodeD
nodeE → nodeF (return r3)
```

**Step 5 — Recognize Loop Pattern:**
- The back-edge from `nodeE` to `nodeD` forms a **natural loop**.
- The loop induction variable is `r3` (starts at 0, incremented by 1 each iteration).
- The exit condition is when `r4 == 0` (loaded byte is zero) OR when initial `r1 == 0`.

**Step 6 — Recognize String Operation Pattern:**
- The loop reads *bytes* (note the `ldrb` instruction, B-suffix = byte load).
- It stops when a zero byte is encountered — the classic **strlen-like pattern**.
- It also has an early-exit guard: if the second argument `r1` is zero, do nothing.

**Step 7 — Final Decompiled C Pseudocode (Decompiler Output):**

```c
size_t func01(char *param_1, size_t param_2) {
    if (param_2 == 0) {
        return 0;
    }
    size_t index = 0;
    while (param_1[index] != 0) {
        index = index + 1;
    }
    return index;
}
```

This is essentially the **`strnlen`** function from the C standard library — a string length function with a maximum bound.

## 3.2 Derivative Analysis 2 — Cyclomatic Complexity of the Decompiled CFG

**Problem Statement:** Compute the cyclomatic complexity $M$ of the reconstructed CFG above.

**Step-by-Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= E - V + 2P
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = number of nodes (basic blocks) = 6 (nodeA through nodeF)
- $E$ = number of edges = 7 (entry-to-init, init-to-cmp, cmp-to-end, cmp-to-loop, loop-to-end, loop-to-increment, increment-to-loop)
- $P$ = number of connected components = 1 (single function)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 7 - 6 + 2(1) \\
  &= 7 - 6 + 2 \\
  &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $M = 3$ indicates **3 independent paths** through the function:
1. Early-exit path (param_2 == 0)
2. Loop terminates normally (zero byte found)
3. (The third path is implicit — counting is mathematical)

A complexity of 3 is considered *low* — easy to test, easy to maintain. Decompilers often report this metric automatically (Ghidra's "Function Complexity" metric).

## 3.3 Derivative Analysis 3 — SSA Transformation of the Loop

**Problem Statement:** Apply SSA form to the loop induction variable `r3`.

The original IR (pre-SSA) is:
```
init:    r3 = 0
entry:   if (r1 == 0) goto end
loop:    r4 = *(r2 + r3)
         if (r4 == 0) goto end
         r3 = r3 + 1
         goto loop
end:     r0 = r3
         return
```

After SSA conversion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{init:} \quad & r3_0 = 0 \\
\text{entry:} \quad & \text{if } (r1 == 0) \text{ goto end} \\
\text{loop:} \quad & r4_0 = *(r2 + r3_0) \\
& \text{if } (r4_0 == 0) \text{ goto end} \\
& r3_1 = r3_0 + 1 \\
\text{phi-node:} \quad & r3_2 = \phi(r3_0, r3_1) \\
\text{goto loop} & \\
\text{end:} \quad & r0 = r3_2 \\
& \text{return}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\phi$-function merges the two incoming definitions of `r3` (initial value and loop-carried value) into a single SSA name `r3_2`. This transformation is what allows the decompiler to reason cleanly about variable use-def chains.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decompiler Quality Comparison

| Decompiler | Architecture Support | IR Type | Type Recovery Quality | Open Source | KTU-Relevant? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ghidra (NSA)** | 16+ ISAs (ARM, AVR, MIPS, RISC-V, x86) | p-code (custom) | Excellent | Yes (Apache 2.0) | ★★★★★ |
| **IDA Pro (Hex-Rays)** | 80+ ISAs | Microcode (proprietary) | Best-in-class | No ($$$) | ★★★★ |
| **Binary Ninja** | ARM, x86, MIPS, RISC-V | BNIL | Very Good | Partial (commercial) | ★★★ |
| **RetDec (Avast)** | x86, ARM, MIPS, PIC | LLVM IR | Good | Yes (MIT) | ★★★★ |
| **Radare2 / r2dec** | Many | ESIL | Basic | Yes (LGPL) | ★★★ |
| **JEB Decompiler** | ARM, x86, MIPS | Custom | Good | No (commercial) | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** Ghidra is the recommended tool for KTU practical sessions because it is free, supports embedded architectures, and provides a Java-based GUI scripting interface for automation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB --> nodeC{Cmp r1 zero}
    nodeC -- yes --> nodeF[Return r0=r3]
    nodeC -- no --> nodeD[Loop Body ldrb r4 r2 r3]
    nodeD --> nodeD2{Cmp r4 zero}
    nodeD2 -- yes --> nodeF
    nodeD2 -- no --> nodeE[Increment r3=r3 plus 1]

    nodeF --> nodeG[Exit pop r4 pc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The back-edge from `nodeE` to `nodeD` is the **loop-back edge**. The decompiler's loop-detection algorithm identifies this edge and reconstructs it as a `while` or `for` construct in the high-level output.

# Module 2: Decompilers — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a decompiler. How is it different from a disassembler?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
