- **Task dispatching** (actually performing the context switch)
- **Inter-task communication and synchronization**
- **Interrupt and exception handling**
- **Memory and resource management**

> [!NOTE]
> The kernel operates in **privileged/supervisor mode** (ring 0 in x86, handler mode in ARM Cortex-M), while user applications run in **unprivileged/user mode** (thread mode in ARM Cortex-M). This separation is enforced by the **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** in modern embedded processors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Basic OS Services

### 2.1.1 Process / Task Management

A **process (or task)** is the basic unit of execution scheduled by the OS. In RTOS terminology, a *process* and a *task* are often used interchangeably, but a *thread* is a lightweight sub-unit of a process sharing the same address space.

**Key operations the kernel performs on tasks:**

- **Task Creation:** Allocates a **Task Control Block (TCB)** containing:
  - Task ID / Priority
  - Stack pointer (SP)
  - Program counter (PC)
  - Registers (R0–R15 in ARM)
  - State (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended)
- **Task Deletion:** Deallocates TCB and stack memory.
- **Task State Transition:** Manages the state diagram.
- **Task Scheduling:** Selects the next runnable task.
- **Context Switching:** Saves state of outgoing task, loads state of incoming task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Diagram of an RTOS Task (5 States):**
> **NEW → READY → RUNNING → BLOCKED → READY → ... → TERMINATED**
> Transitions: `Dispatch`, `Preempt`, `Wait (Event)`, `Event Occurs`, `Exit`.

### 2.1.2 Memory Management

In small RTOS (µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS), memory management is provided as a fixed-size block allocator (memory pool) to avoid fragmentation. The kernel offers:

- `OSMemCreate()` — creates a memory partition.
- `OSMemGet()` — allocates a block.
- `OSMemPut()` — returns a block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use `malloc()`/`free()` in RTOS?** Because they are **non-deterministic** — their execution time varies with heap fragmentation. RTOS demands bounded execution time, hence fixed-size block allocation is used.

### 2.1.3 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) & Synchronization

Tasks must communicate and synchronize to share data and resources. The kernel provides:

| Mechanism | Purpose | KTU API Example |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| **Semaphore (Binary)** | Mutual exclusion, signalling | `OSSemCreate()`, `OSSemPend()`, `OSSemPost()` |
| **Mutex** | Mutual exclusion with priority inheritance | `OSMutexCreate()` |
| **Message Queue** | Pass data between tasks | `OSQCreate()`, `OSQPend()`, `OSQPost()` |
| **Mailbox** | Single-message pointer exchange | `OSMboxCreate()` |
| **Event Flags** | Multi-condition synchronization | `OSFlagCreate()` |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic (Board Exam):** "**S**emaphores **S**ignal, **M**utexes **M**utex-exclude, **Q**ueues carry **Q**uantities of data, **M**ailboxes carry **M**essages (pointers), **F**lags carry **F**lags."

### 2.1.4 Time Management

Driven by a **hardware timer interrupt (System Tick)** that fires at a configurable rate (e.g., every 1 ms or 10 ms). Used for:

- Time slicing (round-robin scheduling)
- Task delays (`OSTimeDly()`)
- Timeout specification in IPC waits
- Periodic task wake-up

### 2.1.5 Interrupt Handling

- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)** are *not* tasks — they run in a special interrupt context.
- The kernel interacts via two key functions:
  - `OSIntEnter()` — called at start of ISR (notifies kernel of ISR nesting).
  - `OSIntExit()` — called at end of ISR (kernel may perform a context switch if a higher-priority task is now ready).
- **Interrupt Latency** must be bounded — this is a critical RTOS metric.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Calling blocking kernel APIs (like `OSSemPend()`) from inside an ISR. This is **NOT allowed** because the ISR has no TCB to block. Use non-blocking variants (`OSSemAccept()`) instead.

### 2.1.6 I/O and Device Management

A Device Driver abstracts hardware. The kernel maintains a **device table** and provides uniform APIs (`open`, `read`, `write`, `ioctl`) to applications.

### 2.1.7 Error Detection and Recovery

- **Watchdog Timer (WDT):** Hardware timer that resets the system if the kernel does not "pet" it within a window.
- **Stack Overflow Detection:** Kernel checks a guard pattern (e.g., `0xA5A5A5A5`) at the end of each task's stack.
- **Assertion Macros:** `OS_ASSERT()` for parameter validation.

---

## 2.2 RTOS Kernel — Detailed Types

The **kernel structure** determines the trade-off between **performance** and **modularity**. The KTU syllabus emphasizes three primary kernel architectures.

### 2.2.1 Monolithic Kernel

- All OS services (scheduler, file system, device drivers, network stack) reside **in kernel space** as a single large binary.
- **Pros:** Very fast (no mode switching between services).
- **Cons:** A bug in any service can crash the entire system. Difficult to maintain.
- **Example:** Traditional Unix, µC/OS-II (mostly), VxWorks.

### 2.2.2 Microkernel

- Only the **bare minimum** (scheduler, IPC primitives, basic memory management) is in kernel space.
- All other services (file system, device drivers, network) run as **user-space processes**.
- **Pros:** Highly modular, fault-isolated, secure.
- **Cons:** Performance overhead due to **message passing** between user-space services.
- **Example:** QNX, MINIX, L4, GNU Hurd.

### 2.2.3 Hybrid Kernel (Modified Microkernel)

- A pragmatic mix: most services in kernel space (for speed) but with **modular architecture** (for maintainability).
- **Example:** Windows NT, macOS XNU, modern RTOS like VxWorks.

### 2.2.4 Exokernel

- Extreme minimalism — the kernel only **multiplexes the raw hardware**; OS abstractions are implemented as user-space libraries.
- **Example:** MIT Exokernel, used in research.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises all essential formulas and metrics used in RTOS-based design questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | Typical Value |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **System Tick Period** | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | $T_{tick}$ in seconds, $f_{tick}$ in Hz | 1 ms to 10 ms |
| 2 | **CPU Utilization (single task)** | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = computation time, $T_i$ = period | 0 ≤ U ≤ 1 |
| 3 | **Total CPU Utilization (n tasks)** | $U = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | For independent periodic tasks | < 1 always |
| 4 | **Context Switch Time** | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Saving + loading task context | 1 to 10 µs |
| 5 | **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{lat} = T_{detect} + T_{save} + T_{dispatch}$ | Time from interrupt to first ISR instruction | < 5 µs |
| 6 | **Response Time** | $T_{resp} = T_{release} + T_{ready} + T_{exec}$ | Release to completion | Bounded |
| 7 | **Deadline** | $D \leq T$ (period) | For periodic tasks | $D_i \le T_i$ |
| 8 | **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{tasks}}{T_{window}}$ | Tasks completed per second | tasks/s |
| 9 | **Jitter** | $J = T_{actual} - T_{ideal}$ | Time deviation | µs to ms |
| 10 | **Schedulability (Liu & Layland bound for Rate Monotonic)** | $U \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | For RMS with n tasks | RMS bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule applied:** Absolute value bars and vertical separators inside table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. For example: $U \le n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$.

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Uses an OSEK/VDX-based RTOS kernel for ECU tasks.
- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Mandates rate-monotonic scheduling for flight software.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** FreeRTOS kernel runs on ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.
- **Medical Devices (FDA IEC 62304):** Watchdog + stack checking enforced by kernel.
- **Robotics:** Hard real-time kernel (e.g., Xenomai) controlling motor loops at 1 kHz.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: System Tick Period vs. Scheduler Granularity

This derivation establishes the relationship between the hardware timer frequency, system tick period, and the maximum scheduler granularity (i.e., the minimum time slice the scheduler can allocate).

### Given:
- $f_{tick}$ = the frequency of the **System Tick Timer** (hardware timer interrupt), in Hertz (Hz).

### Find:
- $T_{tick}$ = the time period between two consecutive system tick interrupts.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Definition of Frequency and Period.**

By definition, the period of a periodic signal is the reciprocal of its frequency.

$$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Typical Value.**

For example, in FreeRTOS, the default configuration `configTICK_RATE_HZ` is often set to 1000 Hz. Therefore:

$$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001 \text{ seconds} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Derive Maximum Scheduler Granularity.**

The **minimum time slice** that the scheduler can resolve is exactly one tick. So:

$$T_{slice,min} = T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Engineering Units (microseconds).**

For $f_{tick} = 10{,}000$ Hz:

$$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ µs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A higher tick frequency gives finer scheduling granularity but increases interrupt overhead. Embedded engineers typically choose $f_{tick}$ between 100 Hz and 10 kHz depending on the application's timing needs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total CPU Utilization for a Periodic Task Set

### Given:
- A system with $n$ periodic tasks.
- Task $i$ has computation time $C_i$ and period $T_i$ (where $T_i$ is the relative deadline: $D_i = T_i$).

### Find:
- The total CPU utilization $U$.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Utilization of a Single Task.**

In any period $T_i$, the task consumes $C_i$ time units. The fraction of CPU time consumed is the ratio:

$$U_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

**Step 2 — Summation Over All Tasks (Assumption: Independent Tasks).**

If the tasks are **independent** (no blocking, no precedence), the total CPU load is the sum:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility Condition.**

For a hard real-time system, the CPU cannot be 100% utilised; some idle time must exist for the kernel itself (idle task, context switches, ISRs). The strict feasibility bound is:

$$U < 1$$

A **necessary condition** for schedulability is therefore:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} < 1$$

**Step 4 — Liu & Layland Sufficient Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).**

For RMS (priority inversely proportional to period), a **sufficient** (but not necessary) condition is:

$$U \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

For example, with $n = 3$ tasks:

$$U \le 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7797 \approx 0.78$$

This means **78% of CPU** is the safe upper bound; the remaining 22% provides headroom.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Kernel Type vs. Trade-offs

The following analytical matrix helps decide **which kernel type to use** in a given embedded design — a common KTU 14-mark design question.

| Property | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel | Hybrid Kernel | Exokernel |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| **OS Services Location** | All in kernel space | Minimum in kernel; rest in user space | Most in kernel; some modular | Only hardware multiplexing |
| **Performance (IPC speed)** | ★★★★★ (Fastest) | ★★ (Slower due to IPC overhead) | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Modularity / Maintainability** | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Security / Fault Isolation** | ★ (One bug crashes all) | ★★★★★ (Fault isolated) | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Code Size (Footprint)** | Large | Small kernel, larger total | Large | Smallest |
| **Determinism (RTOS suitability)** | High | High (with care) | High | High |
| **Suitability for Embedded** | ✓ (most RTOS) | ✓ (QNX, safety-critical) | ✓ (VxWorks) | Research only |
| **Real-world Example** | µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS | QNX Neutrino, L4 | Windows NT, VxWorks | MIT Exokernel |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Shortcut:** "**Monolithic = Speed, Microkernel = Safety, Hybrid = Balanced, Exokernel = Research**."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: Task State Transitions

A task in an RTOS moves between states. The transitions are:

**NEW → READY:**
The task is created (`OSTaskCreate()`) and placed in the ready queue.

**READY → RUNNING:**
The scheduler dispatches the task (highest-priority ready task wins). This is a **Dispatch** transition.

**RUNNING → READY:**
A higher-priority task becomes ready, causing **Preemption**.

**RUNNING → BLOCKED:**
The task calls a blocking kernel API (e.g., `OSSemPend()`) and waits for an event. This is a **Wait** transition.

**BLOCKED → READY:**
The awaited event occurs (e.g., ISR calls `OSSemPost()`). The task is unblocked and re-enters the ready queue. This is an **Event-Occurs** transition.

**RUNNING → TERMINATED:**
The task calls `OSTaskDel()` on itself or is deleted by another task. Resources are released.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Understand this state diagram thoroughly — it is asked in both 3-mark and 14-mark questions, often with a request to draw the diagram and label all transitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTOS Layered Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **layered structure of an RTOS**, from hardware at the bottom to the user application at the top.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER\nUser Task Code\nSensors, Actuators, Control Logic"]
    apiLayer["API / SYSTEM CALL LAYER\nPOSIX / FreeRTOS / uC-OS APIs\nOSTaskCreate OSSemPend OSQPend"]
    kernelLayer["KERNEL / SERVICE LAYER\nScheduler\nIPC Manager\nMemory Manager\nTime Manager\nInterrupt Manager"]
    halLayer["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER\nBSP and Device Drivers\nCPU Registers, Timers, UART, GPIO"]
    hwLayer["HARDWARE LAYER\nCPU, RAM, Flash, Peripherals"]

    appLayer -->|"Invokes API calls"| apiLayer
    apiLayer -->|"System call interface"| kernelLayer
    kernelLayer -->|"Hardware register access"| halLayer
    halLayer -->|"Physical signalling"| hwLayer
    hwLayer -.->|"Interrupts, DMA, bus signals"| halLayer
    halLayer -.->|"Hardware events"| kernelLayer
    kernelLayer -.->|"Event notifications, callbacks"| apiLayer
    apiLayer -.->|"Return values, signals"| appLayer
```

---

## 4.2 RTOS Kernel Internal Structure

The kernel is the central component. The diagram below shows its internal sub-modules and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskCtrl["TASK CONTROLLER\nTCB pool\nState machine"]
    scheduler["SCHEDULER\nPriority-based\nRound-Robin / RMS / EDF"]
    dispatcher["DISPATCHER\nContext switcher"]
    ipcMgr["IPC MANAGER\nSemaphores, Mutexes\nQueues, Mailboxes"]
    memMgr["MEMORY MANAGER\nFixed block allocator"]
    timeMgr["TIME MANAGER\nTick counter\nDelays, Timers"]
    intMgr["INTERRUPT MANAGER\nISR entry exit\nLatency bounded"]

    taskCtrl --- scheduler
    scheduler --- dispatcher
    taskCtrl --- ipcMgr
    taskCtrl --- memMgr
    taskCtrl --- timeMgr
    intMgr -->|"Triggers rescheduling"| scheduler
    scheduler -->|"Selects next task"| dispatcher
    dispatcher -->|"Saves loads context"| taskCtrl
    ipcMgr -->|"Unblocks task"| taskCtrl
    timeMgr -->|"Tick expiry"| scheduler
```

---

## 4.3 Task State Transition Diagram

This state diagram illustrates the lifecycle of a task within the RTOS kernel.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew
    stateNew: NEW\nTask created
    stateNew --> stateReady: OSTaskCreate

    stateReady: READY\nIn ready queue
    stateReady --> stateRunning: Dispatched by scheduler
    stateRunning: RUNNING\nExecuting on CPU
    stateRunning --> stateReady: Preempted by higher priority
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked: Calls blocking API
    stateBlocked: BLOCKED\nWaiting for event
    stateBlocked --> stateReady: Event occurs timeout
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated: OSTaskDel
    stateTerminated: TERMINATED\nResources released
    stateTerminated --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (stateNew, stateReady, etc.) and reserved words (e.g., `end`) are avoided as standalone names. Labels are in raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.4 Kernel Type Comparison Flow

The following block diagram maps the four kernel architectures to their respective use cases and engineering examples.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["RTOS KERNEL ARCHITECTURES"]
    mono["MONOLITHIC KERNEL\nAll services in kernel space\nFastest IPC"]
    micro["MICROKERNEL\nMinimal kernel + user services\nHighest safety"]
    hybrid["HYBRID KERNEL\nMostly monolithic, modular interface\nBalanced"]
    exo["EXOKERNEL\nHardware multiplexing only\nResearch oriented"]

    root --> mono
    root --> micro
    root --> hybrid
    root --> exo

    mono --> exMono["Examples: uC OS II, FreeRTOS, VxWorks Classic"]
    micro --> exMicro["Examples: QNX Neutrino, L4, MINIX"]
    hybrid --> exHybrid["Examples: Windows NT, macOS XNU, modern VxWorks"]
    exo --> exExo["Examples: MIT Exokernel, Nemesis"]
```

---

## 4.5 Service Request Flow — From Application to Hardware

The following sequence-flow style block diagram traces what happens when an application task requests a kernel service (e.g., `OSSemPend`).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Step 1: Application calls OSSemPend"]
    A2["Step 2: API layer validates arguments"]
    A3["Step 3: Kernel checks semaphore counter"]
    A4{"Step 4: Semaphore available?"}
    A5["Step 5a: Decrement counter, return success"]
    A6["Step 5b: Block task, save TCB to wait list"]
    A7["Step 6: Scheduler picks next highest priority task"]
    A8["Step 7: Dispatcher performs context switch"]
    A9["Step 8: New task runs on CPU"]
    A10["Step 9: Later, ISR or task calls OSSemPost"]
    A11["Step 10: Kernel unblocks waiting task"]
    A12["Step 11: If unblocked task has higher priority, reschedule"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    A4 -->|Yes| A5 --> A9
    A4 -->|No| A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10 --> A11 --> A12
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part-A question carries **3 marks** as per KTU 2024 continuous + ESE assessment norms. Answer must be concise: **Definition (1 mark) + Explanation/Example (1.5 marks) + Diagram/API (0.5 mark)**.

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define an RTOS. Differentiate between hard and soft real-time systems with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)** is an operating system designed to handle real-time applications where correctness depends on both the logical result and the time within which the result is produced.

| Property | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Deadline** | Strictly enforced; missing it = system failure | Best-effort; missing it = degraded performance |
| **Example** | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Pacemaker | Video streaming, VoIP |

**(3 marks: 1 for RTOS definition + 2 for comparison table with examples.)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**What is a kernel? List any two differences between monolithic and microkernel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kernel** is the central component of an OS that manages system resources (CPU, memory, I/O) and provides services to application software via system calls.

**Differences:**

| Property | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **OS Services Location** | All in kernel space | Minimum in kernel; rest in user space |
| **Performance** | Faster (no message passing) | Slower (IPC overhead) |
| **Example** | µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS | QNX Neutrino, MINIX |

**(3 marks: 1 for kernel definition + 2 for differences.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU mandates an **internal choice** — students answer either **Question A or Question B** from the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of an RTOS**, labelling all the layers and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **basic services of an RTOS** in detail. List the APIs used for task management, IPC, and time management. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Structure of an RTOS — 7 marks**

The structure of an RTOS is organised into four logical layers, as shown in the block diagram below:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["APPLICATION LAYER\nUser tasks e.g. sensor reading, motor control"]
    L2["API LAYER\nFreeRTOS, uC OS II, POSIX APIs"]
    L3["KERNEL LAYER\nScheduler, IPC, Memory, Time, Interrupt managers"]
    L4["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER\nBSP, device drivers, CPU specific code"]
    L5["HARDWARE LAYER\nCPU, RAM, Flash, Timers, Peripherals"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

**Description (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Application Layer (1 mark):** Contains user-defined tasks like reading sensors, controlling actuators. Calls kernel APIs to request services.

2. **API / System Call Layer (1 mark):** Provides a uniform interface (e.g., `xTaskCreate`, `OSQCreate`) that hides kernel internals. Allows portability across hardware.

3. **Kernel Layer (3 marks):** The **heart** of the RTOS containing:
   - **Scheduler:** Decides which task runs next (priority-based, RMS, EDF).
   - **Dispatcher:** Performs context switching.
   - **IPC Manager:** Semaphores, mutexes, queues, mailboxes.
   - **Memory Manager:** Fixed-block allocation.
   - **Time Manager:** System tick, delays.
   - **Interrupt Manager:** ISR entry/exit handling.

4. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (BSP) (1 mark):** CPU-specific code, drivers, and interrupt vector table. Allows the kernel to be ported across processors by changing only the HAL.

5. **Hardware Layer (1 mark):** The physical processor, memory, timers, and peripherals.

---

**(b) Basic Services of an RTOS — 7 marks**

1. **Task Management (2 marks):** Creates, schedules, suspends, resumes, and deletes tasks. Each task is represented by a **Task Control Block (TCB)**.
   - APIs: `OSTaskCreate()`, `OSTaskSuspend()`, `OSTaskResume()`, `OSTaskDel()`.

2. **Memory Management (1.5 marks):** Provides deterministic memory allocation using fixed-size partitions to avoid fragmentation.
   - APIs: `OSMemCreate()`, `OSMemGet()`, `OSMemPut()`.

3. **Inter-Process Communication & Synchronization (2 marks):** Allows safe exchange of data and resource sharing between tasks.
   - **Semaphores:** `OSSemCreate()`, `OSSemPend()`, `OSSemPost()`.
   - **Mutexes:** `OSMutexCreate()`, `OSMutexPend()`.
   - **Message Queues:** `OSQCreate()`, `OSQPend()`, `OSQPost()`.
   - **Mailboxes:** `OSMboxCreate()`, `OSMboxPend()`.
   - **Event Flags:** `OSFlagCreate()`, `OSFlagPend()`.

4. **Time Management (1 mark):** Maintains a system tick; provides time delays, timeouts, and periodic wakeups.
   - APIs: `OSTimeDly()`, `OSTimeDlyHMSM()`, `OSTimeGet()`.

5. **Interrupt Handling (0.5 mark):** Coordinates with ISRs via `OSIntEnter()` and `OSIntExit()`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 1):** Students often confuse the **Task Control Block (TCB)** with the **task code/stack**. The TCB is a *data structure* maintained by the kernel; the stack is a *memory region* allocated to the task. The TCB contains a *pointer* to the stack, not the stack itself. This is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **three primary kernel architectures** (monolithic, microkernel, hybrid) with their advantages, disadvantages, and examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat state diagram, explain the **five states of an RTOS task** and the transitions between them. What is a context switch? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Kernel Architectures — 7 marks**

1. **Monolithic Kernel (2.5 marks):**
   - All OS services (scheduler, file system, drivers, network) reside in a single privileged kernel space.
   - **Advantages:** Highest performance (no IPC overhead between services), simple IPC.
   - **Disadvantages:** A bug in any service can crash the entire kernel; difficult to maintain and extend.
   - **Examples:** Traditional Unix, µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS, VxWorks (Classic).

2. **Microkernel (2.5 marks):**
   - Only **essential services** (scheduler, basic IPC, memory management) run in kernel mode. All other services (file system, drivers, network) run as **user-space processes**.
   - **Advantages:** Highly modular, fault-isolated (a driver crash does not crash the system), secure.
   - **Disadvantages:** Performance overhead due to **message passing** between user-space services.
   - **Examples:** QNX Neutrino, L4 microkernel family, MINIX 3, GNU Hurd.

3. **Hybrid Kernel (2 marks):**
   - A pragmatic blend — most services run in kernel mode (for speed) but the design is modular.
   - **Advantages:** Better performance than microkernel, better maintainability than monolithic.
   - **Disadvantages:** Still inherits some of the monolithic kernel's fault-isolation weakness.
   - **Examples:** Windows NT, macOS XNU, modern VxWorks, BeOS.

---

**(b) Task States & Context Switch — 7 marks**

**Five States of an RTOS Task (5 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sNew
    sNew: NEW
    sReady: READY
    sRun: RUNNING
    sBlock: BLOCKED
    sTerm: TERMINATED

    sNew --> sReady: OSTaskCreate
    sReady --> sRun: Dispatch
    sRun --> sReady: Preempt
    sRun --> sBlock: OSSemPend (no signal)
    sBlock --> sReady: Event occurs
    sRun --> sTerm: OSTaskDel
    sTerm --> [*]
```

**State Descriptions (5 marks breakdown):**

1. **NEW (0.5 mark):** Task is being created. TCB is initialised, stack is allocated, but the task is not yet in the ready queue.

2. **READY (1 mark):** Task is ready to run but is waiting for the CPU because a higher-priority task is currently running.

3. **RUNNING (1 mark):** Task is currently executing on the CPU. Only **one task per CPU core** can be in this state at any instant.

4. **BLOCKED (1.5 marks):** Task is waiting for an event (semaphore, message, time delay). It is removed from the ready queue and placed in a wait list.

5. **TERMINATED (1 mark):** Task has finished or has been deleted. Its TCB and stack are released back to the system.

**Context Switch (2 marks):**

A **context switch** is the procedure of saving the state (context) of the currently running task and loading the state of the next task to be executed. The context includes:

- Program Counter (PC)
- Stack Pointer (SP)
- General-purpose registers (R0–R12 in ARM)
- Status registers (CPSR / APSR)

**Steps of a context switch:**
1. Save outgoing task's context to its TCB.
2. Update outgoing task's state to READY or BLOCKED.
3. Scheduler selects the highest-priority READY task.
4. Load incoming task's context from its TCB.
5. Update incoming task's state to RUNNING.
6. Resume execution at the loaded PC.

**Context switch time** is a critical RTOS metric:

$$T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 2):** In 14-mark questions on kernel architecture, students often forget to mention **real-world examples** for each kernel type. The KTU valuation key explicitly allocates marks for examples (typically 0.5 to 1 mark per type). Always include at least one example (e.g., QNX for microkernel, FreeRTOS for monolithic).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-know for KTU ESE & Internal Assessments:**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- ✅ **RTOS:** OS with deterministic, time-bounded response to events.
- ✅ **Hard vs. Soft vs. Firm Real-Time:** Strict deadline (catastrophic) vs. degraded performance vs. result discarded.
- ✅ **Kernel:** Core of OS that manages resources; runs in privileged mode.
- ✅ **TCB (Task Control Block):** Data structure holding task state — PC, SP, registers, priority, state.

### 🏗️ RTOS Structure
- ✅ Four layers: **Application → API → Kernel → HAL → Hardware**.
- ✅ Kernel is the **heart**; HAL enables portability.

### 🧩 Kernel Architectures
- ✅ **Monolithic:** All in kernel; fastest, less safe (e.g., µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS).
- ✅ **Microkernel:** Minimum in kernel, services in user space; safest, slower (e.g., QNX, L4).
- ✅ **Hybrid:** Mix of both; balanced (e.g., Windows NT, VxWorks).

### 🛠️ OS Services (must remember APIs)
- ✅ **Task Management:** `OSTaskCreate`, `OSTaskSuspend`, `OSTaskResume`, `OSTaskDel`.
- ✅ **IPC:** Semaphores, Mutexes, Message Queues, Mailboxes, Event Flags.
- ✅ **Time Management:** `OSTimeDly`, `OSTimeDlyHMSM`, `OSTimeGet`, System Tick.
- ✅ **Memory:** Fixed-block allocator (`OSMemCreate`, `OSMemGet`, `OSMemPut`).
- ✅ **Interrupt:** `OSIntEnter`, `OSIntExit`.

### 🔁 Task States (5 states — draw the diagram in exam)
- ✅ **NEW → READY → RUNNING → BLOCKED → READY → ... → TERMINATED.**

### 📐 Key Formulas
- ✅ $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tick}}$ — System tick period.
- ✅ $U = \sum \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ — CPU utilization; must be **< 1** for hard real-time.
- ✅ Liu & Layland bound: $U \le n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ for RMS schedulability.
- ✅ $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ — Context switch time.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Calling **blocking APIs inside ISRs** (use non-blocking variants).
- ❌ Confusing **TCB** (data structure) with the **task stack** (memory region).
- ❌ Using `malloc()`/`free()` in RTOS (non-deterministic — use fixed-block allocator).
- ❌ Assuming RTOS = GPOS (GPOS maximises throughput; RTOS guarantees deadlines).
- ❌ Forgetting to mention kernel examples in 14-mark questions.

### 🎯 Frequently Asked KTU Question Patterns
- ✅ "Differentiate between monolithic and microkernel." *(7 marks)*
- ✅ "Draw and explain the RTOS task state diagram." *(7 marks)*
- ✅ "Explain the basic OS services of an RTOS with examples." *(7 or 14 marks)*
- ✅ "Define kernel. Explain its types." *(3 or 7 marks)*
- ✅ "Compare RTOS and GPOS." *(3 or 7 marks)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process, Tasks and Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process, Tasks and Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — PROCESS, TASKS AND THREADS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Process — Formal Definition

A **Process** is defined as an *independent program in execution* along with its associated **execution context** — program counter, CPU registers, stack pointer, memory address space (code, data, heap), open file descriptors, and operating system metadata stored in a **Process Control Block (PCB)**.

In a General Purpose Operating System (GPOS) like Linux or Windows, every process is **isolated** and has its own **virtual address space**, protected by the Memory Management Unit (MMU). A process is therefore classified as a **heavyweight** execution unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In embedded systems using an RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTLinux), the strict textbook distinction between *process* and *task* collapses. Most RTOSs implement everything as **tasks** because full process isolation (via MMU) is too memory-expensive for resource-constrained MCUs like ARM Cortex-M. **For KTU exams, always clarify the OS context (GPOS vs RTOS) before writing definitions.**

**PCB (Process Control Block) — Mandatory Fields for KTU:**

| Field Group | Contents |
|-------------|----------|
| Identification | Process ID (PID), Parent PID, User ID |
| CPU State | Program Counter, Register File, Stack Pointer |
| Memory Mgmt | Page tables base, Segment registers |
| Scheduling | Priority, State, Quantum, Policy |
| I/O & Files | Open file descriptors, Device handles |
| IPC | Signal masks, Message queue pointers |

---

### 1.2 Task — Formal Definition

A **Task** is the fundamental unit of execution in a Real-Time Operating System. It is a *lightweight, single-threaded entity* that owns a dedicated **Task Control Block (TCB)**, a fixed-size stack, and a piece of code. Multiple tasks within a single RTOS instance **share a common address space** (no MMU protection in most cases).

A task is also referred to as a *lightweight process (LWP)* in academic literature when discussing UNIX-style systems.

---

### 1.3 Thread — Formal Definition

A **Thread** is the *smallest schedulable unit of execution* within a process. A thread possesses its own:
- Thread ID
- Program Counter
- Register set
- Stack memory

…but it **shares** the following resources with all peer threads of the same process:
- Heap memory
- Global/static variables
- File descriptors
- Code segment (text segment)

A process containing **N** threads is said to have **N threads of execution**.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a **Restaurant** as a *Process*:
> - The **kitchen, storage room, and dining area** are *shared resources* (heap, globals, files).
> - Each **Chef** working in the kitchen is a *Thread* — they have their own cutting board, knives, and apron (register set, stack, PC) but use the same pantry and stoves.
> - If the restaurant hires a *catering agency* that handles multiple events, each **catering event** is a separate *Task* in an RTOS — it has its own team and supplies but reports to the same manager (the OS kernel).
>
> **Key takeaway:** A *process* is the whole restaurant, a *thread* is an individual chef, and a *task* in RTOS is like an independent work order with its own resources.

---

### 1.5 Process vs Task vs Thread — Comparison Matrix

| Attribute | Process | Task (RTOS) | Thread |
|-----------|---------|-------------|--------|
| Weight | Heavyweight | Lightweight | Lightest |
| Address Space | Isolated, virtual | Shared with all tasks | Shared with peer threads |
| Protection | MMU-based | None (trust-based) | None |
| Context Switch Cost | **High** (~µs to ms) | **Low** (~µs) | **Lowest** |
| Stack | Independent, large | Independent, fixed-size | Independent, small |
| Heap / Globals | Private | Shared | Shared |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets) | Message queues, semaphores | Shared variables, mutexes |
| Creation Cost | High (fork/exec) | Low (`xTaskCreate`) | Lowest (`pthread_create`) |
| Embedded Suitability | Poor (RAM-hungry) | Excellent | Good (used in Linux-embedded) |
| Typical Stack Size | 1–8 MB | 256 B – 4 KB | 256 B – 1 MB |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task Life Cycle — State Machine Analysis

In a preemptive RTOS, every task cycles through a finite set of states defined by the kernel scheduler. The canonical set of states (per the KTU 2024 syllabus) is:

1. **Running** — Currently executing on the CPU. Only ONE task can be in this state on a single-core system.
2. **Ready** — Eligible to run but preempted or never yet executed. Sits in the **Ready Queue**.
3. **Blocked / Waiting** — Awaiting an event (semaphore give, queue receive, delay expiry, I/O completion). *Not* in the ready queue.
4. **Suspended (Dormant)** — Explicitly suspended by the OS API; will not be scheduled until explicitly resumed (FreeRTOS `vTaskSuspend`).
5. **Deleted / Terminated** — Execution finished; TCB marked for cleanup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Suspended" state matters in KTU exams:**
> Many students confuse *Blocked* with *Suspended*. A **Blocked** task is waiting for a *kernel event* (semaphore, queue, delay) and will be automatically unblocked when the event occurs. A **Suspended** task is taken off the ready queue *manually* by API and will not resume automatically.

---

## 2.2 Transitions and Trigger Events

| From State | To State | Trigger Event |
|------------|----------|---------------|
| Ready | Running | Scheduler dispatch (tick interrupt) |
| Running | Ready | Preemption (higher-priority task ready) or time-slice expiry |
| Running | Blocked | Task calls `vTaskDelay()`, waits on semaphore/queue |
| Blocked | Ready | Blocking event is signaled |
| Ready / Running | Suspended | API call `vTaskSuspend()` |
| Suspended | Ready | API call `vTaskResume()` |
| Running | Deleted | Task function returns or `vTaskDelete()` |

---

## 2.3 Context Switching — The Core Mechanism

A **Context Switch** is the procedure of saving the state (context) of the currently running task and loading the state of the next scheduled task. The cost is critical in real-time systems because it directly impacts **schedulability**.

### 2.3.1 Context Switch Steps
1. Tick interrupt (or syscall) fires.
2. CPU saves registers of current task onto its stack (hardware push).
3. Kernel copies stack pointer into the TCB of outgoing task.
4. Scheduler selects next task from Ready Queue (highest priority + ready).
5. Kernel loads stack pointer of incoming task from its TCB.
6. CPU pops registers of incoming task (hardware pop).
7. Execution resumes in new task at saved PC.

### 2.3.2 Context Switch Time Formula

$$
T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{schedule} + T_{load}
$$

Where:
- $T_{save}$ = Time to save outgoing task's context (architecture-dependent, often **10–100 clock cycles**).
- $T_{schedule}$ = Time for the scheduler algorithm to pick the next task.
- $T_{load}$ = Time to restore incoming task's context.

The **effective CPU utilization** lost to context switching across $N$ tasks with average context-switch time $T_{cs}$ and total task switches per second $S$ is:

$$
U_{overhead} = S \times T_{cs}
$$

---

## 2.4 Scheduling Algorithms — KTU High-Yield Analysis

### 2.4.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)
- **Type:** Static priority, preemptive.
- **Rule:** Shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority.
- **Optimality:** Optimal among *static-priority* algorithms.

**Liu & Layland Schedulability Bound** (for $N$ independent, periodic tasks):

$$
U_{bound} = N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)
$$

| $N$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | $\infty$ |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|----------|
| $U_{bound}$ | 1.000 | 0.828 | 0.780 | 0.757 | 0.743 | $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |

If total utilization $U \le U_{bound}$, the task set is **guaranteed schedulable**. If $U > U_{bound}$, it is *inconclusive* (use response-time analysis).

### 2.4.2 Earliest Deadline First (EDF)
- **Type:** Dynamic priority, preemptive.
- **Rule:** Task with the earliest absolute deadline gets the CPU.
- **Optimality:** Optimal among *all* scheduling algorithms.
- **Schedulability bound:** $U \le 1.0$ for independent periodic tasks.

### 2.4.3 Priority Inversion Problem
Occurs when a *high-priority* task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a *low-priority* task, while a *medium-priority* task preempts the low-priority task and runs unboundedly. **Solution:** **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — the low-priority task temporarily inherits the priority of the highest-priority waiter.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | CPU utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = execution time, $T_i$ = period |
| 2 | RMS schedulability bound | $U_{bound} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ | Sufficient, not necessary |
| 3 | EDF feasibility | $U \le 1.0$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| 4 | Context switch cost | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{sched} + T_{load}$ | Measured in **µs** typical |
| 5 | Overhead fraction | $U_{oh} = \dfrac{S \cdot T_{cs}}{T_{hyperperiod}}$ | $S$ = switches per hyperperiod |
| 6 | Hyperperiod | $T_{HP} = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_N)$ | Major cycle of task set |
| 7 | Response time recurrence | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterates until convergence |
| 8 | Stack size estimation | $Stack_{min} = C_{ISR} + C_{nested} + C_{local}$ | Worst-case nested call depth |
| 9 | Task jitter bound | $J_i \le T_i - C_i$ | For RMS schedulable sets |
| 10 | Priority inversion max wait | $W_{max} = \sum_{j: \text{medium}} C_j$ | Without PIP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** RMS and EDF schedulability analysis is used daily in **automotive ECU firmware (AUTOSAR)**, **aerospace flight control (DO-178C)**, and **medical device firmware (IEC 62304)**. Missing a deadline in any of these can be life-threatening. The formulas above are the *exact* gates used during certification.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland RMS Schedulability Bound

This is a **classic KTU derivation question** worth remembering. The bound states:

$$
U_{bound}(N) = N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)
$$

### Step 1 — Define the Critical Instant

A *critical instant* for a task $\tau_i$ occurs when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously** with $\tau_i$. The longest response time of $\tau_i$ is observed at this instant.

### Step 2 — Write the Worst-Case Response Time

At a critical instant, the demand on the CPU by higher-priority tasks during the interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$
W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

Where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with higher priority than $\tau_i$. The task is schedulable iff $\exists t \in (0, T_i]$ such that $W_i(t) \le t$.

### Step 3 — Consider the Worst-Case Task Set

Liu & Layland showed that the *worst-case* utilization bound occurs for task sets where each task's computation time is just below the bound: $C_i = T_i \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)$ for $i = 1, \dots, N$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Bound

Sum the utilizations:

$$
U_{bound} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right) = N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Limit as $N \to \infty$

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right) = \lim_{N \to \infty} N \left( e^{\ln 2 / N} - 1 \right)
$$

Apply the limit identity $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{e^x - 1}{x} = 1$ with $x = \dfrac{\ln 2}{N}$:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} N \cdot \frac{\ln 2}{N} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

**Result:** As the number of tasks grows, RMS cannot guarantee schedulability above ~69.3% utilization.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Three Periodic Tasks under RMS

> **Sample task set used for KTU numerical problems:**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Exec Time $C_i$ (ms) | Utilization $u_i$ | Priority (RMS) |
|------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 5 | 1 | 0.200 | Highest (1) |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 2 | 0.200 | Middle (2) |
| $\tau_3$ | 20 | 4 | 0.200 | Lowest (3) |

### Step 1 — Compute Total Utilization

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{20}
$$

$$
U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.200 = 0.600
$$

### Step 2 — Check Against Liu & Layland Bound for $N = 3$

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
2^{1/3} = 1.2599
$$

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

### Step 3 — Compare

Since $U = 0.600 \le U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798$, the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS.

### Step 4 — Compute Response Time of $\tau_3$ (lowest priority, for deeper analysis)

Use the recurrence $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ with $R^{(0)} = C_3 = 4$.

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil (2) = 4 + 1 + 2 = 7
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil (2) = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil (2) = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8
$$

**Converged at $R_3 = 8$ ms.** Since $R_3 = 8 \le T_3 = 20$ ms, $\tau_3$ meets its deadline. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Thread vs Process Creation Cost (Linux)

> **Comparison useful for KTU conceptual/short questions:**

| Metric | `fork()` (Process) | `pthread_create()` (Thread) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Memory copied | Entire address space (Copy-on-Write) | Only stack allocation (~2 MB default) |
| Kernel overhead | High (new PCB, copy page tables) | Low (shared address space) |
| Typical latency | **~200 µs – 1 ms** | **~20 – 50 µs** |
| IPC needed | Yes (pipes, sockets, shm) | No (shared globals) |
| Failure isolation | Yes (process crash ≠ system crash) | No (one thread crash kills process) |
| RTOS usage | Rare (RAM expensive) | Common in Linux-embedded (Xenomai, PREEMPT_RT) |

**Embedded insight:** For an MCU with 256 KB RAM, you cannot afford 100 threads × 2 MB stack. This is why RTOSes use **cooperative memory budgeting** with statically allocated TCBs and fixed-size stacks.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram (Preemptive RTOS)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready : Task Created
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by Higher Priority
    Running --> Ready : Time Slice Expired
    Running --> Blocked : Wait Event (Sem/Queue/Delay)
    Blocked --> Ready : Event Signaled
    Running --> Suspended : vTaskSuspend Called
    Ready --> Suspended : vTaskSuspend Called
    Suspended --> Ready : vTaskResume Called
    Running --> Deleted : Task Function Returns
    Deleted --> [*] : TCB Cleaned Up
```

## 4.2 Process vs Thread Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph P1["PROCESS 1 (Independent Address Space)"]
        subgraph TCB1["Thread 1"]
            PC1[Program Counter]
            REG1[Registers]
            STK1[Stack 1 - 1 MB]
        end
        subgraph TCB2["Thread 2"]
            PC2[Program Counter]
            REG2[Registers]
            STK2[Stack 2 - 1 MB]
        end
        H1[Shared Heap]
        G1[Global / Static Data]
        C1[Code Segment]
        FD1[File Descriptors]
    end

    subgraph P2["PROCESS 2 (Separate Address Space)"]
        subgraph TCB3["Thread 1"]
            PC3[Program Counter]
            REG3[Registers]
            STK3[Stack 1 - 1 MB]
        end
        H2[Heap]
        G2[Globals]
        C2[Code]
        FD2[FDs]
    end

    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style P2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style TCB1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style TCB2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style TCB3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

## 4.3 RTOS Task Scheduling Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP1[Application Task 1 - High Priority] --> SCHED[Kernel Scheduler]
    APP2[Application Task 2 - Med Priority] --> SCHED
    APP3[Application Task 3 - Low Priority] --> SCHED
    ISRHW[Hardware Interrupt Handler] --> SCHED
    SCHED --> CPU[CPU Core]
    SCHED -.-> RQ[(Ready Queue)]
    SCHED -.-> DLQ[(Delay List)]
    SCHED -.-> SEM[Semaphores / Queues]
    CPU -.context.-> SCHED

    style SCHED fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17
    style CPU fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b
    style RQ fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
    style DLQ fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
    style SEM fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
```

## 4.4 Context Switch Flow Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant T1 as Task A (Running)
    participant KERN as Kernel Scheduler
    participant T2 as Task B (Ready)

    CPU->>T1: Execute Instructions
    Note over CPU,T1: Tick Interrupt Fires
    CPU->>CPU: Save T1 Registers to T1 Stack
    CPU->>KERN: Trap to Kernel (Syscall/IRQ)
    KERN->>KERN: Save T1 SP to T1 TCB
    KERN->>KERN: Run Scheduling Algorithm
    KERN->>KERN: Pick Highest Priority Ready Task = B
    KERN->>CPU: Load T2 SP from T2 TCB
    CPU->>CPU: Restore T2 Registers from T2 Stack
    CPU->>T2: Resume Execution in Task B
```

## 4.5 Multi-Tasking Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    SENSOR1[Sensor Acquisition Task] --> SHARED[Shared Resource - Display Buffer]
    SENSOR2[Communication Task] --> SHARED
    CONTROL[Motor Control Task] --> SHARED
    SHARED --> MUTEX[Mutex - Resource Lock]
    MUTEX --> DISPLAY[LCD / UART Output]

    style SENSOR1 fill:#a5d6a7
    style SENSOR2 fill:#fff59d
    style CONTROL fill:#ef9a9a
    style SHARED fill:#ce93d8
    style MUTEX fill:#ff8a65
    style DISPLAY fill:#90caf9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a process and a thread with respect to memory allocation and context switch overhead.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Address Space | **Isolated, virtual, MMU-protected** | **Shared with peer threads** |
| Memory Cost | Heavy (full address space) | Light (only stack + TLS) |
| Context Switch | **High** (TLB flush, page table switch) | **Low** (shared address space) |
| Communication | Requires IPC mechanisms | Direct global variable access |
| Crash Isolation | Independent | One bad thread kills whole process |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for memory, 1 for context switch comparison.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the difference between "Blocked" and "Suspended" states of an RTOS task.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Blocked** task is waiting for a *kernel-managed event* such as semaphore give, queue receive, or delay expiry. The kernel will automatically move it back to the **Ready** state when the event occurs. In contrast, a **Suspended** task has been *manually* removed from scheduling via an API call (e.g., `vTaskSuspend()` in FreeRTOS) and will not resume automatically — an explicit `vTaskResume()` is mandatory.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for Blocked, 1.5 for Suspended.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the various states of a task in a preemptive RTOS. Discuss the role of the Task Control Block (TCB) in context switching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 4 | 1 |
| $\tau_2$ | 8 | 2 |
| $\tau_3$ | 16 | 4 |

Using **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, determine:
(i) The priority assignment.
(ii) Total CPU utilization.
(iii) Whether the task set is schedulable using the Liu & Layland bound. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Task States and TCB**

**Task States** (with diagram):

The states a task can be in are:
1. **Running** — Currently executing on the CPU.
2. **Ready** — Eligible for execution, sitting in the ready queue.
3. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Waiting for an event (semaphore, queue, delay).
4. **Suspended (Dormant)** — Manually taken out of scheduling.
5. **Deleted** — Terminated, TCB to be cleaned.

Transitions occur on: scheduler dispatch, preemption, event signaling, API calls.

**Task Control Block (TCB) Role:**
The TCB is a kernel data structure storing all state information for a task:
- Stack pointer
- Program counter
- Register snapshot
- Task priority
- Task state
- List pointers (ready/blocked queues)

**During context switching**, the kernel:
1. Saves the outgoing task's CPU registers onto its stack.
2. Copies the stack pointer into the outgoing TCB.
3. Selects the next task.
4. Loads the new stack pointer from the incoming TCB.
5. Pops the incoming task's registers back into the CPU.

This mechanism makes context switch **deterministic and fast** — a key requirement in real-time systems.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram and state explanation, 2 for TCB structure, 2 for context switch role.)*

##### **Part (b) — RMS Schedulability Analysis**

**(i) Priority Assignment (RMS rule: shorter period → higher priority):**

| Task | Period (ms) | RMS Priority |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 4 | **Highest (P1)** |
| $\tau_2$ | 8 | **Middle (P2)** |
| $\tau_3$ | 16 | **Lowest (P3)** |

**(ii) Total CPU Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{8} + \frac{4}{16}
$$

$$
U = 0.250 + 0.250 + 0.250 = 0.750
$$

**(iii) Liu & Layland Bound for $N = 3$:**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Comparison:** $U = 0.750 \le U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798$

✅ **The task set IS SCHEDULABLE** under RMS by the Liu & Layland sufficient condition.

*(7 marks — 2 for priority, 2 for utilization, 3 for bound comparison.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Priority Inversion Problem** in detail. With a timeline diagram, show how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** and **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** algorithms across eight parameters. For the same task set from Question A, re-evaluate schedulability under EDF. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Priority Inversion & PIP**

**Problem Description:**
Priority inversion occurs in priority-preemptive systems when a *high-priority* task (H) is forced to wait for a *low-priority* task (L) to release a shared resource (mutex), while one or more *medium-priority* tasks (M) preempt L and run to completion. The net effect is that **H waits for M's execution time + L's critical section**, even though H has higher priority.

**Unbounded Inversion Example (Mariner 1 / Mars Pathfinder incidents):**
- L acquires mutex R.
- H preempts L, tries to lock R → blocks.
- M preempts L, runs to completion (unboundedly).
- L finally finishes, releases R.
- H unblocks — but only after M has finished.

**Timeline:**
```
T=0   L starts, locks R
T=2   H arrives, blocks on R
T=3   M preempts L, runs (e.g., 5 ms)
T=8   M finishes, L resumes
T=10  L releases R
T=10  H finally runs
```

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Solution:**
When H blocks on R held by L, the kernel **temporarily boosts L's priority to that of H**. Now M cannot preempt L, because L is effectively running at H's priority. L finishes its critical section quickly and releases R. L's priority then reverts to its original value.

**PIP Timeline:**
```
T=0   L starts, locks R
T=2   H arrives, blocks → L's priority boosted to H's
T=3   M tries to preempt L → BLOCKED (L is now higher priority)
T=8   L releases R
T=8   H unblocks and runs immediately
T=10  M finally gets to run
```

*(7 marks — 2 for problem, 2 for diagram, 3 for PIP mechanism.)*

##### **Part (b) — RMS vs EDF + EDF Re-evaluation**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RMS | EDF |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Priority Type | Static | Dynamic |
| Assignment | By period | By absolute deadline |
| Optimality | Optimal among static | Optimal overall |
| Schedulability Bound | $N(2^{1/N}-1)$ | $U \le 1.0$ |
| Implementation | Simple (fixed table) | Complex (recompute at each tick) |
| Runtime Overhead | Low | Higher |
| Transient Behavior | Predictable | May thrash on overload |
| Certification (DO-178C) | Preferred | Allowed but complex |

**EDF Re-evaluation for the same task set:**

For EDF, the sufficient and necessary condition is:

$$
U \le 1.0
$$

From part (a)(ii), $U = 0.750 \le 1.0$.

✅ **Task set is schedulable under EDF** (with ~25% slack CPU time remaining).

*(7 marks — 4 for comparison table, 1 for EDF condition, 2 for re-evaluation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (always write ms / µs).
> 2. **Confusing Blocked vs Suspended** — this is a 3-mark trap question almost every semester.
> 3. **Misapplying RMS bound** — the bound is a *sufficient*, not necessary condition. A set with $U > 0.78$ may still be schedulable; you must use Response-Time Analysis in that case.
> 4. **Priority Inversion diagrams** — examiners want a **timeline**, not a flowchart. Always show time on the X-axis.
> 5. **Context switch cost** — students often write $T_{cs}$ as just one number; mention *save + schedule + load* explicitly for full marks.
> 6. **PCB vs TCB** — In GPOS it's PCB; in RTOS it's TCB. Examiners penalize the wrong term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Process** = Heavyweight, isolated address space, MMU-protected, high context-switch cost.
- ✅ **Task (RTOS)** = Lightweight, shared address space, fixed stack, TCB-based.
- ✅ **Thread** = Lightest, shares heap/globals with peer threads, independent stack.
- ✅ **Five task states**: Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted — know each transition trigger.
- ✅ **Context switch** = Save → Schedule → Load; cost = $T_{save} + T_{sched} + T_{load}$.
- ✅ **RMS bound** = $N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ — sufficient, not necessary; approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ✅ **EDF bound** = $U \le 1.0$ — necessary and sufficient for independent periodic tasks.
- ✅ **Priority Inversion** solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.
- ✅ **Hyperperiod** = LCM of all task periods; used to analyze major cycles.
- ✅ **Response Time Analysis** recurrence: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \rceil C_j$.
- ✅ For KTU: Always specify **RTOS context** (FreeRTOS / VxWorks) when defining task/thread.
- ✅ Embedded RTOSes use **statically allocated TCBs** to avoid heap fragmentation.
- ✅ Stack size = Worst-case nested call depth × bytes per frame + ISR overhead.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Management - Memory Management - Device Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Management - Memory Management - Device Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 — Process, Memory & Device Management in RTOS-based Embedded Systems)**
> In an Embedded System running an **RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)**, the system software is divided into three cooperative subsystems: **Process (Task) Management** (decides *what runs, when, and for how long*), **Memory Management** (decides *where code and data live in RAM/ROM*), and **Device (I/O) Management** (decides *how the CPU talks to peripherals*). Together, they form the **Kernel Services Layer** of any RTOS such as **FreeRTOS, VxWorks, μC/OS-II, or RTEMS**.

## 1.1 Process (Task) Management
A **Process** (more accurately, a **Task** in RTOS terminology) is an independent, sequential program in execution that owns its own stack, register context, and priority. Process Management is the kernel subsystem responsible for **creating, scheduling, dispatching, suspending, resuming, terminating, and inter-communicating** these tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** In a *desktop* OS, a "process" owns its own private address space. In a typical *RTOS*, a "task" shares a single flat address space with all other tasks. This is one of the most tested distinctions in KTU exams.

## 1.2 Memory Management
**Memory Management** is the kernel subsystem that tracks which regions of **ROM, RAM, Flash, EEPROM, and external memory** are currently in use, allocates them safely to tasks, and (optionally) provides **virtual addressing, memory protection, and garbage collection** services.

## 1.3 Device (I/O) Management
**Device Management** abstracts physical peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, timers, GPIO) into a uniform software interface through **Device Drivers**, and provides **I/O scheduling, buffering (queues), DMA coordination, and interrupt handling**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Kitchen"
Imagine a **professional restaurant kitchen** as an embedded system:

| Kitchen Element | Embedded System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Head Chef deciding which dish is cooked next | **Process Scheduler** |
| Recipe cards, prep stations, fridge shelves | **Memory Manager (RAM/ROM/Stack/Heap)** |
| Ovens, mixers, gas burners (physical tools) | **Peripheral Devices (UART, ADC, GPIO)** |
| Waiter carrying orders between chef and tools | **Device Drivers & Interrupt Service Routines** |
| The head chef's clipboard tracking every order | **Process Control Block (PCB / TCB)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every Module 3 question links *all three* subsystems. For example: "How does a UART interrupt trigger a context switch that schedules a higher-priority task which then uses dynamic memory?" — examiners test this integrated understanding.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics You Must Know
- **Jitter (μs or ms):** Variation in task release time. **Hard real-time:** Jitter **= 0** ideally.
- **Latency (μs):** Time from event occurrence to handler start.
- **Throughput (tasks/sec or MB/s):** Work units completed per unit time.
- **Context Switch Time:** Typically **1–10 μs** in modern RTOS.
- **Memory Footprint (KB):** Total RAM + ROM consumed by the kernel. **FreeRTOS ≈ 4–10 KB**, **μC/OS-II ≈ 6–24 KB**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Management — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Task States and Transitions
A task in an RTOS is modeled as a **Finite State Machine** with five canonical states defined by the IEEE 1003.1 / POSIX real-time profile and adopted by μC/OS-II:

1. **Dormant (Created but not yet activated)** — Task Control Block (TCB) exists in the ready list, but execution has not started.
2. **Ready** — All resources available; waiting only for the CPU.
3. **Running** — Currently executing on the CPU. *Only one task per core can be in this state.*
4. **Waiting / Blocked** — Waiting for an event (semaphore, queue, delay, signal).
5. **Suspended (Optional)** — Explicitly put to sleep by another task; no scheduler activity.

**State Transition Triggers:**
- `Ready → Running` when the **scheduler dispatches** the task (preemptive or cooperative).
- `Running → Ready` when a **higher-priority task becomes ready** (preemption).
- `Running → Blocked` on `pend()`, `delay()`, or semaphore wait.
- `Blocked → Ready` on event occurrence (ISR posts semaphore, delay expires).

### 2.1.2 Task Control Block (TCB)
The TCB is the kernel's "identity card" for every task. It stores:
- **Stack Pointer (SP)**
- **Program Counter (PC)** — saved during context switch
- **CPU Registers** (general purpose + status)
- **Task Priority**
- **Task State**
- **Stack Base & Stack Size**
- **Pointer to message queue / semaphore**
- **Task ID / Name**

### 2.1.3 Scheduling Algorithms
| Algorithm | KTU Exam Definition | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** | Static priority = inverse of period. *Optimal fixed-priority* algorithm. | Hard real-time |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic priority = earliest deadline wins. *Optimal dynamic-priority* algorithm. | Hard real-time |
| **Round Robin (RR)** | Equal time quantum per task. | Time-sharing |
| **Priority-based Preemptive** | Highest-priority ready task runs immediately. | μC/OS-II, FreeRTOS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Theorem — Liu & Layland (1973):**
> For *n* independent, periodic tasks scheduled under **RMS** on a single processor:
> $$\text{Schedulability Condition: } U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$
> For *n → ∞*, the bound converges to **ln(2) ≈ 0.693**. EDF has a schedulability bound of **U ≤ 1.0**.

### 2.1.4 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Mechanisms
- **Semaphores** (binary, counting, mutex with priority inheritance)
- **Message Queues**
- **Mailboxes**
- **Event Flags**
- **Pipes / Shared Memory**
- **Mutexes with Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — prevents *priority inversion*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Unbounded Priority Inversion** is a classic KTU short-answer question. The **Mars Pathfinder** (1997) bug was caused by exactly this. Solution: **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.

---

## 2.2 Memory Management — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Memory Regions in an Embedded System
| Region | Volatility | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **ROM / Flash** | Non-volatile | Program code, constants, lookup tables |
| **RAM (SRAM/DRAM)** | Volatile | Stack, heap, global/static variables |
| **EEPROM** | Non-volatile (byte-erasable) | Configuration, calibration data |
| **External Memory (DDR, NAND)** | Volatile / Non-volatile | Large buffers, file systems |

### 2.2.2 Memory Allocation Strategies

**A. Static Allocation (Deterministic — Preferred in Hard RTOS)**
- All memory sized at **compile time**.
- Zero fragmentation, **O(1)** allocation, **WCET = constant**.
- Drawback: Inflexible; cannot grow dynamically.

**B. Dynamic Allocation (Flexible)**
- **Fixed-Size Block Allocator** — Pool of equal blocks. O(1), no internal fragmentation, possible external fragmentation.
- **Variable-Size Allocator (malloc/free)** — First-fit / Best-fit / Buddy system. Possible fragmentation.
- **Slab Allocator** — Object caches; used in Linux but heavy for RTOS.

**C. Memory Protection Unit (MPU) / Memory Management Unit (MMU)**
- **MPU:** Hardware unit in Cortex-M3/M4/M7; provides 8–16 **memory regions** with attributes (read/write/execute, privileged/user).
- **MMU:** Used in Cortex-A, full **virtual-to-physical translation** with page tables. Required for Linux/Android.

### 2.2.3 Stack Overflow Detection
Critical in embedded systems. Techniques:
- **Stack Painting** (fill stack with 0xAA...AA at init; check before use).
- **MPU Guard Page** (mark region just below stack as inaccessible; fault on overflow).
- **Stack High-Water Mark** (track minimum free stack during runtime — supported by FreeRTOS via `uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark`).

---

## 2.3 Device Management — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Device Driver Architecture
A device driver is a software module that:
1. **Initializes** the peripheral hardware.
2. **Provides Open/Close/Read/Write/IOCTL** interface to upper layers.
3. **Handles Interrupts** from the device.
4. **Manages Buffers / DMA descriptors**.

### 2.3.2 I/O Synchronization Mechanisms

| Mechanism | CPU Overhead | Latency | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polling (Busy-Wait)** | 100% (CPU-bound) | **Lowest, deterministic** | Single-task simple MCUs |
| **Interrupt-Driven I/O** | Minimal (only on event) | Very low | Most embedded systems |
| **DMA (Direct Memory Access)** | Near-zero (hardware moves data) | Lowest for bulk transfer | High-speed peripherals (UART, SPI, ADC) |

### 2.3.3 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) Rules
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rules for ISRs:**
> 1. **Keep ISRs short** — defer work to a **Deferred Procedure Call (DPC)** or task via semaphore.
> 2. **Never block** in an ISR (no `pend()`, no `malloc()`).
> 3. **Save context** (typically done in hardware automatically).
> 4. **Acknowledge / clear** the interrupt source before returning.
> 5. **Use `FromISR()`** variants of API in FreeRTOS (e.g., `xQueueSendFromISR`).

### 2.3.4 Device Management Data Structures
- **Device Table** — Array of device descriptors.
- **I/O Request Queue (IRQ)** — Per-device pending requests.
- **Buffer Descriptors (BD)** — Used by Ethernet / DMA controllers.
- **Character Device vs Block Device** distinction.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every Module 3 numerical.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | **CPU Utilization** (fraction of time spent executing) | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $U_{RMS}^{\max} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | **Liu–Layland RMS bound** | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $U_{EDF}^{\max} = 1.0$ | **EDF schedulability bound** | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** | Time units (μs/ms) |
| 5 | $T_{context} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | **Context switch time** = register save + restore | μs |
| 6 | $T_{latency} = T_{detect} + T_{save} + T_{ISR} + T_{schedule}$ | **Interrupt latency** | μs |
| 7 | $Jitter = T_{max} - T_{min}$ | **Release time jitter** | μs |
| 8 | $T_{DMA\_throughput} = \frac{L_{bytes}}{f_{bus} \cdot w_{bus}}$ | **DMA transfer time** | seconds |
| 9 | $M_{stack\_min} = M_{base} - M_{used\_peak}$ | **Stack high-water mark** | Bytes |
| 10 | $\rho = \frac{1}{T_{period}} \sum C_i$ | **Processor load factor** | Dimensionless |

> **Where:**
> - $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of task *i*
> - $T_i$ = Period of task *i*
> - $R_i$ = Worst-Case Response Time of task *i*
> - $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with priority higher than task *i*
> - $L_{bytes}$ = DMA transfer size, $f_{bus}$ = bus frequency, $w_{bus}$ = bus width

### 2.4.1 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR, OSEK):** Uses RMS for engine control, brake-by-wire — bound is critical for ISO 26262 ASIL-D certification.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Static allocation + MPU isolation mandatory; WCET analysis with RTA.
- **IoT Edge Devices (FreeRTOS):** Tiny memory footprint (4–10 KB) enables Cortex-M0+ deployment on $0.50 MCUs.
- **Medical (FDA Class III):** Priority inversion bug = patient death; PIP mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu–Layland RMS Schedulability Bound

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** We want to find the *worst-case* utilization $U$ below which *any* set of *n* periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** is *guaranteed* to meet all deadlines.

**Step 1 — Define Utilization and Assumptions**
Let there be *n* periodic, independent tasks, each characterized by:
- $C_i$ : worst-case computation time per period
- $T_i$ : period (with $T_1 < T_2 < \dots < T_n$ so priorities are highest for smallest periods)

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

Assumptions: tasks are *independent*, *preemptible*, *periodic*, with *deadline = period*, zero context-switch overhead, single processor.

**Step 2 — Consider Critical Instant**
By definition, a task $T_i$ is hardest to schedule when all higher-priority tasks release **simultaneously** (this is the **critical instant theorem**, proven by Liu & Layland).

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Response Time of Task 1 (Highest Priority)**
Task 1 has no higher-priority interference, so:

$$R_1 = C_1$$

Condition $R_1 \le T_1$ reduces to $C_1 \le T_1$, i.e. $\frac{C_1}{T_1} \le 1$.

**Step 4 — Generalize to Task i**
Task *i* can be preempted by all tasks in $hp(i)$ (higher priority). In the worst case, each such task $T_j$ executes $\left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil$ times during the response window of length $R_i$.

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 5 — Bound the Ceilings**
Using the property $\lceil x \rceil \le x + 1$ for any real $x$:

$$\left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \le \frac{R_i}{T_j} + 1$$

**Step 6 — Derive the Utilization Bound**
After substitution and induction (see Liu & Layland, 1973, *Journal of the ACM*), the critical utilization bound is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{RMS}^{\max} &= n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Numerical Examples**

| *n* | Bound $U_{RMS}^{\max}$ | Approx. % |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0000 | 100.0% |
| 2 | 0.8284 | 82.8% |
| 3 | 0.7798 | 78.0% |
| 4 | 0.7568 | 75.7% |
| 5 | 0.7435 | 74.4% |
| 10 | 0.7177 | 71.8% |
| ∞ | $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$ | 69.3% |

**Step 8 — Limit as n → ∞**
Using the limit identity $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln(2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{RMS}^{\max} &= \ln(2) \\
&\approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** If the question states "$U = 0.85$ for 3 tasks", the answer is **"Not schedulable under RMS"** because $0.85 > 0.7798$, but **schedulable under EDF** because $0.85 < 1.0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Interrupt Latency Equation

**Step 1 — Components of Interrupt Latency**
Total interrupt latency is the sum of four sequential components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{latency} &= T_{detect} + T_{save} + T_{ISR} + T_{schedule}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Component Definitions**
- $T_{detect}$ = Time for hardware to recognize the interrupt (1–10 clock cycles on Cortex-M).
- $T_{save}$ = Time to push CPU registers onto the stack (depends on register count; ARM Cortex-M = automatic, ~12 cycles).
- $T_{ISR}$ = Execution time of the Interrupt Service Routine.
- $T_{schedule}$ = Time for the scheduler to decide whether to preempt current task.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example**
Assume:
- $f_{CPU} = 100$ MHz → $T_{clock} = 10$ ns
- $T_{detect} = 4$ cycles → $40$ ns
- $T_{save} = 12$ cycles → $120$ ns
- $T_{ISR} = 200$ cycles → $2000$ ns
- $T_{schedule} = 30$ cycles → $300$ ns

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{latency} &= 40 + 120 + 2000 + 300 \text{ ns} \\
&= 2460 \text{ ns} \\
&= 2.46 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-Case vs Best-Case**
- **Best case** (no other ISRs active, no priority masking): above value.
- **Worst case** (another ISR of higher priority running): adds $T_{current\_ISR}$ plus all higher-priority ISR execution times.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Process Mgmt | Memory Mgmt | Device Mgmt |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Data Structure** | TCB (Task Control Block) | Memory Map / Page Table | Device Descriptor / File Operations Table |
| **Key Algorithm** | RMS / EDF / RR | First-fit / Buddy / Slab | Polling / Interrupt / DMA |
| **Hard Real-Time Compatible?** | ✅ Yes (with proper algorithm) | ✅ Yes (static allocation) | ✅ Yes (deterministic ISR) |
| **Worst-Case Bounded?** | ✅ (RTA equation) | ✅ (static) / ❌ (malloc) | ✅ (ISR bounded) |
| **Failure Mode** | Deadlock, priority inversion | Fragmentation, leak, overflow | Data loss, missed interrupt |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | ~40% of Module 3 | ~30% of Module 3 | ~30% of Module 3 |
| **Reference RTOS Example** | FreeRTOS `xTaskCreate()` | FreeRTOS `pvPortMalloc()` | FreeRTOS `xQueueSendFromISR()` |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTOS Kernel Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER User Tasks T1 T2 T3"]
    apiLayer["API LAYER POSIX FreeRTOS VxWorks APIs"]
    pmBox["PROCESS MANAGEMENT Scheduler Dispatcher TCB Pool IPC Semaphores Queues"]
    mmBox["MEMORY MANAGEMENT Static Allocator Heap Manager MPU MMU Stack Monitor"]
    dmBox["DEVICE MANAGEMENT Driver Layer ISR Handler DMA Engine I O Scheduler"]
    hwLayer["HARDWARE LAYER CPU RAM ROM Peripherals UART SPI ADC GPIO"]

    appLayer --> apiLayer
    apiLayer --> pmBox
    apiLayer --> mmBox
    apiLayer --> dmBox
    pmBox --> mmBox
    pmBox --> dmBox
    mmBox --> hwLayer
    dmBox --> hwLayer
    pmBox --> hwLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A typical KTU question asks "What happens when a UART byte arrives?" — Trace: Peripheral triggers **ISR (Device Management)** → ISR posts a **semaphore (Process Management)** → Scheduler unblocks the **receiving task (Process Management)** → Task reads from a **buffer allocated in Memory Management**.

---

## 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> dormant: TaskCreated
    dormant --> ready: TaskActivated
    ready --> running: SchedulerDispatch
    running --> ready: Preempted
    running --> blocked: WaitEvent
    blocked --> ready: EventOccured
    running --> suspended: SuspendCall
    suspended --> ready: ResumeCall
    running --> [*]: TaskDeleted
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Hierarchy in an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reg["REGISTERS Fastest Smallest 32 bytes"]
    cache["CACHE L1 L2 Optional in MCU 4 32 KB"]
    sram["SRAM Internal Fast Volatile 8 256 KB"]
    extRam["EXTERNAL DRAM DDR Optional 1 MB 1 GB"]
    flash["FLASH NOR Program Code 256 KB 16 MB"]
    eeprom["EEPROM Calibration Data Bytes"]
    nand["NAND Optional File System MB GB"]

    reg --> cache
    cache --> sram
    sram --> extRam
    sram --> flash
    sram --> eeprom
    sram --> nand
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Note:** The pyramid above is **inverted** in cost-per-bit. **Registers** are fastest and most expensive; **NAND Flash** is slowest and cheapest. The Memory Manager decides which data lives in which tier.

---

## 4.4 Device Driver — Interrupt-Driven I/O Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AppTask as Application Task
    participant Driver as Device Driver
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine
    participant HW as UART Peripheral
    participant Sched as Scheduler

    AppTask->>Driver: Read request blocking
    Driver->>HW: Enable RX interrupt
    Note over AppTask: Task enters Blocked state
    Sched-->>AppTask: Switch to other task
    HW-->>ISR: Byte received interrupt
    ISR->>ISR: Read byte from data register
    ISR->>Driver: Store in buffer
    ISR->>Sched: Post semaphore via FromISR
    Sched-->>AppTask: Unblock task
    AppTask->>Driver: Resume execution
    Driver-->>AppTask: Return byte
```

---

## 4.5 Priority Inversion Scenario (Mars Pathfinder Bug)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    highP["HIGH PRIORITY TASK H Wants Bus"]
    lowP["LOW PRIORITY TASK L Holds Bus Mutex"]
    medP["MEDIUM PRIORITY TASK M Unrelated"]

    lowP --> medP
    medP --> highP

    note1["L takes mutex then preempted by M"]
    note2["M runs forever H is starved DEADLOCK like behavior"]
    note3["Solution Priority Inheritance L temporarily inherits H priority"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Explain how **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves unbounded priority inversion." Always mention: *the low-priority task L temporarily inherits the priority of H, thereby pre-empting M, releasing the mutex quickly, and restoring its original priority.*
