| Determinism | Poor (depends on period) | Good (hardware-driven) | Excellent (bounded) |
| Suitable for hard real-time | No | Yes (for ISR), but signal work must be deferred | **Yes — recommended pattern** |
| Power consumption | High | Medium | Low |
| Complexity | Lowest | Medium (vector table, nesting) | High (needs RTOS) |
| Risk | Missed events if poll period too long | ISR stack overflow, long ISRs | Deadlock if order wrong |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The semaphore-based pattern is the gold standard for RTOS-based embedded systems because it gives bounded blocking, low power, and clean separation between ISR (top half) and task (bottom half).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Semaphore Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting atomicity** (–2 marks): Always state that `test-and-decrement` is an atomic step. Many students write the pseudocode but omit the atomicity guarantee.
> 2. **Confusing mutex and binary semaphore** (–2 marks): A binary semaphore used for mutual exclusion *will* cause unbounded priority inversion. Examiners dock marks if the student uses a binary semaphore where a mutex is required.
> 3. **Signaling a mutex from an ISR** (–3 marks): This is undefined behavior in FreeRTOS and most commercial kernels. Always use a binary semaphore for ISR-to-task signaling.
> 4. **Wrong invariant form** (–1 to –2 marks): Writing $S + W_S = N$ strictly is wrong. Use the **extended invariant** $S + H + W_S \le N$, or clearly state that the equality holds only at quiescent points.
> 5. **Not mentioning Dijkstra** (–1 mark): KTU examiners award a mark for naming the inventor and the year (1965).
> 6. **Drawing a flow chart without explaining transitions** (–1 mark): Diagrams must be accompanied by a one-line justification for each transition.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Inventor & year:** E.W. Dijkstra, **1965**, Dutch mathematician.
- **Two operations:** `wait()` / `P()` / `down()` and `signal()` / `V()` / `up()`. Both must be **atomic**.
- **Three primitive types:** Binary Semaphore, Counting Semaphore, Mutex.
- **Mutex extras:** ownership, recursive lock, **priority inheritance**.
- **ISR rule:** signal a **binary semaphore** from an ISR — **never** signal a mutex from an ISR.
- **Mutex vs Binary Semaphore:** Binary semaphore has *no owner* and *no priority inheritance* — therefore cannot safely provide mutual exclusion in a hard real-time system.
- **Producer–Consumer needs 3 primitives:** `empty` (counting), `full` (counting), `mutex` (binary) — protecting a circular buffer.
- **Semaphore invariant:** $S(t) + H(t) + W_S(t) \le N$, equality at quiescence.
- **Worst-case bypass:** for binary semaphore with $M$ tasks, $B_{worst} = M - 1$.
- **Three correctness properties:** Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting (the **MPB** triple).
- **Priority inversion:** solved by **priority inheritance** mutex, bounded by critical-section length of the lowest-priority holder.
- **Deadlock prevention:** always acquire semaphores in the **same global order** across all tasks; use `trylock` with timeout where ordering is impossible.
- **FreeRTOS API to remember:**
  - `xSemaphoreCreateBinary()`
  - `xSemaphoreCreateCounting(max, initial)`
  - `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()`
  - `xSemaphoreTake()` and `xSemaphoreGive()`
  - `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()` (binary/counting only)
- **Atomicity enforcement:** interrupt-mask, test-and-set, compare-and-swap, or scheduler lock.
- **Common formula trap:** When the kernel queues a waiter, the counter stays at zero — the permit is *implicitly* given to the woken task, not added to the pool.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Message Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Message Queue** is a kernel-managed, indirect Inter-Process Communication (IPC) and task synchronization primitive in which discrete data packets (*messages*) are deposited by one or more **producer** tasks and withdrawn by one or more **consumer** tasks through a shared, addressable mailbox. Each message is treated as an atomic, variable-length (or fixed-length) unit and is typically serviced in **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** order, although many RTOS kernels allow priority-based reordering at the head of the queue.

In formal KTU/embedded-systems nomenclature, a message queue is defined as a *blocking-safe, asynchronous, bounded buffer object* characterized by a finite number of slots, a fixed maximum message width, and a deterministic set of kernel APIs (`create`, `send`, `receive`, `peek`, `delete`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Message queues belong to the broader family of **Task Communication mechanisms** (alongside shared memory, mailboxes, pipes, and semaphores). In the KTU 2024 scheme for Embedded System Design, the focus is on **FreeRTOS-style queues**, **POSIX message queues (`<mqueue.h>`)**, and their application in producer-consumer architectures for real-time embedded firmware.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Order Ticket System"

Imagine a busy South-Indian restaurant kitchen during lunch rush:

1. The **waiter (Producer Task)** takes an order from a customer and writes it on a paper slip (the *message*).
2. The slip is pinned onto a **rotating spindle/clipboard (the Message Queue)** that holds a limited number of slips — say 10.
3. The **chef (Consumer Task)** picks the slip at the *bottom* of the spindle (oldest order, FIFO) and begins cooking.
4. If the spindle is **full**, the waiter must *wait* (block) until the chef removes a slip — this is **queue-full blocking**.
5. If the spindle is **empty**, the chef must *wait* until the waiter pins a new order — this is **queue-empty blocking**.

This spindle is the **message queue**. Notice four engineering-critical properties:

| Real-world property | Embedded equivalent |
|---|---|
| Fixed number of slips on spindle | **Queue Length** (number of slots) |
| Fixed size of each paper slip | **Message Size** (bytes per item) |
| Oldest slip cooked first | **FIFO ordering** |
| Waiter waits when full | **Blocking send** |
| Chef waits when empty | **Blocking receive** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Unlike a *shared variable* (which is like a single white-board anyone can erase), a message queue guarantees that a *whole message* is transferred atomically — no partial reads, no torn writes, and no need for mutual-exclusion locks around the data itself.

### 1.3 Physical / Architectural Constants

The following are the **standard quantitative attributes** of a message queue as defined by POSIX 1003.1b and adopted by FreeRTOS:

- **Queue Length (`mq_maxmsg`)** — maximum number of messages that can reside in the queue at any instant.
- **Maximum Message Size (`mq_msgsize`)** — the largest payload (in bytes) that any single message may carry.
- **Blocking Timeout** — measured in **ticks** (FreeRTOS) or **absolute nanoseconds** (POSIX), default ∞ (wait forever).
- **Priority (`mq_prio`)** — POSIX supports up to **`MQ_PRIO_MAX` (≥ 32)** priority levels per message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these constants matter for embedded boards (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 on STM32):** SRAM is precious (often 64–256 KB total). Every queue consumes `Length × (Size + overhead)` bytes. An incorrectly sized queue can cause silent memory starvation, manifesting as `pvPortMalloc()` failures (the dreaded *`configASSERT( xQueue )` failure on bare-metal targets*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of a Message Queue

A message queue is logically composed of **three** structural layers:

1. **Control Block (Queue Handle)** — a kernel-allocated structure (`QueueHandle_t` in FreeRTOS, `mqd_t` in POSIX) that stores metadata: head index, tail index, message count, item size, waiting-task lists.
2. **Data Buffer (Ring Storage)** — a contiguous byte-array holding the actual message payloads, typically organized as a **circular buffer** for $O(1)$ enqueue/dequeue.
3. **Task Wait Lists** — two doubly-linked lists of TCBs (Task Control Blocks): one for tasks blocked on *send* (queue full), another for tasks blocked on *receive* (queue empty).

The relationship between these layers is:

$$
\text{Memory Footprint} = \underbrace{L \times S}_{\text{ring buffer}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{sizeof(ControlBlock)}}_{\text{metadata}} \;+\; \underbrace{W_s + W_r}_{\text{two wait lists}}
$$

where $L$ = queue length, $S$ = message size (bytes), $W_s$ = bytes per waiting-sender TCB-link, $W_r$ = bytes per waiting-receiver TCB-link.

---

### 2.2 Operational Walk-Through — Why and How

#### Step 1 — Queue Creation
The kernel allocates the control block + ring buffer in **heap memory** (FreeRTOS `pvPortMalloc`) or **user memory** (FreeRTOS `xQueueCreateStatic`).

> *Why heap allocation?* It allows flexible, late-binding instantiation. *Why static allocation?* It guarantees deterministic startup memory and is mandatory for safety-critical certification (e.g., MISRA-C, IEC 61508, DO-178C).

#### Step 2 — Send Operation (`xQueueSend` / `mq_send`)
The producer copies its message *by value* into the slot pointed to by `head`, then advances `head = (head + 1) mod L`. If the queue is **full**, the calling task is moved to the *send-wait list* and the scheduler picks another ready task.

#### Step 3 — Receive Operation (`xQueueReceive` / `mq_receive`)
The consumer copies the message *out* of the slot pointed to by `tail` into its own buffer, then advances `tail = (tail + 1) mod L`. If the queue is **empty**, the task is parked on the *receive-wait list*.

#### Step 4 — Wake-up / Handoff
When a sender writes into an empty queue, it directly **hands the message to a waiting receiver** (a *direct task notification* path) without using the ring buffer — this is a zero-copy optimization present in FreeRTOS.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / API Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / API | FreeRTOS Symbol | POSIX Symbol | Return / Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create queue | `xQueueCreate(uxQueueLength, uxItemSize)` | `mq_open(name, flags, mode, attr)` | Handle or `NULL` / `-1` |
| 2 | Send (to back) | `xQueueSend(xQueue, pvItem, xTicksToWait)` | `mq_send(mqd, buf, len, prio)` | `pdPASS` / `errQUEUE_FULL` |
| 3 | Send (to front / urgent) | `xQueueSendToFront(...)` | (priority-based) | `pdPASS` |
| 4 | Receive | `xQueueReceive(xQueue, pvBuffer, xTicksToWait)` | `mq_receive(mqd, buf, len, &prio)` | `pdPASS` / `errQUEUE_EMPTY` |
| 5 | Peek (non-destructive) | `xQueuePeek(xQueue, pvBuffer, xTicksToWait)` | (no direct; use timed receive + repost) | `pdPASS` |
| 6 | Messages waiting | `uxQueueMessagesWaiting(xQueue)` | `mq_getattr(mqd, attr)` | `UBaseType_t` / `mq_attr.mq_curmsgs` |
| 7 | Spaces available | `uxQueueSpacesAvailable(xQueue)` | derived from `mq_maxmsg − mq_curmsgs` | `UBaseType_t` |
| 8 | Delete | `vQueueDelete(xQueue)` | `mq_close(mqd)` + `mq_unlink(name)` | `void` / `0` on success |

#### Quantitative Formulas (LaTeX)

$$
\text{Total Queue RAM} = L \cdot S + C_{\text{overhead}} \quad \text{(bytes)}
$$

$$
\text{Circular index advance} \;\Rightarrow\; i_{new} = (i_{old} + 1) \bmod L
$$

$$
\text{Worst-case blocking latency} \le T_{tick} \times xTicksToWait
$$

$$
\text{Worst-case message delay} = \frac{L - 1}{f_{\text{consume}}} \quad \text{(seconds, FIFO)}
$$

$$
\text{Messages/sec throughput} = \min\!\left(f_{\text{produce}},\, f_{\text{consume}},\, \frac{1}{T_{\text{copy}}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design a message queue", examiners expect you to compute **(a)** memory footprint, **(b)** worst-case latency, and **(c)** overflow/underflow behavior. The LaTeX formulas above are the *only* numerical relations typically tested.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Message queues are the **workhorse of RTOS firmware** in:

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)**: CAN message frames wrapped into queues for upper-layer COM/PduR processing.
- **IoT sensor hubs**: Accelerometer / temperature producer tasks dumping into a queue consumed by an MQTT publisher task.
- **Industrial PLCs**: Discrete-input interrupts depositing event records into a queue polled by the logic-scan task.
- **Medical devices (FDA Class II/III)**: Defibrillator charge/discharge events queued for the user-interface LCD task — must use *static* queues for deterministic memory.
- **Consumer wearables (e.g., fitness bands)**: BLE stack notifications queued for the OLED display task to avoid race conditions on the display SPI bus.

> [!NOTE]
> The **engineering utility** boils down to a single phrase: **decoupling time and space between producers and consumers**. Without queues, two tasks would need to spin-lock a shared variable, wasting CPU and risking priority inversion.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Memory Footprint of a FreeRTOS Queue

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style numerical):**
A FreeRTOS application running on an STM32F407 (Cortex-M4, 192 KB SRAM) needs a message queue of length **8** with each message being a 32-bit `struct` (`SensorData`) of size **16 bytes**. Compute (a) total RAM consumed by the queue storage, (b) total RAM including FreeRTOS control-block overhead (assume `C_overhead = 80 bytes` for `Queue_t` on this port), and (c) the percentage of total SRAM this consumes.

#### Step 1 — Identify the symbolic quantities

Let $L = 8$ (queue length), $S = 16$ bytes (message width), and $C_{\text{overhead}} = 80$ bytes.

#### Step 2 — Apply the ring-buffer RAM formula

The ring buffer itself requires:

$$
M_{\text{ring}} = L \times S
$$

$$
M_{\text{ring}} = 8 \times 16 = 128 \;\text{bytes}
$$

#### Step 3 — Add the kernel control-block overhead

$$
M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{ring}} + C_{\text{overhead}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 128 + 80 = 208 \;\text{bytes}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute percentage of available SRAM

Let $M_{\text{SRAM}} = 192 \times 1024 = 196608$ bytes.

$$
P = \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{SRAM}}} \times 100
$$

$$
P = \frac{208}{196608} \times 100 \approx 0.1058\,\%
$$

#### Step 5 — Interpret the result

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{total}} = 208 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.106\% \text{ of SRAM}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The queue is *negligibly* small relative to SRAM, but in a system with **50 such queues** the cumulative footprint becomes ~10 KB, which can cause heap fragmentation failures. This is precisely why safety-critical systems prefer `xQueueCreateStatic` with pre-reserved `uint8_t ucQueueStorage[N]` buffers in `.bss`.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case FIFO Latency for a Sensor-Datalogger

**Problem Statement:**
A producer task (ISR-driven) writes sensor samples into a queue every **2 ms**. The consumer task drains the queue every **10 ms**. Queue length $L = 5$. If the consumer is preempted for an unusually long **35 ms** by a higher-priority task, compute (a) the number of messages that will be *lost* (assuming no overflow strategy), and (b) the worst-case age of the oldest message in the queue when the consumer resumes.

#### Step 1 — Compute arrival rate

$$
f_{\text{produce}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{produce}}} = \frac{1}{2\,\text{ms}} = 500 \;\text{msg/s}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the number of producer cycles during the preemption window

$$
N_{\text{produced}} = \frac{T_{\text{preempt}}}{T_{\text{produce}}} = \frac{35\,\text{ms}}{2\,\text{ms}} = 17.5
$$

Since we cannot have half a message:

$$
N_{\text{produced}} = \lfloor 17.5 \rfloor = 17 \;\text{messages}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the *retainable* messages

The queue can only hold $L = 5$ outstanding messages before samples are dropped (assuming the default "drop newest" overflow policy). Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{lost}} = N_{\text{produced}} - L = 17 - 5 = 12 \;\text{messages}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the worst-case age of the oldest unconsumed message

The consumer, when it resumes, will first read the *oldest* message, which entered the queue approximately:

$$
T_{\text{age, max}} = T_{\text{preempt}} + T_{\text{consume cycle}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{age, max}} = 35\,\text{ms} + 10\,\text{ms} = 45\,\text{ms}
$$

#### Step 5 — Final boxed answer

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{lost}} = 12 \text{ messages} \quad ; \quad T_{\text{age, max}} = 45 \text{ ms}}
$$

> **Engineering Mitigation:** To avoid data loss, the designer should either **(i)** increase $L$ to at least $\lceil T_{\text{preempt}}/T_{\text{produce}} \rceil = 18$, **(ii)** raise the consumer's priority above the preempter, or **(iii)** use `xQueueSendToFrontFromISR` with a *drop-oldest* policy via a custom `xQueueOverwrite` style API.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Message Queues vs Alternatives

| Design Criterion | Message Queue | Shared Memory + Mutex | Semaphore (Binary) | Direct Task Notification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carries **data**, not just a signal | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (signal only) | ✅ Yes (32-bit value) |
| **Blocking** semantics on full/empty | ✅ Native | ⚠ Manual | ✅ Give/Take | ✅ SetBits |
| Multi-producer safe | ✅ Kernel-managed | ⚠ Needs mutex | ✅ | ❌ One-to-one |
| Deterministic RAM usage | ✅ Static-allocatable | ⚠ Depends on struct | ✅ Constant | ✅ Constant |
| Latency (zero-copy hand-off) | ⚠ Usually copies | ✅ No copy | ✅ No copy | ✅ No copy |
| Suitable for ISR → Task hand-off | ✅ `FromISR` variants | ❌ Forbidden in ISR | ✅ `FromISR` | ✅ `FromISR` |
| KTU exam frequency (high/medium/low) | **HIGH** | Medium | High | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthesis Insight:** Whenever the KTU question says *"pass data between an ISR and a task"* or *"buffer sporadic sensor events"*, the canonical answer is a **message queue** with `xQueueSendFromISR` and `xQueueReceive` with `portMAX_DELAY`.

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Analytical Problem — Latency Under Priority Inversion

**Problem:** Three tasks share a message queue holding motor-control commands:
- Task M (motor) — priority 3 (high)
- Task C (controller) — priority 2 (mid)
- Task L (logger) — priority 1 (low)

The queue is full at $t = 0$. M attempts to send a *safety-stop* message. Compute the *effective* latency $L_{\text{eff}}$ for M's message to be accepted if L currently holds the CPU for 8 ms (non-preemptively) and C subsequently needs 5 ms.

#### Step 1 — Determine pre-emption order

Since L has lower priority but is currently running and the kernel is non-preemptive during L's critical section (assume), M must wait. Once L yields (at $t = 8$ ms), M pre-empts C (which just started).

#### Step 2 — Compute effective wait

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = T_L + T_{context\_switch} + T_{queue\_insert}
$$

$$
L_{\text{eff}} \approx 8\,\text{ms} + 12\,\mu\text{s} + 800\,\text{ns} \approx 8.013\,\text{ms}
$$

#### Step 3 — Highlight the design flaw

This is the classic **priority inversion** scenario. For *safety-stop* messages, the canonical fix is to **bypass the queue** using `xQueueSendToFront` (urgent delivery) or a **direct task notification** (`vTaskNotifyGiveFromISR`) to the motor task.

### 4.3 State Diagram — Send Operation State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Calling
    Calling --> CheckSpace: Inspect uxQueueSpacesAvailable
    CheckSpace --> DirectHandoff: Queue empty AND receiver waiting
    CheckSpace --> CopyIntoRing: Space available
    CheckSpace --> BlockOnFull: Queue full
    DirectHandoff --> ReturnSuccess
    CopyIntoRing --> WakeReceiver: Was previously empty
    CopyIntoRing --> ReturnSuccess
    WakeReceiver --> ReturnSuccess
    BlockOnFull --> TickTick: Decrement xTicksToWait each tick
    TickTick --> Unblock: Space freed by receiver
    TickTick --> ReturnTimeout: Timeout expires
    Unblock --> CopyIntoRing
    ReturnSuccess --> [*]
    ReturnTimeout --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — ISR → Task Handoff (Zero-Copy Hand-Off Path)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ISR as ISR (GPIO Edge)
    participant Q as Message Queue
    participant TC as Consumer Task
    ISR->>Q: xQueueSendFromISR(payload)
    alt Queue empty AND TC waiting
        Q-->>TC: Direct hand-off (no ring buffer used)
        Q->>Q: Set TC state = READY
    else Queue not empty
        Q->>Q: Copy payload into ring buffer at Head
        Q->>Q: Head = (Head + 1) mod L
    end
    Q-->>ISR: pxHigherPriorityTaskWoken (bool)
    alt Woken == true
        ISR->>ISR: portYIELD_FROM_ISR()
    end
    TC->>Q: xQueueReceive(&payload, portMAX_DELAY)
    Q-->>TC: payload copied out
    TC->>TC: Process motor command
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Notes for the Examiner:**
> 1. The *Direct Hand-off* path is the **highest-priority low-latency path** in FreeRTOS; it is **not present** in POSIX `<mqueue.h>`, where the kernel *always* writes into the ring buffer.
> 2. The ring buffer's circular wrap is essential to achieve $O(1)$ enqueue/dequeue *without* memory moves.
> 3. `portYIELD_FROM_ISR()` is mandatory in ARM Cortex-M ports; omitting it is a common bug that delays latency by one full tick.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a message queue. List any **four** characteristics of an RTOS message queue.*

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

A **message queue** is a kernel-managed, bounded IPC primitive that allows one or more tasks (or ISRs) to deposit discrete data units (*messages*) into a shared buffer from which one or more consumer tasks withdraw them.

Four characteristics:
1. **FIFO ordering** of messages (configurable to priority).
2. **Bounded capacity** defined by queue length and message size.
3. **Blocking semantics** on full / empty conditions with timeout support.
4. **Atomic** message transfer — no torn reads/writes, eliminating the need for explicit user-side mutexes.

> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + 4 × characteristics (0.5 each = 2) = **3 marks**.

---

#### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a **binary semaphore** and a **message queue**. When would you prefer a queue over a semaphore?*

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Message Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Payload | None (signal only) | Carries a structured message |
| State | 0 or 1 | Count of messages (0…N) |
| Primary use | Synchronization / mutual exclusion | Data passing + synchronization |
| Memory | 1 flag | `L × S + overhead` |

**When to prefer a queue:** When the ISR/task must convey *information* (e.g., a sensor reading, a CAN frame, a button-press timestamp) and not merely *signal* an event. Queues decouple time and space between producer and consumer.

> **Marks Distribution:** Comparison table (1.5) + preference criterion (1) + example (0.5) = **3 marks**.
