### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### 📘 Question B-A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of an RTOS message queue, clearly labeling the **control block**, the **ring buffer**, and the **task wait lists**. Discuss the role of the head and tail pointers. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A FreeRTOS-based data-acquisition system uses a queue of length **10** and message size **32 bytes**. The producer writes samples every **5 ms**. Compute (i) total RAM consumed (assume `C_overhead` = 100 bytes), (ii) worst-case blocking latency if `xTicksToWait = 100` and `configTICK_RATE_HZ = 1000`, and (iii) the steady-state queue occupancy if the consumer drains at **200 Hz**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for B-A

#### (a) Internal Architecture of an RTOS Message Queue **[7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (re-drawn textually for answer sheet):**

```
 +-----------------------------+
 |   Queue Control Block       |
 |   ----------------------    |
 |   | uxHead              |   |
 |   | uxTail              |   |
 |   | uxMessagesWaiting   |   |
 |   | uxItemSize          |   |
 |   | uxLength            |   |
 |   | xTasksWaitingToSend |   |
 |   | xTasksWaitingToRecv |   |
 |   ----------------------    |
 +-----------------------------+
              |
              v
 +-----------------------------+
 |  Ring Buffer (ucStorage[])  |
 |  [Slot0][Slot1]...[SlotN-1] |
 +-----------------------------+
```

**Roles (5 marks for explanation):**
1. **Control Block (`Queue_t`)** — holds metadata: head, tail, count, item size, length, and the two task wait lists. The handle `QueueHandle_t` is a pointer to this block.
2. **Ring Buffer (`ucQueueStorage[]`)** — the actual storage; messages are copied *by value* into slots at the head index.
3. **Head pointer (`uxHead`)** — points to the *next* free slot for an enqueue; advanced modulo length: `uxHead = (uxHead + 1) % uxLength`.
4. **Tail pointer (`uxTail`)** — points to the *next* slot to be read; same modulo arithmetic.
5. **Sender Wait List** — TCBs of tasks blocked because the queue is full.
6. **Receiver Wait List** — TCBs of tasks blocked because the queue is empty.

**Diagram quality, labels, and discussion (2 marks).**

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution **[7 marks]**

**Given:** $L = 10$, $S = 32$ bytes, $C_{\text{overhead}} = 100$ bytes, $xTicksToWait = 100$, `configTICK_RATE_HZ = 1000`, producer period $T_p = 5$ ms, consumer rate $f_c = 200$ Hz.

**(i) Total RAM consumed:**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = L \times S + C_{\text{overhead}} = (10)(32) + 100
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{total}} = 420 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**(ii) Worst-case blocking latency:**

$$
T_{\text{tick}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
L_{\text{block}} = xTicksToWait \times T_{\text{tick}} = 100 \times 1\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{block}} = 100\,\text{ms}}
$$

**(iii) Steady-state queue occupancy:**

Producer rate $f_p = 1/T_p = 1/0.005 = 200$ Hz. Consumer rate $f_c = 200$ Hz.

Since $f_p = f_c$, the queue neither grows nor shrinks in steady state:

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{ss}} \approx 1 \text{ message (steady state)}}
$$

*(The "1" comes from the fact that each consumer read consumes the message produced just before; with phase offset it can briefly be 0 or 2, but the average is 1.)*

> **Marks Distribution:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

#### 📘 Question B-B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare **POSIX message queues** (`<mqueue.h>`) with **FreeRTOS queues** under the headings: (i) namespace/identification, (ii) message-size handling, (iii) blocking semantics, (iv) ISR-safety, and (v) persistence. Highlight which is more suitable for a bare-metal Cortex-M firmware. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write the **FreeRTOS C code** to create a queue of 5 messages, each of type `struct Sensor { uint16_t id; float value; uint32_t timestamp; }`. Show the producer (in a periodic task) sending and the consumer (in another task) receiving, including the correct `FromISR` macro for an external interrupt that pre-empts everything. Explain what happens if the queue is full when the ISR fires. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for B-B

#### (a) POSIX vs FreeRTOS Queues **[7 marks]**

| # | Aspect | POSIX `<mqueue.h>` | FreeRTOS `queue.h` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identification | Named in filesystem (e.g., `/mq_sensors`); survives process exit | Anonymous handle (`QueueHandle_t`); destroyed with `vQueueDelete` |
| 2 | Message size | Variable up to `mq_msgsize`; copied as raw bytes | Fixed at creation (`uxItemSize`); copied by value |
| 3 | Blocking | `mq_timedreceive(mqd, buf, len, &prio, abs_timeout)` | `xQueueReceive(xQ, buf, xTicksToWait)` |
| 4 | ISR-safety | ❌ Not ISR-safe on most ports | ✅ `xQueueSendFromISR` + `portYIELD_FROM_ISR` |
| 5 | Persistence | Optional `mq_attr` + filesystem | None; RAM-only |

**Suitability for bare-metal Cortex-M (2 marks):** **FreeRTOS queues** are vastly more suitable because (i) they don't require a POSIX-compliant filesystem (the Cortex-M usually has no MMU and no VFS), (ii) they have ISR-safe variants, and (iii) they have deterministic `Static` allocation for safety certification.

---

#### (b) FreeRTOS C Code Solution **[7 marks]**

```c
/* --- Sensor.h --- */
typedef struct {
    uint16_t id;
    float    value;
    uint32_t timestamp;
} Sensor_t;

#define QUEUE_LENGTH    5
#define QUEUE_ITEM_SIZE sizeof(Sensor_t)

/* --- Globals --- */
QueueHandle_t xSensorQueue;

/* --- Producer Task (Periodic, 10 ms) --- */
void vProducerTask(void *pvParameters) {
    Sensor_t xSample;
    TickType_t xLastWakeTime = xTaskGetTickCount();
    for (;;) {
        xSample.id        = 0xABCD;
        xSample.value     = 23.7f;
        xSample.timestamp = xTaskGetTickCount();
        if (xQueueSend(xSensorQueue, &xSample, 0) != pdPASS) {
            /* Queue full: drop or log */
        }
        vTaskDelayUntil(&xLastWakeTime, pdMS_TO_TICKS(10));
    }
}

/* --- Consumer Task --- */
void vConsumerTask(void *pvParameters) {
    Sensor_t xReceived;
    for (;;) {
        if (xQueueReceive(xSensorQueue, &xReceived, portMAX_DELAY) == pdPASS) {
            /* Process xReceived.id, .value, .timestamp */
        }
    }
}

/* --- ISR (e.g., EXTI0_IRQHandler) --- */
void EXTI0_IRQHandler(void) {
    BaseType_t xHigherPriorityTaskWoken = pdFALSE;
    Sensor_t xIsrSample;
    xIsrSample.id        = 0xFF00;
    xIsrSample.value     = 99.9f;
    xIsrSample.timestamp = xTaskGetTickCountFromISR();

    /* Send to queue — non-blocking from ISR */
    xQueueSendFromISR(xSensorQueue,
                      &xIsrSample,
                      &xHigherPriorityTaskWoken);

    portYIELD_FROM_ISR(xHigherPriorityTaskWoken);
}

/* --- Initialization --- */
int main(void) {
    xSensorQueue = xQueueCreate(QUEUE_LENGTH, QUEUE_ITEM_SIZE);
    configASSERT(xSensorQueue != NULL);
    xTaskCreate(vProducerTask, "Prod", 256, NULL, 2, NULL);
    xTaskCreate(vConsumerTask, "Cons", 256, NULL, 3, NULL);
    vTaskStartScheduler();
    for (;;);
}
```

**Explanation of "queue full when ISR fires" (2 marks):**
`xQueueSendFromISR` does *not* block (there is no `xTicksToWait`). If the queue is full, the call returns `errQUEUE_FULL` and the ISR drops the message. Best practice: (i) size the queue large enough that an ISR burst cannot overflow it within one consumer cycle, or (ii) use `xQueueOverwriteFromISR` to replace the oldest message.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `portYIELD_FROM_ISR()`** after `xQueueSendFromISR()` → −1 to −2 marks; latency penalty in exam scenarios.
> 2. **Confusing `xQueueSend` and `xQueueSendToFront`** — the latter is for *urgent* (LIFO-style) messages.
> 3. **Wrong memory-footprint formula** — forgetting `C_overhead`. Always state your assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Passing a pointer into the queue** instead of a *value-copy* — FreeRTOS queues copy by value. Pointers-to-stack-variables cause silent corruption.
> 5. **Not declaring the queue as `static`** in safety-critical contexts — examiners reward this in 2024 scheme questions tagged with MISRA/IEC 61508.
> 6. **Using `printf` / `malloc` inside ISR** to "debug" — automatic 0 marks in any practical/lab follow-up.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- ✅ A **message queue** is a kernel-managed, bounded FIFO buffer for atomic inter-task data transfer.
- ✅ Composed of **Control Block + Ring Buffer + Wait Lists**.
- ✅ Key APIs: `xQueueCreate`, `xQueueSend`, `xQueueReceive`, `xQueuePeek`, `xQueueSendFromISR`, `vQueueDelete`.
- ✅ **Memory formula**: $M = L \times S + C_{\text{overhead}}$.
- ✅ **Blocking timeout**: $T_{\text{block}} = xTicksToWait / f_{\text{tick}}$.
- ✅ **Circular index**: $i \leftarrow (i + 1) \bmod L$.
- ✅ ISR-safe variants exist (`*FromISR`); always pair with `portYIELD_FROM_ISR`.
- ✅ Three failure modes: **overflow** (queue full), **underflow** (queue empty + blocking), **priority inversion**.
- ✅ POSIX queues are *named, filesystem-based*; FreeRTOS queues are *anonymous, RAM-based* — choose FreeRTOS for bare-metal Cortex-M.
- ✅ Use `xQueueCreateStatic` in safety-critical firmware for deterministic memory.
- ✅ Always size the queue using $L \geq \lceil T_{\text{max consumer delay}} / T_{\text{producer period}} \rceil$.
- ✅ A message queue **decouples time and space** between producer and consumer — its primary engineering value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mailboxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mailboxes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) for embedded systems, a **Mailbox** is a kernel-managed, fixed-size data-passing primitive used for **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** between two or more tasks (or between a task and an ISR). Unlike a simple flag or semaphore, a mailbox transfers a **discrete message unit** — typically a pointer to a data buffer, a status word, or a fixed-length message — through a synchronizing, queued channel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Aligned to PEEBT415 – Module 4: Inter-Process Communication & Synchronization):** > A **Mailbox** is a lightweight kernel object containing a single message slot (or a small, bounded ring of slots) into which a sending task deposits a message and from which a receiving task extracts it. The kernel guarantees **atomicity** of the deposit-withdraw operation, thereby eliminating the race conditions associated with shared global variables in a preemptive multi-tasking environment. The fundamental characteristics that distinguish a mailbox from other IPC mechanisms are: | Property | Mailbox Characteristic | | :--- | :--- | | **Capacity** | Typically **1 message slot** (or shallow queue of 2–4) | | **Message Size** | Fixed-size pointer, word, or small structure | | **Directionality** | Primarily **unidirectional** (sender → receiver) | | **Blocking Behavior** | Tasks may **block** on full (sender) or empty (receiver) | | **Ownership** | Kernel object (system resource) | | **Atomicity** | Guaranteed by the kernel dispatcher | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Office Drop-Box"** > Imagine a small wall-mounted letterbox between two office rooms. Room A wants to send a single **brief note** to Room B. The note is slid into the box (one slot only). If the box is empty, Room B can wait beside it. If the box is already full, Room A can choose to wait or leave a return-receipt. The receptionist (the **kernel**) ensures no one tampers with the box while a note is being inserted or removed. The note itself is a **pointer** pointing to a larger document kept on Room A's desk — only a "reference slip" travels through the box, not the entire document. This analogy reveals three key engineering insights: 1. **Decoupling** — Sender and receiver do not need to know each other's identity, only the mailbox handle. 2. **Reference Passing** — The mailbox carries a pointer, allowing large data blocks to be transferred without copying (zero-copy IPC). 3. **Synchronization** — The act of sending/receiving inherently synchronizes the two tasks in time. ## 1.3 Why Mailboxes are Required in Embedded Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > In preemptive, multi-tasking embedded firmware, multiple tasks may need to communicate state changes, sensor readings, or commands. Without a kernel-managed IPC primitive like a mailbox, designers are forced to use: > - **Polling of global flags** → wastes CPU cycles and is non-deterministic. > - **Shared memory with manual locking** → introduces priority-inversion and deadlock risks. > - **Mailboxes solve this** by providing a deterministic, low-overhead, race-free channel with a defined blocking/timeout semantics — essential for hard real-time deadlines. **Standard metrics (KTU expects familiarity with):** - **Latency:** Time from send call to message availability — typically **1–10 µs** in commercial RTOS like μC/OS-II, FreeRTOS (via direct-to-task notification). - **Memory footprint:** Kernel object size ≈ **16–32 bytes** (handle + payload slot + wait lists). - **Blocking timeout:** Configurable in **clock ticks** or **milliseconds**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The Send/Receive Lifecycle A mailbox operates as a **finite state machine (FSM)** governed by the kernel scheduler. The lifecycle of a single message is decomposed below: ### A. Mailbox Creation The system designer (or a startup task) requests the kernel to instantiate a mailbox object. In μC/OS-II this is a call to `OSMboxCreate(void *pmsg)`; in POSIX, it resembles `mq_open()`. ### B. Sender (Producer) Path 1. The task invokes the kernel's send primitive, e.g., `OSMboxPost(mailbox_handle, message_pointer)`. 2. The kernel acquires its **dispatcher lock** (interrupts disabled or scheduler lock acquired). 3. The kernel inspects the mailbox state: - **Empty slot available** → The pointer is written into the slot atomically; any task blocked on `pend` (receive) is moved to the ready queue. - **Slot already occupied** → If `OS_OPT_POST_NON_BLOCK` is not set, the sender is **suspended** on a "full" wait list; if non-blocking, an error code (e.g., `OS_ERR_MBOX_FULL`) is returned. 4. Scheduler runs and may perform a **context switch** if a higher-priority task was unblocked. ### C. Receiver (Consumer) Path 1. The task invokes `OSMboxPend(mailbox_handle, timeout, &err)`. 2. The kernel dispatcher checks the slot: - **Slot occupied** → The pointer is read out, the slot is marked empty, the caller continues; if any task was blocked on a full slot, the highest-priority sender is released. - **Slot empty** → If timeout ≠ 0, the receiver is **suspended** on the "empty" wait list with a timeout countdown; if timeout = 0, an error is returned immediately. 3. The receiver dereferences the pointer to access the actual payload data. ### D. Mailbox Deletion `OSMboxDel()` removes the object and wakes all waiting tasks with an appropriate error return value, preventing "zombie waiters". ## 2.2 Architectural Variants > [!NOTE] > **KTU often tests the distinction between these variants — memorize the table below:** | Variant | Capacity | Use Case | Example RTOS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Single-Slot Mailbox** | 1 message | Tightest sync, lowest RAM | μC/OS-II `OS_MBOX` | | **Multi-Slot Message Queue** | N messages | Burst-tolerant comms | μC/OS-II `OS_Q`, FreeRTOS `xQueue` | | **Direct-to-Task Notification** | 1 word | Ultra-low overhead, ISR→Task | FreeRTOS `xTaskNotify` | | **Broadcast Mailbox** | 1 → many | One source, many sinks | VxWorks `msgQ` with broadcast | ## 2.3 Blocking, Polling, and Timeouts — The Tri-Mode Every send/receive primitive typically supports three execution modes: 1. **Blocking (Infinite Wait)** — Task suspends until operation succeeds. Risk: **priority inversion** if not protected by a mutex or priority inheritance. 2. **Non-Blocking (Polling)** — Operation returns immediately with success/fail status. Risk: busy-wait on receive polling wastes CPU. 3. **Timed Blocking (Timeout = T)** — Task suspends for **at most T ticks**, then is awakened with a timeout error. **This is the preferred hard-real-time mode** in safety-critical embedded code (DO-178C, IEC 61508). ## 2.4 Pitfalls and Failure Modes > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU-Valuation Errors:** > - Confusing **mailbox capacity** with **message size** (capacity = number of slots; size = bytes per slot). > - Forgetting that a **mailbox transfers a pointer**, not the actual data — students often incorrectly claim the payload is copied through the mailbox. > - Omitting the **timeout argument** when describing `pend()` — without timeout, a stalled receiver can deadlock the system. ## 2.5 KTU Formula & Design Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` vertical pipe to keep table cells intact. | Parameter / Equation | Expression | Description / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Total Mailbox Memory Footprint | $M_{mbox} = M_{slot} + M_{overhead}$ | Bytes; $M_{slot} = S_{ptr} \times C$ | | Pointer Size (32-bit MCU) | $S_{ptr} = 4$ | Bytes | | Pointer Size (64-bit MCU) | $S_{ptr} = 8$ | Bytes | | Mailbox Capacity | $C$ | Messages (integer, typically $C = 1$ for mailbox, $C > 1$ for queue) | | Per-Object Kernel Overhead | $M_{overhead} \approx 16$ to $32$ | Bytes (wait-list pointers, flags) | | End-to-End IPC Latency | $T_{ipc} = T_{post} + T_{sched} + T_{pend}$ | Microseconds; sum of three phases | | Send API Cost | $T_{post} \approx 0.5$ to $2\,\mu s$ | Time to atomically write slot | | Receive API Cost | $T_{pend} \approx 0.5$ to $2\,\mu s$ | Time to atomically read slot | | Context-Switch Cost | $T_{sched} \approx 1$ to $10\,\mu s$ | Triggered only if a higher-prio task is unblocked | | Maximum Blocking Latency (Worst-Case) | $T_{max} = T_{post} + (N-1) \times T_{cs} + T_{sched}$ | For a chain of N-1 intermediate tasks waking the consumer | | Throughput Bound | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{C}{T_{ipc}}$ | Messages per second (only meaningful if $C > 1$) | | CPU Utilization Overhead | $U_{ipc} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot T_{ipc}}{T_{total}}$ | Fraction of CPU consumed by IPC at arrival rate $\lambda$ | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Mailboxes are the **workhorse IPC primitive** in: - **Automotive ECUs** (CAN stack task ↔ application task communication of diagnostic UDS messages). - **Industrial PLCs** (sensor-acquisition task → control-law task handoff). - **IoT edge devices** (radio ISR ↔ protocol-stack task in BLE/Zigbee stacks). - **Avionics** (ARINC 653 partitioned communication uses mailbox-like sampling/queuing ports). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mailbox Memory Footprint Calculation **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-Mark / 3-Mark):** A 32-bit embedded RTOS (μC/OS-II style) creates a mailbox with a single slot. The pointer size on this MCU is 4 bytes. The kernel allocates a per-mailbox overhead of 24 bytes for wait-list management, event flags, and object name. Compute the **total RAM footprint** of one mailbox object. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the parameters from the problem.** The MCU is 32-bit → pointer width is $S_{ptr} = 4\text{ bytes}$. Capacity (single-slot mailbox) → $C = 1\text{ message}$. Per-object kernel overhead → $M_{overhead} = 24\text{ bytes}$. **Step 2 — Recall the footprint formula.** $$M_{mbox} = M_{slot} + M_{overhead}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the slot memory.** $$M_{slot} = S_{ptr} \times C$$ $$M_{slot} = 4 \text{ bytes} \times 1$$ $$M_{slot} = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the footprint equation.** $$M_{mbox} = 4 \text{ bytes} + 24 \text{ bytes}$$ $$M_{mbox} = 28 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** Each mailbox object consumes **28 bytes of RAM**. If the firmware instantiates 5 such mailboxes, the total IPC RAM cost is $5 \times 28 = 140\text{ bytes}$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** On a Cortex-M0 MCU with 8 KB SRAM, 140 bytes represents only $140/8192 \approx 1.71\%$ of memory — confirming why mailboxes are preferred over message queues when RAM is at a premium. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case IPC Latency with N-Task Wake Chain **Problem Statement:** A sensor-ISR deposits a pointer into a mailbox using `OSMboxPost()`. The post call unblocks Task A (priority 5), which processes the message and `OSMboxPost()`s a derivative message into Mailbox-2, unblocking Task B (priority 6), which in turn posts to Mailbox-3 and unblocks Task C (priority 7). The measured per-API cost is $T_{post} = 1.5\,\mu s$ and $T_{sched} = 3\,\mu s$ (one context switch). Compute the worst-case latency from ISR completion to Task C execution start. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Map the chain.** There are $N = 3$ inter-task hand-offs. The latency model is: $$T_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_{post,i} + T_{sched,i} \right)$$ **Step 2 — Note the assumption.** Per the problem, all post and scheduling costs are uniform. Thus: $$T_{post} = 1.5\,\mu s, \qquad T_{sched} = 3\,\mu s$$ **Step 3 — Apply the latency formula for N hand-offs.** $$T_{max} = N \times \left( T_{post} + T_{sched} \right)$$ $$T_{max} = 3 \times \left( 1.5\,\mu s + 3\,\mu s \right)$$ $$T_{max} = 3 \times 4.5\,\mu s$$ $$T_{max} = 13.5\,\mu s$$ **Step 4 — Compare to the deadline (engineering judgment).** If the control loop must close within $100\,\mu s$, this chain consumes $13.5\%$ of the available budget — leaving $86.5\%$ for actual processing. > [!TIP] > **Design Rule:** When the wake chain length $N$ grows, the latency grows **linearly**. For tight deadlines, collapse the chain by raising the priority of intermediate tasks, or by direct-to-task notification (FreeRTOS-style) to bypass the mailbox hop. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput Bound of a Single-Slot Mailbox **Problem Statement:** A single-slot mailbox has an end-to-end IPC cost of $T_{ipc} = 5\,\mu s$ per message. Determine the maximum steady-state message throughput $\lambda_{max}$ and compare it to a 4-slot message queue operating at the same per-message cost. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Apply the throughput bound formula.** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{C}{T_{ipc}}$$ **Step 2 — Case A: Single-Slot Mailbox (C = 1).** $$\lambda_{max,mbox} = \frac{1}{5\,\mu s} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}\,s} = 2 \times 10^{5}\text{ messages/second}$$ $$\lambda_{max,mbox} = 200{,}000\text{ msg/s}$$ **Step 3 — Case B: 4-Slot Message Queue (C = 4).** $$\lambda_{max,queue} = \frac{4}{5\,\mu s} = 8 \times 10^{5}\text{ messages/second}$$ $$\lambda_{max,queue} = 800{,}000\text{ msg/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the ratio.** $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\lambda_{max,queue}}{\lambda_{max,mbox}} = \frac{4}{1} = 4\times$$ **Step 5 — Conclude.** A 4-slot message queue sustains **4× the throughput** of a 1-slot mailbox, because the queue can absorb bursts while the mailbox forces a strict handshake (every send must wait for a prior receive). However, the queue costs $4 \times$ the memory and increases deterministic latency for the first message. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which IPC Primitive to Use? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Frequently Asks:** "When would you choose a mailbox over a semaphore or message queue?" Use this matrix in your answer. | Criterion | Semaphore | Mailbox (1-slot) | Message Queue (N-slot) | Direct-to-Task Notification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Carries data?** | No (only signal) | Yes (1 pointer) | Yes (N pointers) | Yes (1 word, 32-bit) | | **Synchronization?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Latency** | Lowest | Low | Low–Medium | Lowest | | **Memory** | ~12 B | ~28 B | ~28 + N×4 B | ~12 B | | **Burst tolerance** | None | None | Yes | None | | **Bidirectional** | Yes (binary) | No (unidirectional) | No (unidirectional) | No (unidirectional) | | **ISR-safe** | Yes (give only) | Yes (post only) | Yes (post only) | Yes (give from ISR) | | **Best for** | Resource locking | Task handoff | Stream / log | Lightweight ISR→Task | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Mailbox Architecture (Block Diagram) The diagram below illustrates the **functional architecture** of a single-slot mailbox, showing the kernel-managed object, the producer (Sender Task / ISR), and the consumer (Receiver Task), along with the synchronization wake-up paths. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PRODUCER_SIDE[PRODUCER SIDE] senderTask[Sender Task
Priority 5] isrSource[Hardware ISR
e.g. UART RX] dataBuffer[(Application
Data Buffer
large struct)] end subgraph KERNEL_MAILBOX[KERNEL MAILBOX OBJECT] slotEngine[Mailbox Slot
1 pointer wide] stateFlag[State Flag
EMPTY or FULL] sendWaitList[Send Wait List] recvWaitList[Receive Wait List] end subgraph CONSUMER_SIDE[CONSUMER SIDE] recvTask[Receiver Task
Priority 7] workQueue[(Consumer
Processing Logic)] end senderTask -- OSMboxPost ptr --> slotEngine isrSource -- OSMboxPostFromISR ptr --> slotEngine dataBuffer -. "pointer points here" .-> slotEngine slotEngine -- updates --> stateFlag stateFlag -- EMPTY blocks sender --> sendWaitList stateFlag -- FULL unblocks receiver --> recvWaitList slotEngine -- OSMboxPend returns ptr --> recvTask recvTask --> workQueue workQueue -- dereferences ptr --> dataBuffer recvWaitList -- wakeup --> recvTask sendWaitList -- wakeup --> senderTask ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The dashed line from `dataBuffer` to `slotEngine` represents the **logical link** — the slot only stores a *pointer* (4 bytes on a 32-bit MCU), while the actual data resides in the producer's buffer. This is the zero-copy mechanism. ## 4.2 Mailbox State Machine The mailbox itself transitions between two primary states. The transitions are triggered by send and receive calls and, crucially, depend on the **wait lists**. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> EMPTY_INIT: OSMboxCreate NULL EMPTY_INIT --> EMPTY: pointer initialized EMPTY --> FULL: Post call
slot filled FULL --> EMPTY: Pend call
slot drained FULL --> FULL: Post call
with non-blocking
returns OS_ERR_FULL EMPTY --> EMPTY: Pend call
with non-blocking
returns OS_ERR_PEND FULL --> FULL: Pend call
blocks task
on recv wait list EMPTY --> EMPTY: Post call
blocks task
on send wait list FULL --> [*]: OSMboxDel
wakes waiters
with OS_ERR_DEL EMPTY --> [*]: OSMboxDel
wakes waiters
with OS_ERR_DEL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway from FSM:** A 1-slot mailbox **cannot buffer** — it is a strict rendezvous. The Post and Pend must alternate to make progress. This is why it is used for *synchronization-with-data* rather than streaming. ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — ISR → Task Hand-off via Mailbox ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HW as Hardware Event participant ISR as ISR Handler participant KERN as RTOS Kernel participant MB as Mailbox Object participant T as Low-Prio Worker Task HW->>ISR: Interrupt Asserted activate ISR ISR->>ISR: Read sensor into local buffer ISR->>KERN: OSMboxPostFromISR(MB, &buf) KERN->>MB: Write pointer atomically KERN->>KERN: Mark slot FULL KERN->>KERN: Move T from recv wait list to ready KERN-->>ISR: Return (no context switch from ISR) deactivate ISR KERN->>T: Context switch (post-ISR hook) activate T T->>KERN: OSMboxPend(MB, 0) KERN->>MB: Read pointer, mark EMPTY KERN-->>T: Returns &buf T->>T: Process data deactivate T ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Mailbox in an RTOS context. Mention its capacity and the type of data it transfers. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **mailbox** is a kernel-managed Inter-Process Communication (IPC) object used in RTOS-based embedded systems to pass a **single message** (typically a pointer to a data buffer) between two tasks, or between an ISR and a task. **(1 mark)** Its capacity is **one message slot** — it is essentially a 1-deep queue used as a strict rendezvous point. **(1 mark)** The transferred data unit is a **pointer (4 bytes on 32-bit MCUs, 8 bytes on 64-bit)**, not the actual payload — this is the "zero-copy" mechanism that minimizes RAM use and copy time. **(1 mark)** ### Question 2: Differentiate between a Semaphore and a Mailbox. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Semaphore | Mailbox | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Purpose** | Synchronization / mutual exclusion | Synchronization **+** data transfer | | **Data carried** | None (pure signal/count) | A pointer to a data buffer | | **State variable** | Counter (≥ 0) | State flag (EMPTY / FULL) | | **Typical API** | `OSSemPost` / `OSSemPend` | `OSMboxPost` / `OSMboxPend` | | **Capacity** | N tokens (counting) | 1 message | | **Use case** | Resource lock, event signal | Task handoff with payload | **(3 marks: 1 for purpose, 1 for data distinction, 1 for state/API/capacity contrast.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means OR between two complete questions. Both choices are given below. ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the concept of a **Mailbox** as an IPC mechanism in embedded RTOS. With a neat diagram, describe the **send (post)** and **receive (pend)** operations and explain the role of wait lists. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A **mailbox** is a kernel-managed IPC object that allows a sending task (or ISR) to deposit a single message — implemented as a **pointer to a data buffer** — into a 1-slot buffer, from which a receiving task extracts it. **(1 mark)** **Send (Post) Operation — `OSMboxPost(mbox, pmsg)`:** 1. The kernel disables interrupts (or takes the scheduler lock) to ensure atomicity. **(½ mark)** 2. The kernel checks the slot state. **(½ mark)** 3. If **EMPTY**, the pointer `pmsg` is written into the slot, the state is set to FULL, and any task pending on the receive wait list is moved to the ready queue. A higher-priority ready task triggers an immediate context switch. **(1½ marks)** 4. If **FULL** and the call is non-blocking, an error `OS_ERR_MBOX_FULL` is returned. If blocking, the sender is suspended on the send wait list. **(1 mark)** **Receive (Pend) Operation — `OSMboxPend(mbox, timeout, &err)`:** 1. The kernel atomically reads the slot pointer. **(½ mark)** 2. If **FULL**, the slot is marked EMPTY, and any task pending on the send wait list is released. The pointer is returned to the caller. **(1 mark)** 3. If **EMPTY** and timeout is 0 (non-blocking), an error is returned. If timeout is non-zero, the task is suspended on the receive wait list with a tick countdown. **(1 mark)** **Role of Wait Lists:** The send and receive wait lists decouple the temporal execution of the two tasks. They allow a producer to post when the consumer is not yet ready, and vice versa, with optional timeouts preventing indefinite blocking. The lists are priority-ordered so the kernel can wake the highest-priority waiter first when the slot becomes available. **(½ mark)** **Diagram:** (Student should draw the architecture from Section 4.1 with labeled wait lists.) — **(0 marks awarded if not drawn, but mandatory for full marks)** --- **(b)** A 32-bit MCU uses a single-slot mailbox. Compute the **RAM footprint** of one mailbox object, the **maximum throughput** if the IPC cost is $4\,\mu s$, and discuss **two real-world scenarios** where a mailbox is preferred over a message queue. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. RAM Footprint:** **(2 marks)** - $S_{ptr} = 4$ bytes (32-bit MCU) - $C = 1$ (single slot) - $M_{overhead} = 24$ bytes (typical) - $M_{slot} = S_{ptr} \times C = 4 \times 1 = 4$ bytes - $M_{mbox} = 4 + 24 = 28$ bytes **2. Maximum Throughput:** **(2 marks)** - $\lambda_{max} = C / T_{ipc} = 1 / 4\,\mu s = 250{,}000\text{ msg/s}$ **3. Real-World Scenarios:** **(2 marks)** - **(i) ISR-to-Task handoff with a single sensor sample:** The mailbox carries a pointer to the latest sample, ensuring the consumer always sees the most recent value without buffering — ideal for control loops (e.g., motor speed sensor → PID task). - **(ii) Command dispatch in an automotive ECU:** A diagnostic task sends a single command pointer to a CAN-transmit task. Strict rendezvous semantics prevent stale commands from being re-executed, improving safety. **Conclusion:** A mailbox is preferred when the data is a single coherent state and the application requires tight synchronization rather than buffering. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** With the help of a **state transition diagram**, explain the operation of a **single-slot mailbox** in an RTOS. Clearly mention the four legal transitions and the error returns for illegal operations. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A single-slot mailbox is a 2-state FSM with the states **EMPTY** and **FULL**. The legal transitions are: **(½ mark)** 1. **EMPTY → FULL:** Triggered by a successful `Post` call from a task or ISR. The pointer is stored. **(1 mark)** 2. **FULL → EMPTY:** Triggered by a successful `Pend` call. The pointer is returned and the slot is cleared. **(1 mark)** 3. **EMPTY → EMPTY (blocked):** A `Post` call when the slot is full and blocking is enabled — the sender is suspended on the send wait list. The state does not change; the task is in the wait list. **(1 mark)** 4. **FULL → FULL (blocked):** A `Pend` call when the slot is empty and timeout ≠ 0 — the receiver is suspended on the recv wait list. **(1 mark)** **Error Returns for Illegal Operations:** **(1 mark)** - `Post` on FULL with non-blocking option → `OS_ERR_MBOX_FULL` - `Pend` on EMPTY with timeout = 0 → `OS_ERR_PEND` - `Pend` on EMPTY with timeout expired → `OS_ERR_TIMEOUT` **State Diagram:** (Draw the FSM from Section 4.2 with labels for error returns.) — **(1½ marks)** --- **(b)** Compare **mailbox**, **message queue**, and **semaphore** in terms of **(i)** data carried, **(ii)** capacity, **(iii)** memory footprint, **(iv)** typical use case. Identify the IPC primitive you would choose for an **ultra-low-latency ISR-to-Task notification** on a Cortex-M0 MCU with only 4 KB SRAM. Justify. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Property | Semaphore | Mailbox | Message Queue | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **(i) Data carried** | None (count) | 1 pointer | N pointers | | **(ii) Capacity** | N tokens (counting) | 1 message | N messages | | **(iii) Memory** | ~12 B | ~28 B | ~28 + N×4 B | | **(iv) Use case** | Resource lock, event flag | Task hand-off | Stream buffer | **(3 marks — 1 mark for each property comparison, 1 mark for correct use-case identification.)** **Choice for Cortex-M0 / 4 KB SRAM ISR-to-Task:** **(4 marks)** **Best choice: Direct-to-Task Notification** (semantically equivalent to a 1-word mailbox with no wait list). - **Justification:** - **Memory:** ~12 B per object vs. ~28 B for a mailbox — on a 4 KB MCU, every byte counts. **(1 mark)** - **Latency:** `vTaskNotifyGiveFromISR` is the fastest API, typically <1 µs, since it does not require a wait-list scan or full dispatcher lock. **(1 mark)** - **ISR-safe:** Designed specifically for `FromISR` variants, avoiding critical sections. **(1 mark)** - **Drawback:** Cannot carry a pointer; only a 32-bit word. If a full data buffer pointer is needed, fall back to a single-slot mailbox. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students LOSE MARKS on Mailbox questions:** > 1. **Confusing mailbox with message queue** — failing to specify the 1-slot capacity loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Stating "the mailbox stores the data"** — incorrect. It stores a *pointer*. Examiners penalize this heavily (–2 marks). > 3. **Forgetting the timeout parameter** in `Pend` — leads to claims of "infinite wait", which is unsafe in hard real-time systems. > 4. **Omitting the diagram** — Part B (a) sub-questions almost always require a diagram; without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction. > 5. **Ignoring ISR-safety rules** — calling `OSMboxPost` directly from an ISR (rather than `OSMboxPostFromISR`) is a critical error in many RTOS designs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Mailbox = 1-slot IPC object transferring a POINTER, not the data itself.** - [x] **Two states: EMPTY and FULL.** Transitions are atomic (kernel-locked). - [x] **Three APIs:** Create, Post (send), Pend (receive). Often a Delete API. - [x] **Memory footprint:** $M_{mbox} = S_{ptr} \times C + M_{overhead} \approx 28$ B for a 32-bit, 1-slot mailbox. - [x] **Throughput bound:** $\lambda_{max} = C / T_{ipc}$ — equals 1/$T_{ipc}$ for a 1-slot mailbox. - [x] **Zero-copy mechanism** — large payloads stay in the producer's buffer; only the 4-byte address travels. - [x] **Wait lists are priority-ordered** — kernel wakes the highest-priority waiter first. - [x] **Three operating modes:** Blocking, Non-blocking (polling), Timed-blocking. - [x] **Strict rendezvous** — mailbox cannot buffer; Post and Pend must alternate to make progress. - [x] **ISR-safe variant:** `OSMboxPostFromISR` must be used inside interrupt service routines. - [x] **Use it for:** task hand-off, command dispatch, sensor ISR → control task. **Avoid it for:** logging, streaming, multi-message buffering (use a message queue instead). - [x] **Latency grows linearly** with hand-off chain length N: $T_{max} = N \times (T_{post} + T_{sched})$. - [x] **Key contrast with semaphore:** semaphore carries no data; mailbox does. - [x] **For ultra-low-resource MCUs (Cortex-M0, 4 KB SRAM),** consider direct-to-task notification as a mailbox replacement if the data fits in one 32-bit word.

topics.title: Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Embedded System Design (KTU 2024 Scheme – PEEBT415, Module 4)**, a **Pipe** is defined as a unidirectional, kernel-buffered **Inter-Process Communication (IPC) channel** that allows byte-stream or message-stream data flow between two or more tasks/processes executing in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) or Embedded Linux environment. Pipes are typically classified into **Unnamed (Anonymous) Pipes** and **Named Pipes (FIFOs – First-In-First-Out)**, both of which abstract the underlying producer-consumer synchronization model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A pipe is fundamentally a **FIFO queue** maintained in kernel space, presenting a file-descriptor-like interface to user-space tasks. It obeys the classical **Producer → Consumer** paradigm and is widely used in shell scripting (`ps | grep`) and embedded task pipelines (sensor → filter → actuator).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pipeline** connecting two water tanks:
- **Tank A (Producer)** fills water into the pipe.
- **Tank B (Consumer)** drains water from the other end of the pipe.
- The pipe has a **fixed diameter (buffer size)** and a **one-way flow direction**.

You **cannot send water back** through the same pipe (it is unidirectional), but if you need bidirectional communication, you would lay down **two pipes** side by side. This is exactly how UNIX/embedded pipes operate — a one-way channel for byte streams between processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Metric (Bold):** The default pipe buffer size in Linux is **65,536 bytes (64 KiB)** as defined in `/proc/sys/fs/pipe-max-size`, and the minimum atomic write size is **`PIPE_BUF = 4,096 bytes`** (on most POSIX systems).

### 1.3 Position in the Embedded Stack

| Layer | Component | Role |
|-------|-----------|------|
| Application | User Task / Process | Initiates read/write |
| System Call | `pipe()`, `read()`, `write()` | Kernel interface |
| Kernel | VFS Pipe Driver | Manages wait queues & buffers |
| Hardware | RAM (circular buffer) | Physical storage |
| Synchronization | Mutex / Wait Queue | Producer-Consumer lock |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Pipe

A pipe in an embedded RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, Embedded Linux, VxWorks) follows a strict six-stage operational lifecycle:

1. **Creation** – The `pipe()` system call allocates a pair of file descriptors: one for **reading** (`fd[0]`) and one for **writing** (`fd[1]`).
2. **Hand-off** – The descriptors are passed to two related processes (typically via `fork()`), giving one process the read-end and the other the write-end.
3. **Writing** – The producer calls `write(fd[1], buf, n)`. Data is copied from user space into the kernel pipe buffer.
4. **Buffering** – The kernel maintains a **circular FIFO buffer** (default **64 KiB** in Linux). If the buffer is full, `write()` blocks (or returns `EAGAIN` in non-blocking mode).
5. **Reading** – The consumer calls `read(fd[0], buf, n)`. Data is copied from the kernel buffer into user space.
6. **Termination** – When **all write-ends** are closed, `read()` returns `0` (EOF — End of File).

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Engineering Rationale

- **Why unidirectional?** – Simplifies synchronization; eliminates race conditions on a single channel.
- **Why kernel-buffered?** – Decouples producer/consumer speeds, providing **flow control**.
- **How does blocking work?** – The kernel uses **wait queues**: a write task sleeps on the **writer wait queue** when the buffer is full; a reader sleeps on the **reader wait queue** when the buffer is empty. A kernel scheduler wakes them when conditions change.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|----------------------|------|---------------------|
| Pipe Capacity | $C_{pipe} = B_{size} \times N_{stages}$ | bytes | $B_{size} \le$ `pipe-max-size` (default 65,536 B) |
| Throughput | $T = \dfrac{D_{bytes}}{t_{transfer}}$ | B/s | $D_{bytes} \le PIPE\_BUF$ for atomic writes |
| Latency | $L = t_{write} + t_{queue} + t_{read}$ | seconds | $t_{queue} \ge 0$ |
| Effective Bandwidth | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{atomic} \times PIPE\_BUF}{t_{cycle}}$ | B/s | $N_{atomic} \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| Blocking Write Time | $t_{bw} = \dfrac{B_{full} - B_{free}}{R_{write}}$ | s | $B_{free} = 0 \Rightarrow$ blocks |
| Pipe Speedup (k-stage) | $S_k = \dfrac{n \cdot t_{stage}}{(n + k - 1) \cdot t_{stage}}$ | ratio | $S_{max} = k$ as $n \to \infty$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constant:** The POSIX atomic write threshold is **`PIPE_BUF = 4,096 bytes`**. Writes smaller than this are guaranteed to be **atomic**; larger writes may interleave with concurrent writers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shell pipelines** (`dmesg $\vert$ grep -i error`) — text processing.
- **Sensor Data Acquisition** in IoT — raw sensor bytes piped into a filtering task.
- **Audio/Video Streaming** on embedded boards (Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone).
- **GStreamer pipelines** for multimedia processing on embedded Linux.
- **RTOS Task Communication** in FreeRTOS using stream/message buffers (pipe-like).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Throughput of a Pipe in an Embedded System

**Problem:** A sensor task writes **1,024 bytes** of data into a pipe every **10 ms**, and an actuator task reads the data. Compute the **effective throughput** and the **buffer fill time**.

**Given:**
- Write size: $D_{bytes} = 1{,}024$ bytes
- Write period: $T_{write} = 10 \text{ ms} = 0.01 \text{ s}$
- Pipe buffer size: $B_{size} = 65{,}536$ bytes
- POSIX atomic threshold: $PIPE\_BUF = 4{,}096$ bytes

**Step 1 — Compute Write Rate ($R_{write}$):**
The write rate is the amount of data pushed per unit time:

$$
R_{write} = \frac{D_{bytes}}{T_{write}}
$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$
R_{write} = \frac{1{,}024 \text{ B}}{0.01 \text{ s}} = 102{,}400 \text{ B/s} = 100 \text{ KB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Check Atomicity:**
We compare $D_{bytes}$ with $PIPE\_BUF$:

$$
D_{bytes} = 1{,}024 \text{ B} \;<\; PIPE\_BUF = 4{,}096 \text{ B}
$$

Since the write size is less than the atomic threshold, every write operation is **guaranteed atomic** by the kernel — no interleaving with concurrent writers occurs.

**Step 3 — Compute Effective Throughput ($B_{eff}$):**
Effective throughput equals the write rate (assuming the consumer keeps up):

$$
B_{eff} = R_{write} = 102{,}400 \text{ B/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Buffer Fill Time ($t_{fill}$):**
The time to fully fill the pipe if the consumer halts is:

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{B_{size}}{R_{write}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{65{,}536 \text{ B}}{102{,}400 \text{ B/s}} = 0.640 \text{ s} = 640 \text{ ms}
$$

**Result:** The pipe can absorb approximately **640 ms** of sensor data before blocking, giving the actuator task a **640 ms grace window** to recover from a transient delay.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipeline Speedup for a Multi-Stage Embedded DSP

**Problem:** A 5-stage pipelined DSP processor executes **100 instructions**. Each stage takes **2 ns**. Compute the **speedup** $S_5$ and the **throughput**.

**Given:**
- Number of stages: $k = 5$
- Instructions: $n = 100$
- Stage time: $t_{stage} = 2 \text{ ns}$

**Step 1 — Non-Pipelined Execution Time:**
Without pipelining, every instruction passes through all 5 stages sequentially:

$$
T_{non-pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot t_{stage}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{non-pipe} = 100 \times 5 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 1{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined Execution Time:**
With pipelining, the first instruction takes $k \cdot t_{stage}$ to fill the pipeline, and the remaining $(n - 1)$ instructions emerge at one per cycle:

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot t_{stage}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{pipe} = (5 + 100 - 1) \times 2 \text{ ns} = 104 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 208 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Speedup $S_5$:**

$$
S_5 = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{1{,}000 \text{ ns}}{208 \text{ ns}} \approx 4.808
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Throughput:**
Throughput is instructions completed per unit time:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{n}{T_{pipe}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{100}{208 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \approx 4.81 \times 10^{8} \text{ instructions/s} = 481 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Maximum Speedup:**

$$
S_{max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1} = k = 5
$$

As $n \to \infty$, the speedup approaches the number of pipeline stages $k$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Unnamed Pipe (Anonymous) | Named Pipe (FIFO) |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Identifier | File-descriptor pair | Filesystem pathname |
| Visibility | Parent-child processes only | Any process with permissions |
| Persistence | Exists only while open | Exists as a file in `/tmp` or `~/.` |
| Creation | `int pipe(int fd[2]);` | `int mkfifo(const char *path, mode_t mode);` |
| Use Case | Parent-child IPC | Client-server, daemon logging |
| Lifespan | Until all fds closed | Until unlinked |
| Synchronization | Implicit (blocking) | Explicit (`open()` blocks until peer) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pipe (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Producer Process] --> B[write fd1]
    B --> C[Kernel Pipe Buffer 64KB]
    C --> D[read fd0]
    D --> E[Consumer Process]
    F[Writer Wait Queue] -. blocks .-> B
    G[Reader Wait Queue] -. blocks .-> D
    H[VFS Layer] --> C
    I[Scheduler] --> F
    I --> G
```

### 4.2 Unnamed Pipe Lifecycle (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Task Calls pipe fd] --> S2[Kernel Allocates Buffer 64KB]
    S2 --> S3[Returns fd0 read and fd1 write]
    S3 --> S4{Producer Writes}
    S4 -->|Buffer full| S5[Producer Blocks]
    S5 -->|Consumer reads| S4
    S4 -->|Data available| S6[Consumer Reads]
    S6 -->|No data| S7[Consumer Blocks]
    S7 -->|Producer writes| S6
    S6 -->|All writers close| S8[EOF Return 0]
    S8 --> S9[Pipe Destroyed]
```

### 4.3 5-Stage Embedded Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Instruction 1] --> IF1[IF Fetch]
    IF1 --> ID1[ID Decode]
    ID1 --> EX1[EX Execute]
    EX1 --> MEM1[MEM Access]
    MEM1 --> WB1[WB Writeback]

    I2[Instruction 2] --> IF2[IF Fetch]
    IF2 --> ID2[ID Decode]
    ID2 --> EX2[EX Execute]
    EX2 --> MEM2[MEM Access]
    MEM2 --> WB2[WB Writeback]

    I3[Instruction 3] --> IF3[IF Fetch]
    IF3 --> ID3[ID Decode]
    ID3 --> EX3[EX Execute]
    EX3 --> MEM3[MEM Access]
    MEM3 --> WB3[WB Writeback]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In a 5-stage pipeline, after the first **5 cycles**, one instruction completes per cycle, achieving the maximum throughput of $\dfrac{1}{t_{stage}}$ instructions per second.

### 4.4 Producer–Consumer Synchronization Flow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer Task
    participant K as Kernel Pipe Buffer
    participant C as Consumer Task
    P->>K: write 1024 bytes
    K-->>P: ACK success
    P->>K: write 1024 bytes
    Note over K: Buffer 50 percent full
    C->>K: read 2048 bytes
    K-->>C: returns 2048 bytes
    Note over P,C: Asynchronous flow control
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 2-Mark Conceptual Questions (Module Level)

**A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define a pipe. List its two main types.*

> **Model Answer (2 Marks):**  
> A **pipe** is a kernel-buffered, unidirectional communication channel used for inter-process communication in an RTOS or embedded Linux. It obeys the **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** discipline.  
> **Two types:** (i) **Unnamed (Anonymous) Pipe** – used between related processes (e.g., parent-child). (ii) **Named Pipe (FIFO)** – identified by a filesystem path; allows unrelated processes to communicate.

**A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*What is `PIPE_BUF`? Why is it significant?*

> **Model Answer (2 Marks):**  
> `PIPE_BUF` (typically **4,096 bytes** on Linux) is the maximum number of bytes that the kernel guarantees to write **atomically** into a pipe. Significance: writes $\le PIPE\_BUF$ are guaranteed not to interleave with concurrent writers, ensuring **data integrity** in multi-producer scenarios.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture and working** of an unnamed pipe in an embedded RTOS. With a neat diagram, describe the `pipe()`, `read()`, and `write()` system calls. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A sensor task writes **2,048 bytes** of data to a pipe every **5 ms**, and the actuator task reads the data continuously. Given `PIPE_BUF = 4,096` bytes and buffer size = 65,536 bytes, compute: (i) the **write rate** (ii) the **buffer fill time** if the consumer halts (iii) whether the writes are **atomic**. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture & Working (7 Marks):**

An **unnamed pipe** is created using the `pipe()` system call:

```c
int fd[2];
pipe(fd);  // fd[0] = read end, fd[1] = write end
```

**Step-by-step working:**

1. **Creation:** Kernel allocates a 64 KiB circular buffer and returns two file descriptors.
2. **Forking:** A child process inherits both descriptors via `fork()`.
3. **Communication:** Parent writes to `fd[1]`, child reads from `fd[0]`.
4. **Synchronization:** The pipe blocks writes when the buffer is full and blocks reads when the buffer is empty.
5. **Termination:** When all write-ends close, `read()` returns 0 (EOF).
6. **Cleanup:** When all descriptors close, the buffer is deallocated.

**Architecture Diagram (textual):**

```
   Parent Process                 Kernel                Child Process
   [fd[1] WRITE]   ---->    [Circular Buffer]    ---->   [fd[0] READ]
                          (64 KiB FIFO Queue)
```

> (Full marks require explicit mention of: file descriptor pair, kernel buffering, blocking semantics, and EOF condition.)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $D_{bytes} = 2{,}048$ B, $T_{write} = 5$ ms = 0.005 s, $B_{size} = 65{,}536$ B, $PIPE\_BUF = 4{,}096$ B.

**(i) Write Rate:**

$$
R_{write} = \frac{D_{bytes}}{T_{write}} = \frac{2{,}048 \text{ B}}{0.005 \text{ s}} = 409{,}600 \text{ B/s} = 400 \text{ KB/s}
$$

**(ii) Buffer Fill Time:**

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{B_{size}}{R_{write}} = \frac{65{,}536 \text{ B}}{409{,}600 \text{ B/s}} = 0.160 \text{ s} = 160 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Atomicity Check:**

$$
D_{bytes} = 2{,}048 \text{ B} \;<\; PIPE\_BUF = 4{,}096 \text{ B} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Atomic} \;\checkmark
$$

**Result:** Writes are **atomic**; the consumer has a **160 ms** recovery window.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **unnamed pipes and named pipes (FIFOs)** in detail. Mention any two use cases of each in embedded systems. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain a **5-stage instruction pipeline** used in an embedded RISC processor. Compute the **speedup** for executing **200 instructions** if each stage takes **1.5 ns**. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Unnamed Pipe | Named Pipe (FIFO) |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| Naming | Anonymous | Filesystem pathname |
| Scope | Related processes | Unrelated processes |
| Creation | `pipe(fd)` | `mkfifo(path, mode)` |
| Visibility | Kernel-internal | Visible in `ls -l` |
| Lifespan | Until closed | Until `unlink()` |
| Use Case 1 | `fork()` IPC | Daemon logging |
| Use Case 2 | Shell `$\vert$` | Client-server messaging |

**Embedded use cases:** Unnamed – sensor-to-filter task handoff in FreeRTOS. Named – log rotation in `/var/log/embedded.log` on Raspberry Pi.

**(b) 5-Stage Pipeline (7 Marks):**

**Stages:** IF (Instruction Fetch) → ID (Instruction Decode) → EX (Execute) → MEM (Memory Access) → WB (Writeback).

**Given:** $n = 200$, $k = 5$, $t_{stage} = 1.5$ ns.

**Non-pipelined time:**

$$
T_{non-pipe} = n \times k \times t_{stage} = 200 \times 5 \times 1.5 \text{ ns} = 1{,}500 \text{ ns}
$$

**Pipelined time:**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \times t_{stage} = (5 + 199) \times 1.5 \text{ ns} = 306 \text{ ns}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
S_5 = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{1{,}500}{306} \approx 4.902
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget the **`(k - 1)`** fill-cycle overhead in the pipelined time formula. Writing $T_{pipe} = n \cdot t_{stage}$ instead of $T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot t_{stage}$ **loses 2 marks**. Always account for the pipeline startup latency.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pipe** = kernel-buffered, unidirectional FIFO IPC channel.
- **Two types:** Unnamed (anonymous, parent-child) and Named (FIFO, filesystem path).
- **Default buffer size:** **65,536 bytes (64 KiB)** in Linux.
- **Atomic write threshold:** `PIPE_BUF = 4,096` bytes.
- **Blocking semantics:** Writers block when full; readers block when empty.
- **EOF signal:** `read()` returns **0** when all write-ends are closed.
- **Pipeline speedup formula:** $S_k = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$, approaches $k$ for large $n$.
- **Throughput formula:** $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{t_{stage}}$ instructions/second at steady state.
- **POSIX standard guarantees** atomicity for writes $\le PIPE\_BUF$.
- **Embedded use cases:** Sensor pipelines, GStreamer, RTOS task communication, shell scripting.
- **Key APIs:** `pipe()`, `read()`, `write()`, `close()`, `mkfifo()`, `open()`.
- **Always mention** file descriptor pair (`fd[0]` for read, `fd[1]` for write) in exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sockets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sockets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Socket** is a software abstraction that serves as an endpoint for inter-process communication (IPC) or network communication between two nodes (which may reside on the same machine or across a network). In the context of **Embedded System Design**, a socket is the fundamental Application Programming Interface (API) construct provided by the **TCP/IP protocol stack** (typically implemented in the OS kernel, e.g., Embedded Linux, RTOS with networking stack like lwIP, or Contiki) that allows embedded applications to exchange data using standard Internet protocols (TCP/UDP over IPv4/IPv6).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** A socket is a combination of an **IP address** (logical network address of the host) and a **port number** (a 16-bit identifier of a specific process/application on that host), uniquely identifying an endpoint of a bidirectional communication channel.

Mathematically, a socket endpoint can be represented as the ordered tuple:

$$\text{Socket} = \{\text{IP}_{\text{local}},\, \text{Port}_{\text{local}},\, \text{IP}_{\text{remote}},\, \text{Port}_{\text{remote}},\, \text{Protocol}\}$$

For connectionless UDP communication, the 5-tuple is essential; for connection-oriented TCP, a unique connection is identified by the same 5-tuple.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Postal/Postal-Code" Intuition

Imagine two offices (embedded devices) located in different cities (networks) want to exchange letters (data packets):

| Real-World Concept | Socket Equivalent | Technical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| City name + Pincode | **IP Address** | Logical address of the device on the network (e.g., `192.168.1.10`) |
| Office desk / department number | **Port Number** | Identifies the specific application/service (e.g., port `80` = Web Server) |
| The letter box / reception desk | **Socket (file descriptor)** | The actual communication endpoint inside the OS |
| Registered post with acknowledgment | **TCP Socket (`SOCK_STREAM`)** | Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented |
| Ordinary post (no acknowledgment) | **UDP Socket (`SOCK_DGRAM`)** | Fast, connectionless, best-effort delivery |

So a socket is essentially the **"letterbox"** of an application — a standardized interface through which the application posts outgoing data and receives incoming data, without worrying about the physical wiring (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, CAN, etc.) underneath.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sockets matter in embedded systems:** Modern IoT edge devices (smart sensors, industrial gateways, wearables) MUST communicate with cloud servers, mobile apps, and other devices. Sockets provide the **portable, OS-independent abstraction** that lets embedded firmware programmers write network code once and run it across diverse hardware (ARM Cortex-M + lwIP, ARM Cortex-A + Linux, ESP32 + FreeRTOS, etc.).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are **fixed by protocol standards (IANA, RFC 791, RFC 793, RFC 768)** and must be memorized for KTU exams:

- **Port Number Size:** **16 bits (2 bytes)**, unsigned, range = **0 to 65535**.
- **IP Address (IPv4) Size:** **32 bits (4 bytes)**, typically written in **dotted-decimal** notation (e.g., `192.168.1.1`).
- **IP Address (IPv6) Size:** **128 bits (16 bytes)**, written in **colon-hexadecimal** notation (e.g., `2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334`).
- **Maximum Segment Size (MSS) for TCP:** typically **1460 bytes** (Ethernet MTU 1500 − 20 IP header − 20 TCP header).
- **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) for Ethernet:** **1500 bytes**.
- **Well-Known Ports (0–1023):** Reserved for privileged services (HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53).
- **Registered Ports (1024–49151):** Assigned by IANA to user applications.
- **Dynamic/Private Ports (49152–65535):** Used by client-side ephemeral connections.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pearl:** Always mention **"a socket is identified by an IP + Port pair"** in the first sentence of any KTU answer on sockets. This is the universal opening line that earns instant partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sockets (Operational Taxonomy)

Sockets are classified along **two orthogonal axes** — the **address family** (which protocol suite) and the **socket type** (which communication semantics).

### A. By Address Family (Domain)

| Domain Constant | Full Form | Address Size | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| `AF_INET` | Address Family – Internet (IPv4) | 32-bit IP | Internet / LAN communication in IPv4 |
| `AF_INET6` | Address Family – Internet (IPv6) | 128-bit IP | Modern IoT, IPv6 networks |
| `AF_UNIX` / `AF_LOCAL` | Address Family – Unix domain | Filesystem pathname | Inter-process communication on the **same** embedded board (e.g., between two Linux processes on a Raspberry Pi gateway) |
| `AF_PACKET` | Address Family – Packet (Raw) | N/A | Bypassing TCP/IP — used for custom low-level protocols (rare in apps) |

### B. By Socket Type (Communication Semantics)

| Type Constant | Protocol | Reliability | Order | Connection | Use-Case in Embedded Systems |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `SOCK_STREAM` | TCP | **Guaranteed** (ACK + retransmission) | **Preserved** (sequence numbers) | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Firmware updates (OTA), MQTT over TCP, HTTP, SSH |
| `SOCK_DGRAM` | UDP | **Best-effort** (no ACK) | **Not guaranteed** | Connectionless | DNS queries, VoIP, real-time sensor streaming, CoAP, NTP, TFTP |
| `SOCK_RAW` | IP/ICMP direct | Application-defined | N/A | N/A | Building custom protocols, ping (ICMP) |
| `SOCK_SEQPACKET` | SCTP | Reliable | Preserved | Connection-oriented | Telecom signaling (rare in embedded) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The 3-way TCP handshake (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`) happens **inside the kernel** and is completely transparent to the application. The application only sees the `connect()` call returning success/failure.

## 2.2 The Generic Socket Address Structure (`struct sockaddr`)

To make the socket API uniform across address families, the kernel uses a **generic container** that is later cast to a family-specific structure.

```c
struct sockaddr {
    sa_family_t sa_family;    // Address family (AF_INET, AF_INET6, AF_UNIX)
    char        sa_data[14];  // Protocol-specific address (opaque 14 bytes)
};
```

The practical IPv4-specific structure used by programmers is:

```c
struct sockaddr_in {
    sa_family_t     sin_family;   // AF_INET
    in_port_t       sin_port;     // 16-bit port (NETWORK byte order)
    struct in_addr  sin_addr;     // 32-bit IP  (NETWORK byte order)
    char            sin_zero[8];  // Padding (unused, set to 0)
};
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The padding rule** is critical: `sizeof(sockaddr_in) == sizeof(sockaddr) == 16 bytes`. This allows the cast `(struct sockaddr *)&my_addr_in` to be type-safe at the C level, which the kernel inspects via `sa_family` to interpret the rest of the bytes correctly.

## 2.3 Byte-Ordering Conversions — The "Endianness Problem"

Different CPU architectures store multi-byte integers in different orders:
- **Big-Endian:** MSB at lowest address (network standard, used by Motorola 68K, older PowerPC).
- **Little-Endian:** LSB at lowest address (used by x86, ARM Cortex-M/A in most configs).

The TCP/IP protocols **mandate** that all multi-byte header fields (port numbers, IP addresses, sequence numbers) be transmitted in **Network Byte Order = Big-Endian**.

The BSD sockets API therefore provides four conversion functions, and they are **mandatory** in every KTU socket-programming answer:

| Function | Purpose | Direction | Example Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `htons()` | Host-TO-Network Short | Host (any endian) → Big-endian | Converting `sin_port` |
| `htonl()` | Host-TO-Network Long | Host → Big-endian | Converting `sin_addr.s_addr` |
| `ntohs()` | Network-TO-Host Short | Big-endian → Host | Reading received port |
| `ntohl()` | Network-TO-Host Long | Big-endian → Host | Reading received IP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Short" and "Long" instead of "16-bit" and "32-bit"?** Because the C `short` and `long` sizes vary by platform. The functions adapt to whatever the host defines as `short` and `long`, which historically corresponded to 16 and 32 bits. On modern 64-bit Linux, `short` is still 16 bits but `long` is 64 bits — so always use `htonl` for IP addresses and `htons` for port numbers (not the other way around).

## 2.4 The Berkeley Sockets API — Server-Side and Client-Side Call Sequence

### A. TCP Server (Connection-Oriented) — Five Mandatory Calls

The server's life-cycle is **strictly sequential** (each call depends on the previous one returning a valid file descriptor):

1. **`socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)`** — Create the endpoint. Returns an integer file descriptor `server_fd` (a small non-negative integer, e.g., 3, 4, 5...).
2. **`bind(server_fd, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr))`** — Attach the socket to a specific IP + Port on the local machine. After `bind()`, the OS knows this process owns this address.
3. **`listen(server_fd, backlog)`** — Mark the socket as **passive** (willing to accept incoming connection requests). The `backlog` parameter (typically 5–128) defines the queue length for pending connections.
4. **`accept(server_fd, ...)`** — **Blocks** the calling thread until a client `connect()` arrives. Returns a **new file descriptor** `client_fd` dedicated to that specific connection. The original `server_fd` continues listening.
5. **`recv(client_fd, buf, len, 0)` / `send(client_fd, buf, len, 0)`** — Exchange data. These calls block (in default mode) until data is available / sent.
6. **`close(client_fd)`** — Terminate the specific client connection.
7. **`close(server_fd)`** — Shut down the listener (usually in the cleanup / signal handler).

### B. TCP Client

1. **`socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)`** → `client_fd`
2. **`connect(client_fd, (struct sockaddr*)&server_addr, sizeof(server_addr))`** — Initiates the TCP 3-way handshake internally. Returns 0 on success.
3. **`send()` / `recv()`** — Data exchange.
4. **`close(client_fd)`** — Tear down the connection (initiates the 4-way FIN handshake).

### C. UDP (Server & Client) — No Connection

- **Server:** `socket()` → `bind()` → loop on `recvfrom()` → `sendto()` → `close()`.
- **Client:** `socket()` → optionally `bind()` (for receiving replies) → `sendto()` → `recvfrom()` → `close()`.
- There is **no `listen()`, no `accept()`, no `connect()`** in UDP — the sender simply stamps the destination address onto each packet via `sendto()`.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Port number range | $0 \le P \le 2^{16} - 1$ | $0$ to **65535** (unsigned 16-bit) |
| 2 | Number of well-known ports | $2^{10} = 1024$ | $0$–$1023$ |
| 3 | Number of registered ports | $2^{15} - 2^{10} = 30720$ | $1024$–$49151$ |
| 4 | Number of dynamic ports | $2^{16} - 2^{15} = 16384$ | $49152$–$65535$ |
| 5 | IPv4 address count | $2^{32}$ | $\approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ addresses |
| 6 | IPv6 address count | $2^{128}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |
| 7 | IPv4 dotted-decimal byte | $0 \le B \le 255$ (8 bits) | Decimal value of each dotted byte |
| 8 | Dotted-decimal → 32-bit integer | $I = B_1 \cdot 2^{24} + B_2 \cdot 2^{16} + B_3 \cdot 2^{8} + B_4$ | e.g., `192.168.1.1` = `0xC0A80101` |
| 9 | Ethernet MTU (typical) | $\text{MTU} = 1500$ bytes | Including IP header |
| 10 | TCP MSS (typical) | $\text{MSS} = \text{MTU} - 40 = 1460$ bytes | $= 1500 - 20_{\text{IP}} - 20_{\text{TCP}}$ |
| 11 | Maximum TCP payload / packet | $\le 1460$ bytes (MSS) | Without IP/TCP headers |
| 12 | UDP header size | $8$ bytes | Fixed |
| 13 | TCP header size | $20$–$60$ bytes | $20$ base + options |
| 14 | IPv4 header size | $20$–$60$ bytes | $20$ base + options |
| 15 | Special IP — loopback | `127.0.0.1` (or `127.x.x.x`) | Refers to **local host** itself |
| 16 | Special IP — any (bind) | `INADDR_ANY = 0.0.0.0` | Server binds to all local interfaces |
| 17 | listen() backlog guidance | $\text{backlog} \approx 1.5 \times N_{\text{expected\_peak\_clients}}$ | Empirical, kernel-dependent |
| 18 | Round-trip packets for TCP open | $3$ (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | The 3-way handshake |
| 19 | Round-trip packets for TCP close | $4$ (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) | The 4-way teardown |
| 20 | Half-open connection detection | Sequence-number tracking via keep-alive | TCP keepalive timer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real embedded systems (e.g., a temperature sensor reporting every 10 ms), if the payload per sample is 8 bytes, you can fit up to $\lfloor 1460 / 8 \rfloor = 182$ samples per TCP segment — useful when sizing the application buffer in `recv()`.

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Embedded Engineering

| Embedded Application | Socket Type | Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| OTA firmware update | TCP | HTTP/MQTT | Cannot tolerate packet loss — brick risk |
| Real-time audio streaming | UDP | RTP | Latency > reliability for voice |
| Smart meter → cloud | TCP or UDP | MQTT (TCP) / CoAP (UDP) | Trade-off between bandwidth and reliability |
| Industrial robot → PLC | TCP | Modbus-TCP, EtherNet/IP | Determinism, no retransmission storms |
| IoT sensor → gateway | UDP | CoAP over DTLS | Low power, intermittent connectivity |
| Local IPC on gateway | Unix domain | AF_UNIX | Faster than loopback TCP, no IP header overhead |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting IPv4 Dotted-Decimal to 32-Bit Network Byte Order

This is a routine **2-mark KTU question** and a critical practical skill.

**Given:** A dotted-decimal IPv4 address $A = B_1.B_2.B_3.B_4$ (e.g., `192.168.1.10`).

**To find:** The 32-bit integer value $N$ (in hexadecimal or decimal) suitable for assignment to `sin_addr.s_addr` *after* `htonl()`.

### Step 1 — State the bit-weighting formula

Each byte $B_i$ in the dotted-decimal occupies 8 bits. Since IPv4 is big-endian on the wire, the leftmost byte is the **most significant** (highest-order 8 bits).

$$
N \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{4} B_i \cdot 2^{8 \cdot (4 - i)}
$$

The exponents for a 32-bit address are: $24, 16, 8, 0$.

### Step 2 — Substitute the values for `192.168.1.10`

Let $B_1 = 192$, $B_2 = 168$, $B_3 = 1$, $B_4 = 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 192 \cdot 2^{24} \;+\; 168 \cdot 2^{16} \;+\; 1 \cdot 2^{8} \;+\; 10 \cdot 2^{0} \\
&= 192 \cdot 16\,777\,216 \;+\; 168 \cdot 65\,536 \;+\; 1 \cdot 256 \;+\; 10 \cdot 1 \\
&= 3\,221\,225\,472 \;+\; 11\,010\,048 \;+\; 256 \;+\; 10 \\
&= 3\,232\,235\,786
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to hexadecimal (for in-code use)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{hex}} &= 0x\text{C0} \cdot 0x01000000 \;+\; 0x\text{A8} \cdot 0x00010000 \;+\; 0x01 \cdot 0x00000100 \;+\; 0x0A \cdot 0x00000001 \\
&= 0x\text{C0A8010A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\texttt{inet\_aton("192.168.1.10", \&addr)} \rightarrow \texttt{addr.s\_addr} = 0xC0A8010A = 3\,232\,235\,786$ ✓

### Step 4 — Code representation (C, Linux)

```c
struct in_addr ip;
inet_pton(AF_INET, "192.168.1.10", &ip);  // Library does htonl() internally
// OR manual:
// ip.s_addr = htonl( (192UL << 24) | (168UL << 16) | (1UL << 8) | 10UL );
```

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why `INADDR_ANY` (`0.0.0.0`) Binds to All Interfaces

### Step 1 — Multi-homed device context

An embedded gateway (e.g., a Raspberry Pi running Linux with a Wi-Fi STA interface `wlan0 = 192.168.1.50` and an Ethernet interface `eth0 = 10.0.0.5`) has **two IP addresses**. If the server program binds to `192.168.1.50:8080`, clients on `10.0.0.x` cannot reach it.

### Step 2 — Socket API behavior

When `bind()` is called with `sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY)` (which is just `0x00000000`), the kernel installs the listening socket on the **wildcard** address. Every incoming TCP segment whose destination IP matches **any** local interface is delivered to this socket.

### Step 3 — Mathematical interpretation

For a packet with destination IP $D$ arriving at the host, the kernel evaluates:

$$
\text{Deliver to socket} \iff \big( D = S_{\text{bind}} \big) \;\lor\; \big( S_{\text{bind}} = \text{INADDR\_ANY} \big)
$$

That is, either the packet is destined to the bound address, **or** the socket is wildcard-bound (accepts everything).

### Step 4 — Practical consequence

| Server binds to... | Reachable from `192.168.1.0/24` | Reachable from `10.0.0.0/24` |
|---|---|---|
| `INADDR_ANY` (0.0.0.0) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.1.50` only | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (RST or no response) |
| `127.0.0.1` only | ❌ Only from same device | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always recommend `INADDR_ANY` for production embedded servers, **unless** security policy requires the service to be exposed on only one network (e.g., a management interface).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TCP MSS from MTU — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A sensor node uses an Ethernet PHY with MTU = 1500 bytes. It runs IPv4 + TCP.

**To find:** Maximum bytes of application payload per TCP segment, and how many sensor readings (each 16 bytes) can be packed per segment.

### Step 1 — Subtract IPv4 header

IPv4 header is normally 20 bytes (no options).

$$
L_{\text{IP\_payload}} = \text{MTU} - L_{\text{IP\_header}} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract TCP header

TCP header is normally 20 bytes (no options, no padding).

$$
\text{MSS} = L_{\text{IP\_payload}} - L_{\text{TCP\_header}} = 1480 - 20 = 1460 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute number of sensor readings per segment

Each reading is 16 bytes. We need the floor of the division.

$$
N_{\text{readings}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{MSS}}{L_{\text{reading}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1460}{16} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 91.25 \right\rfloor = 91 \text{ readings}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute remaining buffer (to avoid partial-read bugs)

$$
L_{\text{remainder}} = \text{MSS} - N_{\text{readings}} \cdot L_{\text{reading}} = 1460 - 91 \cdot 16 = 1460 - 1456 = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** If the embedded app expects exactly 16-byte readings, the 4 leftover bytes will sit in the kernel buffer until the next segment arrives (or a timer expires). Programmers must design their `recv()` loop to handle **partial reads** — TCP is a **stream** protocol, not a **datagram** protocol. This is a classic KTU exam pitfall.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TCP vs UDP Sockets (Board-Exam Ready)

| Parameter | TCP Socket (`SOCK_STREAM`) | UDP Socket (`SOCK_DGRAM`) |
|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | Mandatory (`connect()` / `accept()`) | None — send directly with address |
| Reliability | Guaranteed (ACK + retransmit + sequence) | Not guaranteed (fire-and-forget) |
| Order | Preserved | Not preserved — packets may arrive out-of-order |
| Flow control | Yes — sliding window (receiver advertises window) | No |
| Congestion control | Yes — slow start, cwnd, AIMD, Reno/Cubic | No |
| Header overhead | 20+ bytes (TCP) | 8 bytes (UDP) |
| Bandwidth efficiency (low payload, many pkts) | Lower (overhead heavy) | Higher |
| Latency | Higher (handshake + ACK) | Lower (no handshake) |
| Stateful? | Yes — kernel TCB per connection | No — each datagram independent |
| Max payload / datagram | Limited by MSS (~1460 B) | Limited by MTU − 28 = 1472 B (theoretical), often 512–8192 B in practice |
| `send()` semantics | May buffer, may block, may partial-send | One call = one datagram (or error) |
| `recv()` semantics | Returns any number of bytes (1 to MSS) | Returns **one complete datagram** per call (if buffer large enough) |
| API calls (server) | `socket, bind, listen, accept, recv, send, close` | `socket, bind, recvfrom, sendto, close` |
| API calls (client) | `socket, connect, send, recv, close` | `socket, sendto, recvfrom, close` |
| Use in embedded | OTA updates, MQTT, HTTP, SSH | DNS, NTP, TFTP, CoAP, RTP/VoIP |
| OSI layer of API | L4 (Transport) | L4 (Transport) |
| Example KTU question weight | More common (8–10 mark questions) | Common (4–6 mark comparisons) |

---

## 3.5 Algorithm: TCP Server Pseudocode (Mark-Worthy)

```c
int server_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);          // (1) Create
struct sockaddr_in addr = {0};
addr.sin_family      = AF_INET;
addr.sin_port        = htons(8080);                       // Big-endian port
addr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY);                 // 0.0.0.0
bind(server_fd, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr));   // (2) Bind
listen(server_fd, 5);                                     // (3) Listen, backlog 5
while (1) {
    struct sockaddr_in client;
    socklen_t len = sizeof(client);
    int client_fd = accept(server_fd, (struct sockaddr*)&client, &len);  // (4) Block
    char buf[1024];
    int n = recv(client_fd, buf, sizeof(buf), 0);         // (5) Receive
    send(client_fd, buf, n, 0);                           //     Echo back
    close(client_fd);                                     // (6) Close
}
```

**Call-by-call validation table for KTU:**

| Call | Returns on success | Returns on error | Common errno |
|---|---|---|---|
| `socket()` | non-negative FD | $-1$ | `EACCES`, `EAFNOSUPPORT` |
| `bind()` | $0$ | $-1$ | `EADDRINUSE`, `EACCES` (port < 1024 without root) |
| `listen()` | $0$ | $-1$ | `EADDRINUSE` |
| `accept()` | non-negative FD | $-1$ | `EAGAIN` (non-blocking), `ECONNABORTED` |
| `connect()` | $0$ | $-1$ | `ECONNREFUSED`, `ETIMEDOUT` |
| `recv()` | bytes received (can be 0 = EOF) | $-1$ | `ECONNRESET` |
| `send()` | bytes sent | $-1$ | `EPIPE` (after remote close) |
| `close()` | $0$ | $-1$ | `EBADF` |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — TCP Server–Client Lifecycle (Mandatory 6-7 Mark Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0A([Embedded Application Start]) --> S1A[Step 1: socket AF_INET SOCK_STREAM 0]
    S1A --> S2A[Step 2: Prepare sockaddr_in: family, htons port, htonl addr]
    S2A --> S3A[Step 3: bind server_fd to local IP:Port]
    S3A --> S4A[Step 4: listen server_fd backlog 5]
    S4A --> S5A{Step 5: accept loop - blocking call}
    S5A -- new TCP connection --> S6A[Step 6: accept returns client_fd]
    S6A --> S7A[Step 7: recv from client_fd into buffer]
    S7A --> S8A[Step 8: process / handle request]
    S8A --> S9A[Step 9: send response to client_fd]
    S9A --> S10A[Step 10: close client_fd]
    S10A --> S5A
    S5A -- SIGINT / shutdown signal --> S11A[Cleanup: close server_fd, exit]

    C0A([Client Application Start]) --> C1A[Step 1: socket AF_INET SOCK_STREAM 0]
    C1A --> C2A[Step 2: Prepare server sockaddr_in]
    C2A --> C3A{Step 3: connect to server}
    C3A -- success / 0 --> C4A[Step 4: send request to server_fd]
    C3A -- failure / -1 --> C3ERR[Print error: ECONNREFUSED or ETIMEDOUT]
    C3ERR --> C0A
    C4A --> C5A[Step 5: recv response from server]
    C5A --> C6A[Step 6: process response]
    C6A --> C7A[Step 7: close client_fd]
    C7A --> C8A([End])

    S6A -.->|TCP 3-way handshake| C3A
```

flowchart LR
