## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — TCP 3-Way Handshake State Transition (Conceptual)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: client connect()
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: rcvd SYN-ACK, sent ACK
    CLOSED --> LISTEN: server listen()
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD: rcvd SYN, sent SYN-ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED: rcvd ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: close()
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: rcvd ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: rcvd FIN, sent ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> [*]: 2MSL timeout
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT: rcvd FIN, sent ACK
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK: close()
    LAST_ACK --> [*]: rcvd ACK
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Slot 1]`

**Define a socket. With a neat diagram, explain the 5-tuple that uniquely identifies a TCP connection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~150 words):**

A **socket** is the endpoint of a bidirectional communication channel between two processes running on a network. It is the abstraction provided by the **BSD Sockets API** to allow applications to send/receive data over TCP/IP without managing the protocol details.

A unique **TCP connection** is identified by the following 5-tuple (an ordered set of 5 values):

$$
\text{Connection} = \{\text{Source IP},\, \text{Source Port},\, \text{Dest IP},\, \text{Dest Port},\, \text{Protocol = TCP}\}
$$

| Component | Size | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Source IP | 32 / 128 bit | `192.168.1.10` |
| Source Port | 16 bit | `54321` (ephemeral) |
| Dest IP | 32 / 128 bit | `93.184.216.34` |
| Dest Port | 16 bit | `80` (HTTP) |
| Protocol | 8 bit | `6` (TCP) |

This 5-tuple is stored in the kernel's **Transmission Control Block (TCB)** for every active connection. **1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for 5-tuple list, **1 mark** for example / TCB remark.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Slot 2]`

**Differentiate between `SOCK_STREAM` and `SOCK_DGRAM` socket types. Give one embedded-systems use-case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~120 words):**

| Feature | `SOCK_STREAM` (TCP) | `SOCK_DGRAM` (UDP) |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery via ACK + retransmit | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Order | Sequence numbers preserve order | May arrive out of order |
| Header overhead | 20+ bytes | 8 bytes |
| Latency | Higher (handshake, ACK) | Lower (send immediately) |
| API | `connect`/`accept`/`send`/`recv` | `sendto`/`recvfrom` |

**Embedded use-cases:**
- **`SOCK_STREAM` →** Firmware Over-The-Air (OTA) update of an ESP32 sensor node via HTTPS. Reliability is critical because a lost packet can corrupt the firmware and brick the device.
- **`SOCK_DGRAM` →** Publishing real-time temperature/humidity readings from a wireless sensor to a CoAP gateway every 1 second. A lost sample is acceptable; latency matters more.

**1 mark** for each socket-type description (3 bullet points), **1 mark** for use-cases (½ mark each).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 4]`

**(a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the complete TCP server-side socket programming sequence using the BSD Sockets API. List every API call along with its purpose, parameters, and return value.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the `sockaddr_in` structure in detail. Why is byte-order conversion (`htons`, `htonl`) necessary, and what happens if you forget to call it on a little-endian host?** **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a) — TCP Server Sequence (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Create the socket (1 mark)**

```c
int server_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
```
- **Purpose:** Allocate a new socket in the kernel and return a file descriptor.
- **Parameters:** `AF_INET` (IPv4), `SOCK_STREAM` (TCP), `0` (let OS pick TCP from the family).
- **Return:** Non-negative integer FD on success; $-1$ on failure (errno set).

**Step 2 — Construct and bind the address (1.5 marks)**

```c
struct sockaddr_in addr;
memset(&addr, 0, sizeof(addr));        // Zero-initialize (sin_zero)
addr.sin_family      = AF_INET;
addr.sin_port        = htons(8080);
addr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY);
bind(server_fd, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr));
```
- **Purpose:** Reserve port 8080 on all local interfaces for this process.
- **Why `htons(8080)`:** Network byte order is big-endian; most embedded CPUs (ARM, x86) are little-endian, so we must convert.
- **Why `INADDR_ANY`:** So the server accepts clients on any local IP.

**Step 3 — Listen (1 mark)**

```c
listen(server_fd, 5);
```
- Marks the socket as a **passive** listener. Backlog = 5 (max pending connections in the queue).
- Returns 0 on success, $-1$ on failure.

**Step 4 — Accept loop (1.5 marks)**

```c
while (1) {
    struct sockaddr_in client;
    socklen_t len = sizeof(client);
    int client_fd = accept(server_fd, (struct sockaddr*)&client, &len);
    // ... process client_fd ...
    close(client_fd);
}
```
- **`accept()`** blocks until a client completes the 3-way handshake, then returns a **new** FD for that connection. `server_fd` keeps listening.
- Fills `client` with the client's IP and port (in network byte order).

**Step 5 — Communicate (1 mark)**

```c
char buf[1024];
int n = recv(client_fd, buf, sizeof(buf), 0);   // Block until data or EOF
send(client_fd, buf, n, 0);                     // Echo back n bytes
```
- `recv()` returns the number of bytes actually received (may be less than buffer size — **TCP is a stream!**).
- `send()` returns number of bytes sent; handle short writes.

**Step 6 — Cleanup (1 mark)**

```c
close(client_fd);   // per-connection
close(server_fd);   // on shutdown
```
- Always pair every successful `socket()` with a `close()` to avoid FD leaks.

**Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above) — **extra credit**.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b) — `sockaddr_in` and Byte-Order (7 marks)

**Structure definition (2 marks):**

```c
struct sockaddr_in {
    sa_family_t     sin_family;    // AF_INET
    in_port_t       sin_port;      // Port in network byte order (big-endian)
    struct in_addr  sin_addr;      // 32-bit IPv4 in network byte order
    char            sin_zero[8];   // Padding to match sizeof(sockaddr)=16
};
struct in_addr {
    in_addr_t s_addr;   // 32-bit unsigned int
};
```

**The 16-byte rule (1 mark):** `sizeof(sockaddr_in) == sizeof(sockaddr) == 16`. The padding `sin_zero[8]` is **mandatory** so that casting `(struct sockaddr *)&my_addr_in` is valid C and the kernel can re-interpret the bytes via `sa_family`.

**Byte-order necessity (2 marks):**

TCP/IP was designed when big-endian (e.g., IBM mainframes, Motorola 68K) was the dominant CPU order. The RFCs **mandate** big-endian ("network byte order") for all multi-byte fields. Modern CPUs are predominantly **little-endian** (x86, ARM Cortex-M/A, RISC-V in many configs). If you assign `addr.sin_port = 8080;` on a little-endian host without `htons()`, the bytes `8080 = 0x1F90` will be stored as `90 1F 00 00` in memory. The network stack, expecting `00 1F 90 00`, will interpret this as port `0x001F = 31` — a completely different port — and your `bind()` will fail with `EADDRINUSE` or succeed against the wrong port.

**Conversion functions (2 marks):**

| Function | Converts |
|---|---|
| `htons(uint16_t host16)` | 16-bit host order → network order (for `sin_port`) |
| `htonl(uint32_t host32)` | 32-bit host order → network order (for `sin_addr.s_addr`) |
| `ntohs(...)` / `ntohl(...)` | reverse direction (for reading received data) |

**Symmetry:** `ntohs(htons(x)) == x` always holds (the functions are inverses on the same host). On a big-endian host, these functions are often no-ops (macros that return their input unchanged).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Slot 1]`

**(a) Compare TCP and UDP sockets across 8 parameters. Explain why UDP is preferred for real-time sensor data in IoT, despite being unreliable.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Write the complete UDP client–server pseudocode (with API calls) and explain `sendto()` and `recvfrom()` in detail. How does UDP achieve demultiplexing without connections?** **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a) — TCP vs UDP + IoT Justification (7 marks)

**Comparison table (4 marks — ½ mark per relevant row, 8 rows max):**

| # | Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Connection | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| 2 | Reliability | ACK + retransmit | No ACK, no retransmit |
| 3 | Order | Guaranteed (sequence numbers) | Not guaranteed |
| 4 | Flow control | Sliding window | None |
| 5 | Congestion control | Yes (Cubic, Reno) | None |
| 6 | Header size | ≥ 20 bytes | 8 bytes |
| 7 | Setup latency | 1 RTT (3-way handshake) | 0 RTT |
| 8 | State in kernel | TCB per connection | No per-flow state |

**Why UDP is preferred for real-time IoT sensors (3 marks):**

1. **Latency is critical:** A retransmitted packet in TCP arrives *after* its usefulness window. In a vibration-monitoring sensor sampling at 1 kHz, a 50 ms retransmission delay = **50 samples of stale data**, which defeats the purpose of real-time monitoring.
2. **Newer data is more valuable than old data:** If packet #5 is lost, the receiver should display packet #6 immediately rather than wait for #5 to be retransmitted. UDP's "lossy is OK" model fits this naturally.
3. **Bandwidth efficiency:** A 20-byte TCP header on top of a 4-byte sensor reading is **500% overhead**. UDP's 8-byte header gives only **200% overhead**, which matters on LoRa / NB-IoT links where total air-time is precious.
4. **State scalability:** An IoT gateway serving 10 000 sensors cannot afford 10 000 TCP connections (each consuming a TCB ≈ 1–4 KB). UDP needs only one socket per listening port and a small receive buffer.
5. **Application-level recovery:** Protocols like CoAP define their **own** reliability layer (confirmable messages, token-based retransmission) tuned to the application's actual needs, avoiding TCP's "one-size-fits-all" guarantees.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b) — UDP Code + Demultiplexing (7 marks)

**UDP server (3 marks):**

```c
int fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0);                  // (1)
struct sockaddr_in addr = {AF_INET, htons(5000), {htonl(INADDR_ANY)}};
bind(fd, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr));           // (2)
char buf[1024];
while (1) {
    struct sockaddr_in client;
    socklen_t len = sizeof(client);
    int n = recvfrom(fd, buf, sizeof(buf), 0,              // (3)
                     (struct sockaddr*)&client, &len);
    // process buf[0..n-1]
    sendto(fd, buf, n, 0,                                   // (4)
           (struct sockaddr*)&client, len);
}
close(fd);                                                  // (5)
```

**UDP client (1.5 marks):**

```c
int fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0);
struct sockaddr_in srv = {AF_INET, htons(5000), {htonl(...server IP...)}};
sendto(fd, "PING", 4, 0, (struct sockaddr*)&srv, sizeof(srv));
int n = recvfrom(fd, buf, sizeof(buf), 0, NULL, NULL);
close(fd);
```

**`sendto()` explained (1 mark):**
```c
ssize_t sendto(int sockfd, const void *buf, size_t len, int flags,
               const struct sockaddr *dest_addr, socklen_t addrlen);
```
- Sends **one UDP datagram** to `dest_addr`. The kernel stamps the source IP/port and computes the UDP checksum. If `len` exceeds the path MTU, the packet is fragmented (or dropped with `EMSGSIZE` if `DF` is set).

**`recvfrom()` explained (1 mark):**
```c
ssize_t recvfrom(int sockfd, void *buf, size_t len, int flags,
                 struct sockaddr *src_addr, socklen_t *addrlen);
```
- Receives **one complete datagram** (or truncates with `MSG_TRUNC` flag). Fills `src_addr` with the sender's IP+port so the server can reply. Returns the size of the datagram in bytes.

**UDP demultiplexing without connections (0.5 mark):**

The kernel uses a **socket lookup** keyed by the 4-tuple `{local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port}` (note: only 4 fields, not 5, because there is no connection state). If the local port is bound with `INADDR_ANY`, any source IP matches the local-IP wildcard. If multiple clients send to the same port, the kernel keeps a small hash table of the source addresses and delivers each `recvfrom()` to the appropriate application instance. The application distinguishes clients by reading `src_addr` in `recvfrom()`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `htons()`** on the port number → answer marked wrong even if logic is correct (lose 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing `accept()` (TCP only) with `recvfrom()` (UDP only)** in the code — mixing them up shows conceptual gap (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Not mentioning `INADDR_ANY`** in the bind step — partial loss.
> 4. **Writing `sin_port = 8080;` directly** without `htons()` — examiners specifically watch for this.
> 5. **Claiming UDP is "faster" without quantifying** — say "no 3-way handshake, no ACK, smaller header" instead of just "faster."
> 6. **Forgetting to `close()` the file descriptor** — FD leak is a real bug; mention it.
> 7. **Confusing `SOCK_STREAM` with `AF_INET`** — they are orthogonal (one is type, one is family).
> 8. **Not distinguishing `recv()` (stream) from `recvfrom()` (datagram)** — TCP can return partial data; UDP returns one full datagram.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin this list before the exam:**

- ✅ A **socket** is an endpoint identified by `{IP, Port, Protocol}` — opening sentence of any answer.
- ✅ **Port = 16 bits** → range **0 to 65535**; well-known 0–1023, registered 1024–49151, dynamic 49152–65535.
- ✅ **TCP = `SOCK_STREAM`** (reliable, ordered, connection-oriented); **UDP = `SOCK_DGRAM`** (best-effort, connectionless).
- ✅ TCP server sequence = `socket → bind → listen → accept → recv/send → close` (six canonical steps).
- ✅ TCP client sequence = `socket → connect → send/recv → close`.
- ✅ UDP sequence = `socket → bind (server only) → sendto/recvfrom → close` — **no `listen`, `accept`, or `connect`**.
- ✅ The **5-tuple** identifies a TCP connection: `{src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port, protocol}`.
- ✅ `struct sockaddr_in` is **16 bytes**, has `sin_family`, `sin_port`, `sin_addr`, `sin_zero[8]` padding.
- ✅ `htons()` for `sin_port`, `htonl()` for `sin_addr.s_addr` — **NEVER skip these on little-endian hosts**.
- ✅ Network byte order = **big-endian**, mandated by IP/TCP/UDP RFCs.
- ✅ `INADDR_ANY` = `0.0.0.0` = wildcard bind on all local interfaces.
- ✅ Loopback IP = `127.0.0.1` (same-host communication only).
- ✅ TCP MSS = MTU − 40 = **1460 bytes** for typical Ethernet (MTU = 1500).
- ✅ TCP = 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK); TCP close = 4-way (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK).
- ✅ UDP header = **8 bytes**; TCP header = **20+ bytes**.
- ✅ TCP maintains a **TCB (Transmission Control Block)** per connection in the kernel; UDP is stateless.
- ✅ For real-time IoT sensor data, prefer **UDP** (low latency, low overhead, loss-tolerant).
- ✅ For OTA firmware updates, file transfers, MQTT, SSH — use **TCP** (loss-intolerant).
- ✅ For inter-process communication **on the same board**, use **Unix domain sockets** (`AF_UNIX`) — faster, no IP header.
- ✅ Always call `close()` for every successful `socket()` to avoid FD leaks.
- ✅ TCP is a **byte stream** (not message-oriented) → `recv()` may return partial data; loop until done.
- ✅ UDP is **datagram-oriented** → each `recvfrom()` returns exactly one complete datagram.
- ✅ Examiner loves: comparison tables, neat sequence diagrams, and explicit byte-order conversion in code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT415 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Remote Procedure Call (RPC)** is a high-level inter-process communication (IPC) paradigm that enables a program (the *client*) to cause a procedure or subroutine to execute in a different address space — typically on a remote networked node (the *server*) — using a syntax that mirrors a normal local procedure call. The programmer writes a call as if invoking a local function; the RPC framework transparently handles **marshalling** (serializing parameters and return values into a transmittable wire format), network transmission, **unmarshalling** (reconstructing the data on the receiver), and result delivery.

In the context of KTU's Embedded System Design syllabus, RPCs are studied as the foundational distributed-computing abstraction that allows resource-constrained embedded nodes, sensors, and controllers to invoke services across a **Local Area Network (LAN)**, **Controller Area Network (CAN)**, or the **Internet**, while preserving the simplicity of the traditional function-call programming model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RPCs are categorized under *Distributed Embedded System Communication Models* in Module 4. Students must master the layered architecture (Stub–Skeleton model), the call lifecycle, parameter-passing semantics (pass-by-value, pass-by-reference with *copy/restore*), and the failure modes (*at-most-once*, *at-least-once*, *exactly-once* semantics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology — Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Client Stub / Proxy:** A local function on the client machine that mimics the remote procedure's signature.
> - **Server Skeleton / Stub:** The corresponding counterpart on the server that demultiplexes incoming calls.
> - **Marshalling:** Conversion of in-memory data structures into a platform-independent byte stream (e.g., XDR, CDR, JSON, Protocol Buffers).
> - **Unmarshalling:** Reconstruction of data structures from the received byte stream.
> - **IDL (Interface Definition Language):** A language-neutral contract (e.g., Sun XDR, CORBA IDL, gRPC Proto) that declares the procedure signatures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Phone-Call to a Distant Colleague" Model

Imagine you are working in an office in Kerala and you need a colleague in Bengaluru to perform a calculation for you. You cannot physically reach into their desk, so:

1. You **write down the inputs** on a slip of paper (this is **marshalling**).
2. You **hand the slip to the postal courier** (this is the **transport layer** — TCP/UDP).
3. Your colleague in Bengaluru **reads the paper, performs the calculation**, and **writes the answer** on a return slip (this is **server-side execution**).
4. The courier **brings the slip back** to you.
5. You **read the answer** and continue with your work (this is **unmarshalling**).

From your perspective, it *looked* as if you just asked a question and got a result. The geographic distance, the postal service, and the paper format were all hidden from you. **An RPC framework hides network complexity behind a familiar function-call syntax.**

> [!TIP]
> A *local procedure call* uses the CPU's call stack and shared memory. An *RPC* uses a network protocol stack (Application → Transport → Network → Data-Link → Physical). The transformation between these two worlds is precisely what the **Stub-Skeleton mechanism** accomplishes.

### 1.3 RPC vs. Local Procedure Call (LPC) — Key Differences

| Parameter | Local Procedure Call (LPC) | Remote Procedure Call (RPC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Same process / shared memory | Separate processes on different machines |
| **Communication Medium** | CPU stack & registers | Network (LAN, CAN, Wi-Fi, Internet) |
| **Latency** | **< 1 µs** (nanosecond range) | **1 ms – 500 ms** (millisecond range) |
| **Failure Modes** | Stack overflow, segfault | Network timeout, host down, partial failure |
| **Parameter Passing** | Direct memory reference | Marshalled, value-copy (no shared pointers) |
| **Synchronization** | Implicit (single thread) | Explicit (synchronous / asynchronous) |
| **Endianness** | Native (homogeneous) | Heterogeneous (big-endian vs little-endian) |

### 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

RPC was conceptualized by **Bruce Jay Nelson** in 1981 (Xerox PARC) and later formalized in **Sun Microsystems' RFC 1050 / RFC 1057 (1988)**. Modern derivatives include **XML-RPC** (1998), **JSON-RPC** (2005), and Google's **gRPC** (2015), all of which are widely deployed in **IoT gateways**, **automotive ECU networks**, and **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern embedded designs, RPCs underpin cloud-to-device telemetry (AWS IoT Core uses gRPC over MQTT-like channels), automotive diagnostics (UDS over DoIP uses RPC-style service calls), and robotic swarm coordination. Understanding RPCs is therefore essential for any KTU 2024 graduate entering the IoT, automotive, or industrial-automation sectors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight-Phase RPC Lifecycle

A single RPC invocation traverses **eight distinct phases**. Mastering this lifecycle is mandatory for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Phase 1 — Client Invocation**
The client program executes a statement such as `result = add(a, b);`. The compiler binds this to the **client stub** (a local proxy function) — NOT directly to the server. The stub's signature exactly matches the remote procedure's signature.

**Phase 2 — Parameter Marshalling**
The stub packages the arguments (`a`, `b`) into a **wire-format message**. For a system using XDR (External Data Representation, RFC 1014), this includes:
- A unique **transaction ID** (XID) — typically a 32-bit unsigned integer.
- The **program number** and **procedure number** (each 32 bits).
- The **credentials** and **verifier** fields for authentication.
- The marshalled arguments.

**Phase 3 — Message Framing & Transport**
The marshalled buffer is handed to the **transport layer**. RPC typically runs over **TCP** (for reliability) or **UDP** (for low latency). The message is segmented, addressed, and transmitted. In embedded systems, this is often replaced by **CAN frames** or **6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4**.

**Phase 4 — Network Transmission**
The packet traverses physical media. Latency depends on: propagation delay, transmission delay, queuing delay, and processing delay at intermediate routers/switches.

**Phase 5 — Server-Side Demultiplexing**
The **server skeleton** (also called the *server stub*) receives the incoming packet, identifies the target program/procedure using the program/procedure numbers, and dispatches the request to the actual server procedure.

**Phase 6 — Argument Unmarshalling**
The skeleton converts the wire-format bytes back into in-memory data structures compatible with the server's architecture (handling endianness, padding, alignment).

**Phase 7 — Server Execution & Result Marshalling**
The actual server procedure runs, producing a result. The result is then marshalled into a reply message containing the same XID (to match it to the original request), a *reply status* (accepted/rejected), and the result data.

**Phase 8 — Reply Transmission & Client Unmarshalling**
The reply traverses the network back to the client stub, which unmarshals the result and returns it to the calling program. From the client's perspective, the function simply "returned" a value.

### 2.2 Parameter Passing Semantics in RPC

Unlike local calls — which can pass pointers (references) freely — RPCs cannot transmit raw memory addresses across machines. KTU examiners frequently test the three valid RPC parameter-passing modes:

1. **Pass-by-Value (Call-by-Value):**
   - The client stub copies the argument value into the message buffer.
   - Safe and simple; the server cannot mutate the client's original variable.
   - *Example:* `int x = 5; add(x, y);` — the stub sends `5` as a 4-byte integer.

2. **Pass-by-Reference with Copy/Restore (Call-by-Copy-Restore):**
   - The client stub sends the *current value* of the variable to the server.
   - The server modifies its local copy during execution.
   - The modified value is sent *back* in the reply and overwritten into the client's variable.
   - *Risk:* If two concurrent RPCs modify the same variable, results are non-deterministic. KTU questions often probe this race condition.

3. **Pass-by-Reference with Global Reference (Shared Memory Simulation):**
   - Used in distributed shared memory (DSM) systems.
   - The stub transmits a *global pointer* (a network-wide unique ID) instead of the data itself.
   - Both machines can access the data via this global reference.
   - *Performance:* Low network traffic but high coordination overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Arrays, Structures & Pointers:** Arrays are passed element-by-element (or as a counted block with a length prefix). Structures are flattened. Pointers to dynamic data are dereferenced and the pointed-to data is recursively marshalled. KTU students should be ready to write marshalling code for nested structures.

### 2.3 RPC Failure Semantics — High-Yield Topic

Because networks are unreliable, an RPC can fail in many ways: packet loss, server crash, network partition, slow server. The system must guarantee *some* level of delivery. Three formal guarantees exist:

| Semantics | Guarantee | Implementation Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **At-Most-Once** | The procedure executes 0 or 1 time. No duplicates. | Requires idempotency + filtering duplicates by XID. | Bank transactions, vote casting |
| **At-Least-Once** | The procedure executes 1 or more times. Duplicates possible. | Simple retry on timeout. | Non-idempotent logging (with care) |
| **Exactly-Once** | The procedure executes exactly 1 time. | Combines at-most-once with persistent retry logs. | Critical control systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once semantics. When would you choose each for an embedded automotive application?"* — Be prepared to discuss **rollback mechanisms** (sagas) and **idempotency tokens** for exactly-once.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. The vertical pipe symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $T_{RTT} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | $T_{prop} = d/c$ (distance ÷ speed of light in medium) |
| 2 | **Propagation Delay** | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{medium}}$ | $v_{medium} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber |
| 3 | **Transmission Delay** | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| 4 | **Effective RPC Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{calls}}{T_{RTT} \times N_{calls} + T_{server}}$ | Calls per second |
| 5 | **Message Size (with overhead)** | $M_{wire} = H_{transport} + H_{RPC} + S_{marshalled}$ | Bytes; $H_{TCP} = 20$ B, $H_{IP} = 20$ B, $H_{RPC} \approx 28$ B |
| 6 | **Bandwidth Utilization** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{payload}}{S_{payload} + H_{overhead}}$ | Expressed as percentage |
| 7 | **Idempotency Check Probability** | $P_{dup} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t_{retry}}$ | $\lambda$ = call rate, $t_{retry}$ = timeout window |
| 8 | **Timeout Setting Rule** | $T_{timeout} = 2 \times T_{RTT\_expected} + T_{server\_max}$ | Conservative upper bound |
| 9 | **Maximum Concurrent Calls** | $N_{max} = \dfrac{M_{mem} - M_{stub}}{S_{per-call-context}}$ | Memory-limited concurrency |
| 10 | **Goodput vs Throughput** | $G = \Theta \times S_{payload}$ | Bytes/sec of *useful* data |

### 2.5 Why RPCs Matter in Embedded Engineering

- **Modular Firmware Design:** RPC allows a sensor node to *delegate* heavy computation (e.g., FFT, ML inference) to a gateway or edge server while the node itself runs a lightweight RTOS like FreeRTOS.
- **Vendor Interoperability:** A common IDL enables firmware from different vendors to interoperate (e.g., OpenAPI for REST, `.proto` for gRPC).
- **Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates:** RPC frames can carry update commands and acknowledge results uniformly.
- **Simulation & Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL):** Real embedded code can be tested against a simulated server stub during development, accelerating KTU semester projects.
- **Determinism Trade-off:** Unlike pure message-passing (publish-subscribe), synchronous RPC provides clear request-response ordering, which is critical for control loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — End-to-End RPC Latency Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A KTU student is designing an embedded weather station that calls a remote FFT procedure over a Wi-Fi link. The Wi-Fi link has a bandwidth of **54 Mbps**, the physical distance to the server is **30 m** through air, the packet size (marshalled message) is **256 bytes**, the server's processing time is **4 ms**, and the queueing delay is negligible.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the total round-trip time for a single RPC call, assuming an acknowledgement piggybacked in the reply.
> **(b)** If the procedure is invoked **50 times per second**, what fraction of the available CPU time is *idle* waiting for replies?
> **(c)** Recommend a design change if the student needs the system to respond in under **10 ms**.

#### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Total RTT Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the four delay components.**

We apply the canonical delay model:

$$T_{RTT} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$$

Given:
- $d = 30$ m (one-way distance)
- $v_{medium} \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (speed of light in air, used as $v_{wireless}$)
- $L = 256$ bytes $= 256 \times 8 = 2048$ bits
- $R = 54 \text{ Mbps} = 54 \times 10^{6}$ bps
- $T_{server} = 4$ ms
- $T_{queue} \approx 0$

**Step 2: Compute Propagation Delay (one-way).**

$$T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{medium}} = \dfrac{30}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.1 \text{ µs}$$

> *Why so small?* Because the distance is only 30 m, the signal traverses it in 100 nanoseconds. In a continental WAN, this term would dominate.

**Step 3: Compute Transmission Delay (one-way).**

$$T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{2048}{54 \times 10^{6}} = 3.79 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} \approx 37.9 \text{ µs}$$

> *Why this size?* The marshalled message (256 B) is small relative to the bandwidth (54 Mbps), so serialization is fast.

**Step 4: Processing Delay.**

For a single switch/router hop, $T_{proc} \approx 50$ µs (typical Wi-Fi AP processing). In a local Wi-Fi network with one AP, we have one hop each direction.

$$T_{proc} = 2 \times 50 \text{ µs} = 100 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 5: Sum the one-way delays and double for round-trip.**

One-way (client → server):

$$T_{one-way} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} = 0.1 + 37.9 + 50 + 0 = 88.0 \text{ µs}$$

Round-trip (request + reply, identical size):

$$T_{RTT} = 2 \times T_{one-way} + T_{server} = 2 \times 88.0 \text{ µs} + 4000 \text{ µs}$$

$$T_{RTT} = 176 \text{ µs} + 4000 \text{ µs} = 4176 \text{ µs} \approx 4.176 \text{ ms}$$

#### 3.1.2 Part (b) — CPU Idle Fraction

**Step 6: Compute total time spent per second on 50 calls.**

$$T_{total\_per\_sec} = 50 \times T_{RTT} = 50 \times 4.176 \text{ ms} = 208.8 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 7: Compute busy fraction and idle fraction.**

$$\text{Busy Fraction} = \dfrac{T_{total\_per\_sec}}{1 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{208.8}{1000} = 0.2088 \text{ (20.88%)}$$

$$\text{Idle Fraction} = 1 - 0.2088 = 0.7912 \text{ (79.12%)}$$

> *Interpretation:* The CPU is idle **~79% of the time** waiting for replies. This is typical for I/O-bound synchronous RPC workloads.

#### 3.1.3 Part (c) — Design Recommendation

**Step 8: Analyze the bottleneck.**

The dominant delay is $T_{server} = 4$ ms, accounting for $\dfrac{4000}{4176} \approx 95.8\%$ of the total RTT. Network delays are negligible in comparison.

**Recommendation:** To meet the **< 10 ms** target (which is already met here, $4.176 \text{ ms} \ll 10 \text{ ms}$), the design is acceptable. However, for *future-proofing*:
- **Batching:** Aggregate multiple calls into one RPC to amortize overhead.
- **Asynchronous RPC:** Fire-and-forget calls that don't block the caller.
- **Local Caching:** Cache frequent results (e.g., time-series predictions) to avoid redundant calls.
- **Edge Offload:** Move FFT computation to a co-processor on the same PCB to eliminate $T_{server}$ entirely.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Between RPC Variants

| Variant | Encoding | Transport | Latency | Best For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sun RPC (ONC)** | XDR (binary) | TCP/UDP | Low | Legacy Unix, NFS | No streaming |
| **XML-RPC** | XML (text) | HTTP | High | Legacy web services | Verbose (~10× payload) |
| **JSON-RPC** | JSON (text) | HTTP/WS | Medium | Browser-facing IoT | Still verbose |
| **gRPC** | Protocol Buffers (binary) | HTTP/2 | Very Low | Modern microservices | Higher client complexity |
| **MQTT-RPC** | Binary | MQTT over TCP | Low | Constrained IoT | Requires broker |
| **CAN-RPC** | CAN frame payload | CAN 2.0B | < 1 ms | Automotive ECUs | Limited payload (8 B) |
| **Thrift RPC** | Binary (TBinaryProtocol) | TCP | Low | Polyglot systems | Facebook-specific tooling |

### 3.3 Derivation — Bandwidth Efficiency of Marshalled Messages

> *Given:* A procedure `setMotorSpeed(int motorID, float rpm)` is called once per 10 ms. Each `int` = 4 bytes, each `float` = 4 bytes. RPC header = 28 bytes. TCP/IP header = 40 bytes. Wi-Fi link = 54 Mbps.
>
> *Find:* (i) Wire size per call, (ii) Bandwidth utilization, (iii) Whether the link is saturated.

**Step 1: Compute marshalled payload.**

$$S_{payload} = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2: Add RPC header.**

$$S_{RPC} = 28 + 8 = 36 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3: Add transport overhead.**

$$S_{wire} = 40 + 36 = 76 \text{ bytes} = 608 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Bandwidth efficiency.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{8}{76} = 0.1053 = 10.53\%$$

> *Observation:* The protocol overhead is **9.5× larger** than the application payload. This is a classic "tiny-message problem" in IoT.

**Step 5: Link utilization at 100 Hz call rate.**

$$\rho = 100 \times 608 \text{ bits/s} = 60{,}800 \text{ bps} \approx 0.113\% \text{ of 54 Mbps}$$

> *Conclusion:* The link is far from saturated. The real issue is *per-message latency*, not bandwidth.

### 3.4 Analytical Expression — Number of Round-Trips for Multi-Stage RPC

For composite operations requiring **k** dependent sub-procedures, the total wall-clock time is:

$$T_{composite} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{RTT,i} = k \times T_{RTT\_avg}$$

If the sub-procedures are *independent* and can be pipelined, the wall-clock time approaches $T_{RTT\_max}$, with throughput approaching $\dfrac{k}{T_{RTT\_max}}$ calls/sec. This motivates **asynchronous / parallel RPC** in latency-sensitive embedded designs.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RPC Architecture — Layered Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT_NODE["CLIENT NODE EMBEDDED BOARD"]
        direction TB
        APP1["Client Application Program"]
        STUB1["Client Stub / Proxy"]
        MAR1["Marshalling Engine"]
        TX1["Transport Layer TCP or UDP"]
        NET1["Network Interface Wi-Fi or Ethernet"]
        APP1 --> STUB1
        STUB1 --> MAR1
        MAR1 --> TX1
        TX1 --> NET1
    end

    subgraph NETWORK["NETWORK MEDIUM LAN or WAN or CAN BUS"]
        direction TB
        PACKET["Marshalled RPC Packet 28 byte header plus payload"]
        ROUTER["Router or Switch or CAN Bridge"]
        PACKET --> ROUTER
    end

    subgraph SERVER_NODE["SERVER NODE GATEWAY or EDGE SERVER"]
        direction TB
        NET2["Network Interface"]
        RX2["Transport Layer TCP or UDP"]
        DEMAR2["Unmarshalling Engine"]
        SKEL2["Server Skeleton Dispatcher"]
        PROC2["Actual Server Procedure Implementation"]
        NET2 --> RX2
        RX2 --> DEMAR2
        DEMAR2 --> SKEL2
        SKEL2 --> PROC2
    end

    NET1 --> PACKET
    ROUTER --> NET2
    PROC2 -.Returns Result.-> SKEL2
    SKEL2 -.Reply Packet.-> DEMAR2
    DEMAR2 -.Via Network.-> STUB1
    STUB1 -.Returns to App.-> APP1

    style CLIENT_NODE fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style SERVER_NODE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style NETWORK fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 RPC Call Flow — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CP as Client Program
    participant CS as Client Stub
    participant TX as Network Transport
    participant SS as Server Skeleton
    participant SP as Server Procedure

    CP->>CS: 1. Call add(5, 3)
    Note over CS: Pack arguments into message
    CS->>CS: 2. Marshalling: serialize 5, 3
    CS->>TX: 3. Send request packet
    Note over TX: TCP or UDP transmission
    TX->>SS: 4. Deliver packet
    SS->>SS: 5. Unmarshalling: extract 5, 3
    SS->>SP: 6. Invoke add(5, 3)
    Note over SP: Computation
    SP-->>SS: 7. Return 8
    SS->>SS: 8. Marshalling: serialize result 8
    SS->>TX: 9. Send reply packet
    TX->>CS: 10. Deliver reply
    CS->>CS: 11. Unmarshalling: extract 8
    CS-->>CP: 12. Return value 8
    Note over CP: Program continues as if local call
```

### 4.3 Parameter Marshalling — Nested Structure Walkthrough

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Client Memory: struct SensorData { int id; float temp; char name[16]; }"]
    B["Marshalled Wire Format: id=4B plus temp=4B plus name=16B plus length-prefix=4B total=28B"]
    C["Server Memory: reconstructed struct SensorData"]

    A -->|"Stub marshals"| B
    B -->|"Transmitted over network"| C

    style A fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.4 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous RPC — Control Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CallInitiated: Client invokes procedure
    CallInitiated --> Blocked: Synchronous RPC: caller blocked
    CallInitiated --> Continued: Asynchronous RPC: caller continues
    Blocked --> WaitingReply: Stub waits for response
    Continued --> Polling: Caller polls for response
    Continued --> Callback: Callback fires on reply
    WaitingReply --> ResultReturned: Reply received
    Polling --> ResultReturned: Reply ready
    Callback --> ResultReturned: Reply delivered
    ResultReturned --> Idle: Procedure complete
```

### 4.5 Failure Recovery Flowchart — Exactly-Once Semantics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Send RPC request with XID=42"] --> B{"Reply received within timeout?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Verify XID matches and return result"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Retry count less than maximum?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> A
    D -- "No" --> E["Log failure and raise exception to client"]
    C --> F{"Server-side log indicates executed?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Suppress duplicate: do NOT re-execute"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Execute server procedure"]
    H --> I["Log execution with XID for dedup"]
    I --> J["Return result to client"]
    G --> J

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define Remote Procedure Call (RPC). List any two advantages of using RPC in distributed embedded systems.

**Model Answer:**
A *Remote Procedure Call (RPC)* is a communication model that allows a program to invoke a procedure or subroutine residing in a different address space (on a remote node) as if it were a local function call. The RPC framework transparently handles parameter marshalling, network transmission, and result delivery, hiding the underlying distributed nature from the application programmer.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Transparency:** Application code is written using familiar function-call syntax, hiding the complexities of network programming (sockets, byte ordering, serialization).
2. **Modularity & Reusability:** Procedures can be developed independently and reused across multiple clients, reducing code duplication in large embedded firmware projects.

*(Acceptable answer: any two of — distributed resource utilization, vendor interoperability via IDL, simplified debugging, language independence. Award full 3 marks for definition + 2 advantages.)*

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q:** What is *marshalling* in the context of RPC? Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer:**
*Marshalling* is the process of converting in-memory data structures (arguments and return values) into a standardized, platform-independent byte stream suitable for transmission over a network.

**Why it is necessary:**
1. **Endianness Normalization:** Different processors use different byte orders (little-endian vs big-endian). Marshalling converts all data into a canonical wire format (e.g., XDR uses big-endian network byte order).
2. **Pointer Resolution:** Pointers valid in one process are meaningless in another. Marshalling dereferences pointers and inlines the pointed-to data.
3. **Type Safety:** A well-defined wire format (defined by an IDL) ensures both client and server interpret the data identically.
4. **Cross-Language Compatibility:** Marshalled data can be unmarshalled by a server written in a different language (e.g., C client → Java server).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse stubs with skeletons.** The *client stub* lives on the client; the *server stub/skeleton* lives on the server. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always mention the XID (Transaction ID).** Examiners specifically look for it in your 14-mark answers on RPC failure handling.
> 3. **Pass-by-reference is NOT directly supported in RPC.** Writing so without qualification is a common error. Always specify *copy-restore* or *global reference* mechanism.
> 4. **Do not write generic "network programming" answers.** Stick to the **stub-skeleton model** and the **eight-phase lifecycle**. Generic answers get 8–9/14, not 14/14.
> 5. **For diagrams, label every block.** Unlabeled boxes in your RPC flow diagram will lose marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**Q:** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture and working of Remote Procedure Call (RPC)**. Discuss the role of **stubs, IDL, and marshalling** in detail. Also explain the different **parameter passing semantics** used in RPC.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Architecture & Working — 7 marks**

*Introduction (2 marks):*
An RPC mechanism allows a client program to call a function on a remote server as if it were a local call. The remote procedure call abstraction was first formalized by Birrell and Nelson (1984) and is implemented in systems like Sun RPC, DCE RPC, and gRPC.

*Diagram (2 marks):* Draw the layered architecture (as in Section 4.1 above) showing: Client Application → Client Stub → Marshalling → Transport → Network → Transport → Unmarshalling → Server Skeleton → Server Procedure. Label all layers and indicate the request and reply paths.

*Working — Eight-Phase Lifecycle (3 marks):*
1. Client invokes local stub with arguments.
2. Stub marshals arguments (serializes to wire format).
3. Stub invokes local OS transport layer (e.g., TCP send).
4. Kernel transmits packet to remote host.
5. Server's transport layer delivers the packet to the server skeleton.
6. Skeleton demultiplexes (identifies program & procedure) and unmarshals arguments.
7. Skeleton calls the actual server procedure.
8. Procedure returns; result is marshalled, sent back; client stub returns result to caller.

**(b) Stubs, IDL, Marshalling & Parameter Passing — 7 marks**

*Stubs (1.5 marks):*
- **Client Stub:** A proxy function on the client that mimics the remote procedure's signature. It packages the call into a message.
- **Server Stub / Skeleton:** A dispatcher on the server that unmarshals incoming messages and invokes the actual server procedure. It also marshals the return value.

*Interface Definition Language / IDL (1.5 marks):*
- An IDL is a *contract language* that declares procedure names, argument types, return types, and program/procedure numbers.
- Examples: Sun XDR, CORBA IDL, Protobuf (`.proto`).
- The IDL is compiled by a *stub generator* (e.g., `rpcgen` for Sun RPC) which produces both client stub and server skeleton code automatically.
- This eliminates manual coding of marshalling routines and ensures cross-language, cross-platform compatibility.

*Marshalling (1.5 marks):*
- Marshalling is the conversion of in-memory data into a platform-independent wire format.
- For simple types (int, float): encoded in fixed-size, fixed-endianness form (network byte order / big-endian).
- For complex types (arrays, structs, strings): use length-prefixed encoding, recursive flattening.
- XDR is one such standard (RFC 1014).

*Parameter Passing Semantics (2.5 marks):*
- **Pass-by-Value:** The value is copied into the message. Safe, but cannot reflect server-side modifications back to the client.
- **Pass-by-Reference with Copy/Restore:** Value sent to server, server modifies, modified value returned and overwritten in client's variable. Two concurrent calls on the same variable produce non-deterministic results.
- **Pass-by-Reference with Global Reference:** A network-wide unique handle is sent; both sides can access the underlying data through this handle. Useful for large structures (avoids copying).
- Discuss why raw pointers cannot be passed: pointers are local to an address space, meaningless across machines.
- Mention that arrays and structures are flattened recursively.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**Q:** Explain the **different failure semantics in RPC: at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once**. Discuss the **client-side and server-side mechanisms** required to implement each, with relevant examples from embedded systems. Mention how **timeouts and retransmissions** are handled.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Failure Semantics — 7 marks**

*Why failures occur (1 mark):*
Networks are unreliable. An RPC can fail due to: packet loss, server crash, network partition, server overload, or even a slow server whose reply arrives after the client times out. The system must specify *what happens* when such failures occur — this is the failure semantics.

*At-Most-Once Semantics (2 marks):*
- **Guarantee:** The remote procedure is executed *zero* or *one* time. Never more.
- **Mechanism:** The client sends a request with a unique XID and sets a timer. If no reply arrives before timeout, the client does NOT retransmit (or if it does, the server must recognize the duplicate XID and suppress re-execution). The server maintains a short-term cache of recently-executed XIDs.
- **Required:** The procedure must be *idempotent* (re-execution produces the same result), OR duplicate detection must be perfect.
- **Use case:** Bank transfer, sensor calibration where duplicate execution would be incorrect.
- **Embedded example:** An automotive ECU sending a brake-by-wire command — duplicates must be suppressed.

*At-Least-Once Semantics (2 marks):*
- **Guarantee:** The procedure executes *one or more* times. Duplicates possible.
- **Mechanism:** Client retransmits the request on timeout. Server always executes on receipt.
- **Risk:** If the original reply was lost in transit, the client will retransmit and the server will execute again, producing a duplicate effect.
- **Use case:** Idempotent operations like setting a temperature setpoint, writing a non-critical log entry.
- **Embedded example:** An IoT device repeatedly sending a "door open" notification until the server ACKs.

*Exactly-Once Semantics (2 marks):*
- **Guarantee:** The procedure executes *exactly one* time. This is the strongest guarantee and the hardest to implement.
- **Mechanism:** Combines at-most-once's duplicate suppression with at-least-once's persistent retry. The client persistently logs the request (to a non-volatile store) and retransmits on timeout. The server logs the execution. Duplicate XIDs are recognized and suppressed, but the original result is replayed from the log.
- **Cost:** High — requires persistent storage on both client and server, atomic commit protocols.
- **Use case:** Critical control actions: medical device dosing, industrial process control, financial settlement.
- **Embedded example:** A pacemaker's "deliver therapy" command — must execute exactly once per trigger.

**(b) Timeouts, Retransmissions & Implementation — 7 marks**

*Timeout Setting (1.5 marks):*
- Timeout must be longer than the expected RTT but shorter than the user's tolerance for unresponsiveness.
- Conservative formula: $T_{timeout} = 2 \times T_{RTT\_expected} + T_{server\_max}$.
- Adaptive timeouts (Karn's algorithm) measure recent RTT samples and update the timeout using exponential smoothing.

*Client-Side Mechanisms (2 marks):*
- **Retry counter:** Limit maximum retries to prevent infinite loops.
- **XID generation:** Use monotonically increasing or random 32-bit IDs.
- **Persistent log:** For exactly-once, write the request to non-volatile storage before sending.
- **Failure callback:** Notify the application when retries are exhausted.

*Server-Side Mechanisms (2 marks):*
- **Reply cache:** Retain recent (XID, reply) pairs to recognize duplicates.
- **Idempotency check:** If a duplicate XID arrives, return the cached reply without re-execution.
- **Persistent execution log:** For exactly-once, log every successful execution to a non-volatile store.
- **Watchdog timer:** Detect server hangs and reset the connection.

*Embedded-Specific Considerations (1.5 marks):*
- Resource-constrained devices may not have non-volatile storage large enough for persistent logs. Workarounds: use *external* EEPROM, or relax to at-least-once.
- Power-cycling the client between retries can lose state — must use battery-backed RAM or flash.
- Discuss the *partial failure* problem in detail: the client doesn't know if the server executed the procedure or not. This is the *Two Generals' Problem* and is unsolvable without additional assumptions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **RPC = Local syntax + Remote execution.** The illusion of locality is created by the stub-skeleton mechanism.
- ☐ **Two-sided stubs:** Client stub (proxy) on caller side, server skeleton (dispatcher) on callee side. Both are auto-generated from the IDL.
- ☐ **IDL → Stub Generator → Stubs:** The IDL is the single source of truth. A change in the IDL requires regenerating both client and server stubs.
- ☐ **Eight-phase lifecycle:** Invocation → Marshalling → Framing → Transmission → Demultiplexing → Unmarshalling → Execution → Return.
- ☐ **Marshalling is platform-independent:** Uses network byte order (big-endian) and length-prefixed encoding for variable-size types.
- ☐ **No raw pointers in RPC:** Use pass-by-value, copy-restore, or global reference handles.
- ☐ **Three failure semantics:** At-most-once (suppress duplicates), At-least-once (allow duplicates), Exactly-once (atomic execution, hardest).
- ☐ **XID is the linchpin:** Every RPC request must carry a unique transaction ID for duplicate detection and request-reply matching.
- ☐ **Timeout rule:** $T_{timeout} = 2 \times T_{RTT} + T_{server\_max}$ (conservative bound).
- ☐ **RPC ≠ REST:** REST is resource-oriented (HTTP verbs on URLs); RPC is action-oriented (verb calls over a network). gRPC blurs this line.
- ☐ **Embedded variants:** CAN-RPC for automotive, MQTT-RPC for IoT, gRPC for edge-cloud, JSON-RPC for browser-facing UIs.
- ☐ **Big-endian wire format:** XDR, CDR, and Protocol Buffers all use big-endian encoding (network byte order) for cross-platform consistency.
- ☐ **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous:** Synchronous blocks the caller (simple model, low throughput); Asynchronous uses callbacks/futures (high throughput, complex code).
- ☐ **At-most-once requires idempotency OR duplicate suppression**; At-least-once requires tolerance of duplicates; Exactly-once requires persistent logs on both ends.
- ☐ **Karn's Algorithm:** Used for adaptive RTT estimation in RPC retransmission — exempts retransmitted segments from RTT averaging to avoid corruption.
- ☐ **Two Generals' Problem:** RPC failure detection is fundamentally limited — you cannot distinguish "server didn't receive" from "server received but reply lost." Choose semantics, not certainty.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "S.T.U.B.S."**
> **S**tubs (client & server)
> **T**ransaction ID (XID)
> **U**nmarshalling on server, Marshalling on client
> **B**ig-endian wire format
> **S**ynchronous = simple; Asynchronous = scalable
